content:
# Practice Guide: Shell Voicings

## Overview

Shell voicings are three-note versions of seventh chords that form the absolute foundation of jazz guitar comping. As Jens Larsen repeatedly emphasizes, these are the best place to start for jazz guitar -- and maybe for jazz in general. A shell voicing consists of just the root, the third, and the seventh of a chord, giving you the essential harmonic information with minimal finger effort.

What makes shell voicings so powerful is their simplicity. Because they only use three notes, they leave your brain free to focus on rhythm, groove, and listening -- the things that actually make jazz sound like jazz. As Jens puts it: "The object in playing guitar is to make music and not to make hard work," quoting Joe Pass. Shell voicings split the guitar neck into three practical zones: two low strings for the bass note, the middle strings for the chord (3rd and 7th), and the top strings left open for adding melody or extensions later.

Jens uses shell voicings as the starting point in nearly every chord-related lesson because they serve as a launchpad for everything else -- comping rhythms, chord melody, walking bass, and even chord soloing. Master these first, and every other chord concept becomes dramatically easier.

## Prerequisites

- Basic knowledge of the guitar fretboard (note names on strings 5 and 6)
- Understanding of what root, 3rd, and 7th mean in a chord
- Ability to read a basic jazz lead sheet or chord chart
- Familiarity with the concept of major and minor 7th chords

## Key Concepts

### What Is a Shell Voicing?

A shell voicing strips a seventh chord down to three essential notes:
- **Root** -- on the 5th or 6th string (provides the bass)
- **3rd** -- defines whether the chord is major or minor
- **7th** -- defines the chord quality (major 7, dominant 7, etc.)

The 5th is omitted entirely because it adds very little harmonic information (it is the same for almost all chord types).

### The Four Main Chord Types

| Chord Type | Formula | Example in C |
|---|---|---|
| Major 7 (maj7) | R - 3 - 7 | C - E - B |
| Dominant 7 (7) | R - 3 - b7 | C - E - Bb |
| Minor 7 (m7) | R - b3 - b7 | C - Eb - Bb |
| Minor 7b5 (m7b5) | R - b3 - b7 | C - Eb - Bb* |

*Note: As Jens points out, the m7b5 shell voicing looks identical to a m7 shell voicing because the 5th is omitted. "Luckily, that almost never causes problems in real songs because you can hear what it is from the context."

### Two String Sets

Each chord type has two voicing positions:
- **Root on 5th string**: chord tones land on strings 4 and 3
- **Root on 6th string**: chord tones land on strings 4 and 3

This gives you complete neck coverage. As Jens demonstrates, you can play through entire songs by alternating between these two sets, and the chords naturally flow together with smooth voice leading.

### The Built-In Split

A crucial feature Jens highlights: shell voicings have a natural split between the bass note (root) and the chord (3rd and 7th). This means you can play them as a unit, or separate bass and chord for more rhythmic variety -- "It's a little bit like being your own rhythm section."

## Recommended Video Sequence

Watch these Jens Larsen videos in order:

1. **Jazz Chord Essentials - Shell Voicings** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=1tThogfLBW0 -- The original foundational lesson on shell voicings, covering construction and all four chord types
2. **The Basic Jazz Chords Every Beginner Should Start With** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=6ZPHbn3coIU -- How to get started with three-note chords and play real songs immediately
3. **Practice Jazz Chords like THIS in 2026 (It Changes EVERYTHING)** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=G-U6933d2bo -- Three exercises for turning shell voicings into real music, including rhythm development
4. **Practice Jazz Chords Like This Every Day & SKYROCKET Your Playing** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=QWbq5DbKVEg -- The complete system for shell voicings on songs like Fly Me to the Moon
5. **Autumn Leaves - Beautiful Jazz Chords (5 Levels)** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=Msd4tPlvm5g -- Five progressive exercises using shell voicings on Autumn Leaves
6. **The 7 Comping Rhythms That Really Matter - Jazz Chords** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=-450hfJp0I4 -- Essential comping rhythms demonstrated with shell voicings
7. **Exactly How To Nail Jazz Blues Comping (It's Simple!)** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=C8cziTSa6pA -- Applying shell voicings to a jazz blues with rhythm, melody, and passing chords
8. **Stop Making Chord Melody Hard - Start With This** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=d1Ro4G6Wc9o -- Using shell voicings as the basis for chord melody on Satin Doll

## Practice Routine

### Week 1-2: Foundation

**Daily practice: 20-30 minutes**

**Exercise 1: Learn the Diatonic Shell Voicings in C Major (10 min)**

Play all seven diatonic seventh chords as shell voicings in C major. Start with root on the 5th string:

```
Cmaj7 - Dm7 - Em7 - Fmaj7 - G7 - Am7 - Bm7b5
```

Then play them with root on the 6th string (starting from Fmaj7, the IV chord):

```
Fmaj7 - G7 - Am7 - Bm7b5 - Cmaj7 - Dm7 - Em7
```

Play each chord cleanly with a metronome at 60 BPM, one chord per bar. Focus on hearing each chord quality. As Jens teaches: "Be aware of what you're playing -- don't just move your fingers mindlessly."

**Exercise 2: Play Through a Simple Song (10 min)**

Apply your shell voicings to Autumn Leaves in G minor. As Jens demonstrates, you only need these chords:

- Cm7 - F7 - Bbmaj7 - Ebmaj7 (II-V-I-IV in Bb major)
- Am7b5 - D7 - Gm6 (II-V-I in G minor)

Play whole notes at first, just getting from chord to chord with smooth voice leading. Notice how Jens points out: "We're just sort of slowly moving down the neck. And you want your chords to flow together."

**Exercise 3: Bass and Chord Split (10 min)**

Take a single chord (e.g., Cm7) and practice splitting it:
1. Play the full voicing together
2. Play bass note alone, then chord alone
3. Alternate: bass on beat 1, chord on beat 2-and

This is what Jens calls the foundation for more interesting comping: "When you split the shell voicing into two parts, so bass and chord, then it's almost as if you have two separate instruments in your hands."

### Week 3-4: Application

**Daily practice: 20-30 minutes**

**Exercise 4: Charleston Rhythm on Songs (10 min)**

Apply the Charleston rhythm (note on beat 1, note on beat 2-and) to your shell voicings through Autumn Leaves or Fly Me to the Moon. Jens identifies this as "probably the most important rhythm to have in your vocabulary." Play at 100-120 BPM.

Once comfortable, practice the split version: bass on beat 1, chord on the 2-and.

**Exercise 5: Rootless Voicings (10 min)**

Start removing the root from your shell voicings, leaving just the 3rd and 7th on the middle strings. As Jens explains in his blues comping lesson: "When you want to add more rhythm to your playing, you've got two parts to play with, low and high."

Practice alternating between:
- Full shell voicing (root + chord)
- Bass note alone
- Rootless chord (just 3rd and 7th)

**Exercise 6: Add Top-Note Melody (10 min)**

On a Bb7 chord, keep the shell voicing shape and experiment with different top notes on the higher strings. As Jens teaches: "Instead of jumping between complicated grips, you just change or add a new top note to the chord that you're already playing. That's it. And suddenly your comping has melody."

Try adding the 5th, 9th, or 13th on top of the basic shape.

### Week 5-6: Integration

**Exercise 7: Comping with Rhythm Variations (15 min)**

Play through a complete song (Autumn Leaves, Satin Doll, or a jazz blues in Bb) using shell voicings with mixed rhythms. Combine:
- Charleston rhythm
- Shifted Charleston (starting on the 1-and)
- Split bass and chord patterns
- Bossa nova pattern (as Jens demonstrates on Autumn Leaves)

Record yourself and listen back. Jens stresses: "You cannot tell how you really sound if you're also busy playing."

**Exercise 8: Chromatic Passing Chords (15 min)**

Add chromatic movement between shell voicings. As Jens explains: "Passing chords are often over-complicated with theory -- these are just about moving notes up or down a fret." For example:
- Approach Ebmaj7 from a half step above (E voicing sliding down)
- Approach D7 from a half step below

## Common Mistakes to Avoid

1. **Trying to learn too many chord types at once.** Start with just maj7, m7, and dom7. The m7b5 can wait. As Jens says: "Focus on easy musical chords so that you can actually play songs, not just practice your finger gymnastics."

2. **Ignoring rhythm.** Shell voicings without rhythm are just chord shapes. Jens is emphatic: "Jazz isn't just about the chords. You need rhythm, too. That's where the real magic happens."

3. **Skipping between random positions.** Always voice-lead smoothly. Jens specifically points out: "You want your chords to flow together. And that's easier if you're not sort of skipping around in different positions."

4. **Memorizing shapes without learning songs.** Every shell voicing exercise should be applied to a real song as quickly as possible. "Let's put it into music" is Jens's recurring approach.

5. **Playing too many notes too soon.** Resist the urge to add extensions before the basic shell voicings are completely comfortable. The three-note foundation must be solid first.

## Key Takeaways

- Shell voicings are three-note chords (root, 3rd, 7th) that are the foundation of all jazz guitar harmony
- Learn two sets: root on the 5th string and root on the 6th string
- Practice diatonic shell voicings through major scales to learn the "family of chords"
- The built-in bass/chord split is your secret weapon for developing rhythm
- Always apply voicings to real songs (Autumn Leaves, Fly Me to the Moon, Satin Doll)
- Rhythm is more important than adding more notes -- the Charleston rhythm is your starting point
- Shell voicings are the foundation you will expand into drop 2, chord melody, and walking bass

## What's Next

After mastering shell voicings, move on to:
- [02 - Drop 2 Voicings](02-drop2-voicings.md) -- Expand to four-note voicings with richer harmony
- [06 - Jazz Blues](06-jazz-blues.md) -- Apply shell voicings to comping a jazz blues
- [07 - II-V-I Progressions](07-ii-v-i.md) -- Deepen your command of the most common jazz progression

title: Drop 2 Voicings
overview:
Drop 2 voicings are the workhorse of jazz guitar chord playing. They are four-note voicings that give you a full, rich harmonic sound while remaining playable on the guitar. The name "Drop 2" comes from a construction method: take a close-position four-note chord (as played on piano), then drop the second-highest note down an octave. The result is a voicing perfectly suited to the guitar's string layout.

Jens Larsen treats Drop 2 voicings as the natural next step after shell voicings. Where shell voicings give you the harmonic skeleton with three notes, Drop 2 voicings fill in that sound with a fourth note -- adding the 5th or other tones that complete the chord. As Jens explains, once you are comfortable with shell voicings, Drop 2 chords let you expand into "full pro chords" with extensions and color that give your comping a more complete and sophisticated texture.

The practical power of Drop 2 voicings is that they cover two main string sets (strings 4-3-2-1 and strings 5-4-3-2), each with four inversions. This gives you eight voicings per chord type -- enough to play through any jazz standard with smooth voice leading and never run out of options. Jens uses Drop 2 voicings extensively in his chord melody arrangements and walking bass demonstrations.
content:
# Practice Guide: Drop 2 Voicings

## Overview

Drop 2 voicings are the workhorse of jazz guitar chord playing. They are four-note voicings that give you a full, rich harmonic sound while remaining playable on the guitar. The name "Drop 2" comes from a construction method: take a close-position four-note chord (as played on piano), then drop the second-highest note down an octave. The result is a voicing perfectly suited to the guitar's string layout.

Jens Larsen treats Drop 2 voicings as the natural next step after shell voicings. Where shell voicings give you the harmonic skeleton with three notes, Drop 2 voicings fill in that sound with a fourth note -- adding the 5th or other tones that complete the chord. As Jens explains, once you are comfortable with shell voicings, Drop 2 chords let you expand into "full pro chords" with extensions and color that give your comping a more complete and sophisticated texture.

The practical power of Drop 2 voicings is that they cover two main string sets (strings 4-3-2-1 and strings 5-4-3-2), each with four inversions. This gives you eight voicings per chord type -- enough to play through any jazz standard with smooth voice leading and never run out of options. Jens uses Drop 2 voicings extensively in his chord melody arrangements and walking bass demonstrations.

## Prerequisites

- Solid command of shell voicings for all four main chord types (maj7, dom7, m7, m7b5)
- Ability to play through at least 2-3 jazz standards with shell voicings and basic rhythm
- Understanding of chord inversions (root position, 1st, 2nd, 3rd inversion)
- Familiarity with the concept of voice leading

## Key Concepts

### Construction from Close Position

A close-position C major 7 chord on piano would be: C - E - G - B (all within one octave). To create a Drop 2 voicing, take the second note from the top (G) and drop it down an octave:

```
Close position:  C - E - G - B
Drop 2:          G - C - E - B  (G dropped one octave)
```

This creates a wider spacing that fits naturally on four adjacent guitar strings.

### Four Inversions

Each chord type has four inversions, determined by which note is in the bass:

| Inversion | Bass Note | Example (Cmaj7) |
|---|---|---|
| Root position | Root | C - E - G - B (rearranged as drop 2) |
| 1st inversion | 3rd | E in bass |
| 2nd inversion | 5th | G in bass |
| 3rd inversion | 7th | B in bass |

### Two Primary String Sets

- **String set 4-3-2-1** (D-G-B-E): The most common set, excellent for comping and chord melody
- **String set 5-4-3-2** (A-D-G-B): Slightly lower, great for fuller sounds and walking bass combinations

As Jens demonstrates in his walking bass lessons, the 5-4-3-2 string set works perfectly when you want to add bass notes on the 6th string underneath.

### Voice Leading Principle

The most important concept with Drop 2 voicings is voice leading: moving from one chord to the next by moving each voice the smallest distance possible. Jens stresses that "you want your chords to flow together" rather than jumping around the neck. With four inversions per chord, you can always find a voicing close to where you already are.

## Recommended Video Sequence

Watch these Jens Larsen videos in order:

1. **Jazz Chord Essentials - Drop 2 voicings part 1** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=qCQS2BbCjOs -- Construction and basic shapes for all four inversions on string set 4-3-2-1
2. **Jazz Chord Essentials - Drop 2 voicings part 2** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=TC6d1Qcc72A -- Expanding to string set 5-4-3-2 and voice leading exercises
3. **Jazz Chord Essentials - Drop 2 voicings part 3** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=2HCfuOXArlE -- Applying Drop 2 chords to songs with extensions and alterations
4. **Drop2 Voicings - How to Understand The Construction** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=cxTdnBn7ljQ -- A clear explanation of the "drop" concept and how to build voicings from scratch
5. **How To Learn Drop 2 Jazz Chords The Right Way** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=kgt2WzhYUMQ -- The practical approach to learning and using Drop 2 voicings in real music
6. **Drop 2 Magic On Satin Doll - This Is How To Use Jazz Chords** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=0y4vj78Z2EQ -- Applying Drop 2 voicings to a real standard with beautiful voice leading
7. **Autumn Leaves - How To Use Drop 2 For An Easy Chord Melody** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=zq3IKsgWmCk -- Building a chord melody arrangement using Drop 2 voicings
8. **Jazz Blues Comping - Drop2 Chords You Need To Know** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=M8Ch1PgYSdw -- Using Drop 2 voicings on a jazz blues progression

## Practice Routine

### Week 1-2: Foundation

**Daily practice: 20-30 minutes**

**Exercise 1: Learn the Four Inversions of Cmaj7 on String Set 4-3-2-1 (10 min)**

Start with C major 7 and find all four inversions on strings 4-3-2-1:

- Root position (C in bass): frets will vary by position
- 1st inversion (E in bass)
- 2nd inversion (G in bass)
- 3rd inversion (B in bass)

Play each inversion, say the notes out loud, and move through them ascending up the neck. Use a metronome at 60 BPM, one chord per bar.

**Exercise 2: Repeat for All Four Chord Types (10 min)**

Once you have Cmaj7, learn the four inversions for:
- Cm7
- C7
- Cm7b5

Notice that many shapes are very similar -- often just one note (or one fret) different between chord types. This is a huge shortcut.

**Exercise 3: Voice Leading on a II-V-I (10 min)**

Take a II-V-I in C major (Dm7 - G7 - Cmaj7) and find the smoothest voice leading:

1. Start with any Dm7 inversion
2. Move to the G7 inversion where the notes move the least
3. Resolve to the Cmaj7 inversion closest to the G7

Practice this in multiple positions. The goal, as Jens teaches, is that "you can play most jazz standards with 2 types of Drop 2 chords" by simply voice-leading between nearby inversions.

### Week 3-4: Application

**Daily practice: 20-30 minutes**

**Exercise 4: Play Through a Standard with Drop 2 Voicings (15 min)**

Choose a standard you already know with shell voicings (Autumn Leaves, Satin Doll, or Fly Me to the Moon). Play through the entire song using only Drop 2 voicings on string set 4-3-2-1.

Rules:
- Stay in one area of the neck (no more than 4-5 frets of movement)
- Voice-lead smoothly from chord to chord
- Use a metronome at 100-120 BPM
- Play the Charleston rhythm (beat 1, beat 2-and)

**Exercise 5: String Set 5-4-3-2 (10 min)**

Repeat the inversion exercises on string set 5-4-3-2. These voicings have a darker, fuller quality. Practice the same II-V-I voice leading on this lower string set.

**Exercise 6: Mix String Sets (5 min)**

Play through your chosen standard alternating between the two string sets. Sometimes a smooth voice leading will naturally move you from one string set to another.

### Week 5-6: Integration

**Exercise 7: Add Extensions to Drop 2 Voicings (15 min)**

Start modifying your basic Drop 2 shapes:
- On a dom7 chord, raise the 5th to get a 13th
- On a dom7 chord, lower the root to get a natural 9th
- On a maj7 chord, raise the 5th to get a #11 (Lydian sound)
- On a m7 chord, add a 9th or 11th by adjusting one note

As Jens teaches, think of these as "the same chord with one note changed" rather than entirely new shapes.

**Exercise 8: Drop 2 Chord Melody (15 min)**

Take the melody of Autumn Leaves and harmonize it with Drop 2 voicings. As Jens demonstrates, the melody note becomes the top voice, and you fill in the chord underneath. Start with just the melody notes that fall on beat 1 of each bar.

**Exercise 9: Walking Bass with Drop 2 (optional, 10 min)**

As Jens shows in his walking bass lessons, Drop 2 voicings on strings 5-4-3-2 leave the 6th string free for bass movement. Practice playing a bass note on the 6th string followed by a Drop 2 chord, alternating through a blues or standard.

## Common Mistakes to Avoid

1. **Trying to memorize all inversions on all string sets at once.** Start with one string set and one chord type. Build outward gradually. Jens consistently advocates for a step-by-step approach.

2. **Ignoring voice leading.** Random chord shapes in random positions will never sound musical. The whole point of learning inversions is smooth connections between chords.

3. **Forgetting rhythm.** Drop 2 voicings are tools for making music, not a shape-memorization exercise. Always practice with a metronome and rhythmic patterns. As Jens says: "Memorizing endless inversions won't get you very far."

4. **Skipping shell voicings.** If you jumped straight to Drop 2 without a solid shell voicing foundation, go back. Shell voicings teach you the harmonic essentials that make Drop 2 voicings make sense.

5. **Not applying to songs immediately.** As soon as you can voice-lead a II-V-I with Drop 2 shapes, start using them on a real standard. Do not wait until you have memorized every inversion in every key.

## Key Takeaways

- Drop 2 voicings are four-note chords created by dropping the second-highest note of a close-position chord down an octave
- Each chord type has four inversions on each string set, giving you extensive neck coverage
- The two primary string sets are 4-3-2-1 and 5-4-3-2
- Voice leading (minimal movement between chords) is the most important skill to develop
- Extensions and alterations come from modifying one note at a time in a basic Drop 2 shape
- Apply to real songs as early as possible -- Satin Doll and Autumn Leaves are ideal starting points
- Drop 2 voicings are the foundation for chord melody, chord soloing, and walking bass comping

## What's Next

After mastering Drop 2 voicings, move on to:
- [01 - Shell Voicings](01-shell-voicings.md) -- If you need to revisit the three-note foundation
- [06 - Jazz Blues](06-jazz-blues.md) -- Apply Drop 2 voicings to blues comping
- [07 - II-V-I Progressions](07-ii-v-i.md) -- Voice-lead Drop 2 chords through the most common jazz progression

Ability to play through at least 2-3 jazz standards with shell voicings and basic rhythm

Familiarity with the concept of voice leading

title: Chord Tone Soloing
overview:
Chord tone soloing is the essential first step in jazz improvisation. Instead of thinking in scales, you learn to navigate through a song by targeting the notes that make up each chord -- the root, 3rd, 5th, and 7th. This is the approach that Jens Larsen consistently teaches as the foundation for playing solos that actually sound like jazz, rather than just running scales over changes.

As Jens explains: "Most jazz beginners hit a wall with arpeggios and end up playing solos that sound like exercises." The solution is not to abandon chord tones, but to learn how to use them musically. In jazz, the magic happens with simple one-octave arpeggios -- not full arpeggio positions spanning the entire neck. If you study transcriptions of Joe Pass, George Benson, or Charlie Parker, you will find that 95% of the arpeggios they use are one-octave shapes played within the scale position they are already in.

The goal of chord tone soloing is not to play arpeggios up and down mechanically. It is to use chord tones as the strong, structural notes in your melodies -- the notes you aim for, land on, and resolve to. Everything else (scale notes, chromatic passing tones, enclosures) serves to connect these harmonic guideposts. As Jens puts it: "It's not about the notes, it's about how you make the arpeggios into a melody."
content:
# Practice Guide: Chord Tone Soloing

## Overview

Chord tone soloing is the essential first step in jazz improvisation. Instead of thinking in scales, you learn to navigate through a song by targeting the notes that make up each chord -- the root, 3rd, 5th, and 7th. This is the approach that Jens Larsen consistently teaches as the foundation for playing solos that actually sound like jazz, rather than just running scales over changes.

As Jens explains: "Most jazz beginners hit a wall with arpeggios and end up playing solos that sound like exercises." The solution is not to abandon chord tones, but to learn how to use them musically. In jazz, the magic happens with simple one-octave arpeggios -- not full arpeggio positions spanning the entire neck. If you study transcriptions of Joe Pass, George Benson, or Charlie Parker, you will find that 95% of the arpeggios they use are one-octave shapes played within the scale position they are already in.

The goal of chord tone soloing is not to play arpeggios up and down mechanically. It is to use chord tones as the strong, structural notes in your melodies -- the notes you aim for, land on, and resolve to. Everything else (scale notes, chromatic passing tones, enclosures) serves to connect these harmonic guideposts. As Jens puts it: "It's not about the notes, it's about how you make the arpeggios into a melody."

## Prerequisites

- Knowledge of one position of the major scale (e.g., C major in the 7th-8th fret area)
- Understanding of what seventh chords are (maj7, m7, dom7, m7b5)
- Ability to play through a simple chord progression (e.g., Autumn Leaves or a II-V-I)
- Basic rhythmic comfort with eighth notes and quarter notes

## Key Concepts

### Chord Tones as Harmonic Guideposts

Every chord in a jazz song has four chord tones:

| Chord | 1 (Root) | 3 | 5 | 7 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cmaj7 | C | E | G | B |
| Dm7 | D | F | A | C |
| G7 | G | B | D | F |

These four notes spell out the harmony. When your solo hits these notes on the strong beats, the listener hears the chord changes. When you miss them, the solo sounds disconnected from the harmony.

### One-Octave Arpeggios, Not Full Positions

Jens is very specific about this: "In jazz you don't spend all your time playing arpeggio positions simply because that's not how you use them in a solo." He points to Joe Pass, who even claimed he rarely used arpeggios -- yet his solos are full of them. The key is that they are one-octave arpeggios used as melodic fragments, not two-octave exercises.

### Target Notes

As Jens teaches in his target notes lesson: "Great jazz solos don't just wander around the fretboard. They're always moving towards something." The best target notes for soloing are typically:
- The **3rd** of each chord (strong, defines the chord quality)
- The **5th** of each chord (surprisingly effective as a target in solos)
- The **7th** works well on dominant chords but can be tricky on minor chords (it sounds too much like the root of the key)

### Diatonic Arpeggios

The most efficient way to practice chord tones is as diatonic arpeggios -- playing the seventh chord arpeggio for each note of the scale. Jens learned this from Barry Harris and calls it one of the most important exercises in jazz: "I learned this exercise from Barry Harris. And this isn't just theory and technique. Playing your arpeggios this way means that they're always right there when you need them for solos."

## Recommended Video Sequence

Watch these Jens Larsen videos in order:

1. **7 Things I Wish Every Jazz Beginner Would Practice** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=478HBkYTI3Y -- Jens's step-by-step method for turning arpeggios into musical solos, starting with the Barry Harris diatonic arpeggio exercise
2. **The 3 Most Important Arpeggios For Jazz** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=EFT_DGLIrGA -- How to use diatonic arpeggios as melodic building blocks in solos, with analysis of Joe Pass and Charlie Parker
3. **Chord Tones on a II V I - Important Skills For Jazz Guitar** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=TPAj4GJDbY0 -- Specific exercises for using chord tones when soloing over the most common jazz progression
4. **Use This Exercise To Get The Most Out Of Arpeggios!** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=PDV0Zpmjc44 -- How to turn ascending and descending arpeggios into real musical phrases
5. **Best approach: Target notes on a II V I** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=q6TRWFf_WQo -- Choosing the right target notes so your solo flows naturally from chord to chord
6. **The Phrasing Skills All The Pros Use But Nobody Talks About** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=f8eH8PWe1mk -- Forward motion, target notes, and connecting ideas across chords like George Benson
7. **Jazz Guitar Is Hard Until You Practice Like THIS!** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=PtY6YHsdO5E -- Building from scales to thirds to triads to seventh chord arpeggios, the step-by-step process

## Practice Routine

### Week 1-2: Foundation

**Daily practice: 20-30 minutes**

**Exercise 1: Diatonic Arpeggios in C Major -- The Barry Harris Exercise (10 min)**

Build up in four steps, all within one position (e.g., 7th-8th fret area in C major):

1. **Play the scale:** C D E F G A B C
2. **Play the scale in 3rds:** C-E, D-F, E-G, F-A, G-B, A-C, B-D
3. **Play diatonic triads:** C-E-G, D-F-A, E-G-B, F-A-C, G-B-D, A-C-E, B-D-F
4. **Play diatonic 7th chord arpeggios:** Cmaj7, Dm7, Em7, Fmaj7, G7, Am7, Bm7b5

As Jens teaches: "Luckily arpeggios are stacks of thirds. So a good exercise to start with is just playing the scale in thirds." Work with a metronome at 60-80 BPM. Play ascending and descending.

**Exercise 2: Arpeggios Through a II-V-I (10 min)**

Take a II-V-I in C major:
- Dm7 arpeggio: D F A C
- G7 arpeggio: G B D F
- Cmaj7 arpeggio: C E G B

Play them connected, ascending and descending. As Jens demonstrates: "The first thing to do is practice soloing with this. Just come up with some lines, use rhythm, and make up some melodies."

Start rubato (no strict time) and compose lines you think sound good. As Jens says: "If you can't compose a decent lick then it's very unlikely that your solos will be full of fantastic phrases."

**Exercise 3: Two-Note Soloing (10 min)**

From Jens's exercise for beginners: limit yourself to just two chord tones per chord. For example:
- Dm7: use F (3rd) and A (5th)
- G7: use B (3rd) and D (5th)
- Cmaj7: use E (3rd) and G (5th)

Choose notes that are close together from chord to chord. Then improvise with just these two notes per chord. Jens explains: "When you take away the stress of picking notes, your brain is free to experiment with rhythm, dynamics, and phrasing."

### Week 3-4: Application

**Daily practice: 20-30 minutes**

**Exercise 4: Add Scale Notes Around Arpeggios (10 min)**

Take your Cmaj7 arpeggio: C E G B. Now "sneak in the notes from the C major scale that surround the arpeggio notes." As Jens teaches: "Instead of jumping straight from arpeggio note to arpeggio note, add a scale note in between. And suddenly your line sounds like music."

Practice composing lines where you use arpeggio notes as the structural skeleton and scale notes as connectors.

**Exercise 5: Triplet Arpeggios (10 min)**

One of the most important bebop devices: play each arpeggio as an eighth-note triplet. As Jens demonstrates: "This is something that immediately gives you a lick." Add a chromatic leading note before the first note of each arpeggio for instant bebop vocabulary.

Practice through the diatonic arpeggios with the triplet rhythm at 80-100 BPM.

**Exercise 6: Solo Over a Standard Using Only Chord Tones (10 min)**

Pick a simple standard (There's No Greater Love, Autumn Leaves). Solo through the entire form using only chord tones (arpeggios) with rhythm. Focus on:
- Starting phrases on different beats (not always beat 1)
- Using quarter notes and half notes, not just eighth notes
- Aiming for the 3rd or 5th of each new chord as your target note

### Week 5-6: Integration

**Exercise 7: Enclosures Around Chord Tones (15 min)**

Jens emphasizes enclosures as a key phrasing technique: "For each note, play a scale note above your target note and then a chromatic note below it." Take the G minor triad (G, Bb, D) and practice enclosures around each note.

Apply enclosures to your II-V-I soloing. As Jens shows with George Benson's solo: "Just on the Gm7 chord, he uses three enclosures."

**Exercise 8: Chromatic Passing Tones (15 min)**

Add chromatic notes between your arpeggio tones. As Jens teaches: "Make sure you resolve those chromatic notes to a chord tone. Just by mixing chromatic and diatonic notes with your arpeggio, you can get something that sounds like real jazz."

Compose 4-5 lines over a II-V-I that combine arpeggios, scale notes, and chromatic passing tones.

## Common Mistakes to Avoid

1. **Running arpeggios up and down mechanically.** Jens warns: "You don't want to just run up and down the arpeggios -- that sounds like an exercise, not music." Use rhythm, change direction, and create melodies.

2. **Using full two-octave arpeggio positions.** Jens is specific: "In jazz, the magic happens with simple one-octave arpeggios. So the most efficient way to practice them is in the scale, not all over the neck."

3. **Practicing arpeggios disconnected from songs.** Always apply to a chord progression. "This exercise makes it possible for you to take a jazz standard and play arpeggios through the entire progression."

4. **Neglecting rhythm and phrasing.** Chord tones are the skeleton, but rhythm is the life. Jens observes: "Too much focus on the right notes and not enough on rhythm and phrasing" is the number one problem for beginners.

5. **Always starting phrases on beat 1.** Jens compares a typical beginner solo to George Benson: the beginner "was starting with a new idea for every bar and was always starting on beat one." Vary your phrase starts.

6. **Only playing ascending arpeggios.** Descending arpeggios are a beautiful, underused melodic device. Practice them equally.

## Key Takeaways

- Chord tones (1, 3, 5, 7) are the structural foundation of jazz soloing
- Practice diatonic arpeggios in the scale, not as isolated position patterns
- Use one-octave arpeggios -- that is how they appear in real jazz solos
- The Barry Harris diatonic arpeggio exercise is the most important exercise you can practice
- Target notes (especially 3rds and 5ths) give your solo direction and connect it to the harmony
- Add scale notes and chromatic notes around chord tones to make melodies, not exercises
- Rhythm and phrasing matter more than note choice

## What's Next

After mastering chord tone soloing, move on to:
- [04 - Arpeggios](04-arpeggios.md) -- Deeper arpeggio practice including superimposed arpeggios and advanced patterns
- [05 - Pentatonic Jazz](05-pentatonic-jazz.md) -- Add pentatonic approaches on top of your chord tone foundation
- [07 - II-V-I Progressions](07-ii-v-i.md) -- Apply chord tone soloing to the backbone of jazz harmony

Understanding of what seventh chords are (maj7, m7, dom7, m7b5)
Ability to play through a simple chord progression (e.g., Autumn Leaves or a II-V-I)
Basic rhythmic comfort with eighth notes and quarter notes

content:
# Practice Guide: Arpeggios

## Overview

Arpeggios are the building blocks of jazz melody. Every great jazz improviser -- from Charlie Parker to Joe Pass to George Benson -- constructs lines that are fundamentally built on arpeggios. As Jens Larsen demonstrates through countless transcription analyses, arpeggios appear in jazz solos far more frequently than pure scale runs. If you study even a short fragment of a Parker solo, "there are more arpeggios than scale runs."

But there is a critical distinction between practicing arpeggios the right way and the wrong way. As Jens warns: "Practicing arpeggios as exercises doesn't help you use them in solos. If you just play exercises and start improvising, it's almost a waste of time." The goal is not to master every arpeggio position across the entire neck. The goal is to learn how arpeggios function as melodic phrases and to develop the skill of weaving them into your solos as musical statements.

Jens compares jazz improvisation to cooking: "Arpeggios are one ingredient, but it doesn't work if it's the only one. And you need to learn how to combine ingredients." This guide will take you from basic diatonic arpeggios through superimposed arpeggios to advanced arpeggio techniques used by bebop masters, always with the emphasis on turning them into music.

## Prerequisites

- Solid knowledge of diatonic seventh chord arpeggios in at least one key (see [03 - Chord Tone Soloing](03-chord-tone-soloing.md))
- Comfortable with one-octave arpeggios for maj7, m7, dom7, and m7b5
- Ability to play through a II-V-I using arpeggios
- Basic understanding of how arpeggios relate to scales (stacking thirds)

## Key Concepts

### The Step-by-Step Build-Up

Jens teaches arpeggios as a four-step process from the scale, which he learned from Barry Harris:

1. **Scale** -- C D E F G A B C
2. **Scale in 3rds** -- C-E, D-F, E-G, F-A, etc.
3. **Diatonic triads** -- C-E-G, D-F-A, E-G-B, etc.
4. **Diatonic 7th chord arpeggios** -- Cmaj7, Dm7, Em7, Fmaj7, G7, Am7, Bm7b5

Each step builds on the previous one, and each is useful in solos.

### One-Octave vs. Full Position

Jens is adamant: "If you look at jazz solos then 90% of the arpeggios used are just the root position and also just the one-octave version." Full two-octave positions are useful for technique, but one-octave arpeggios within your scale position are what you actually use when improvising.

### Ascending and Descending as Melodies

An arpeggio is not just a set of notes -- it is a melody with two basic variations: ascending and descending. As Jens teaches: "Think of it as a melody. The ascending arpeggio is a leap upward that you resolve by moving in the opposite direction. The descending arpeggio works the same way -- opposite directions."

### The Arpeggio from the 3rd

One of the most important concepts Jens teaches: the arpeggio built from the 3rd of a chord is incredibly powerful solo material. For example, over a G7, the arpeggio from the 3rd (B) gives you Bm7b5 (B-D-F-A) -- which contains the 3rd, 5th, 7th, and 9th of G7. This is a superimposed arpeggio that instantly adds color.

### Superimposed Arpeggios

Beyond the basic arpeggio of the chord, you can use arpeggios built on other chord tones to get different extensions:
- **From the 3rd:** adds the 9th
- **From the 5th:** adds the 9th and 11th
- **From the 7th:** adds the 9th, 11th, and 13th

Jens demonstrates this extensively in his lessons on arpeggios for specific chord types, showing how "25 arpeggios can sound amazing on a single chord."

## Recommended Video Sequence

Watch these Jens Larsen videos in order:

1. **The Most Important Arpeggio For Jazz (It Will SKYROCKET Your Playing)** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=wJb2FLRbvVY -- The one arpeggio concept that makes the biggest difference in your soloing
2. **The 3 Most Important Arpeggios For Jazz** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=EFT_DGLIrGA -- Three arpeggio types with analysis of bebop masters, including the descending arpeggio trick
3. **Arpeggios - Things To Get Right From The Beginning** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=oPkYLiT67BU -- Foundation exercises and common mistakes to avoid
4. **Use This Exercise To Get The Most Out Of Arpeggios!** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=PDV0Zpmjc44 -- Counterpoint-inspired exercise for turning arpeggios into phrases
5. **How To Use Arpeggios In Jazz - Important Skills** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=_XLBIzbA9a8 -- Practical application of arpeggios in soloing contexts
6. **7 Ways To Make Arpeggios Sound Great In A Solo** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=RKEOn4jt5HM -- Seven specific techniques for musical arpeggio usage
7. **How To Make One Arpeggio Into 25 Great Jazz Licks** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=b5d47k9MtxQ -- Expanding a single arpeggio into extensive vocabulary
8. **2 5 1 - How To Solo with Diatonic Arpeggios** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=7q2LB45ts0M -- Complete lesson on using arpeggios over the II-V-I progression

## Practice Routine

### Week 1-2: Foundation

**Daily practice: 20-30 minutes**

**Exercise 1: Diatonic 7th Chord Arpeggios in Multiple Keys (10 min)**

Play the diatonic arpeggios through these keys over the first two weeks:
- Week 1: C major, G major, F major
- Week 2: Bb major, Eb major, D major

For each key, play ascending and descending in one position. Know which arpeggio you are playing at all times. Use a metronome at 72-80 BPM, one arpeggio per bar in quarter notes.

**Exercise 2: Triplet Arpeggios with Leading Notes (10 min)**

As Jens teaches from his Barry Harris studies: play each arpeggio as an eighth-note triplet, then add a chromatic leading note before the first note. This "immediately gives you a lick" and "sounds like the Charlie Parker and George Benson solos that you've heard."

Practice ascending and descending versions through the diatonic arpeggios in C major. Tempo: 80 BPM.

**Exercise 3: Pivot Arpeggios -- The Barry Harris Method (10 min)**

Jens explains this exercise from Barry Harris master classes: "First you play the arpeggio and then you end by going down one step in the scale. The second part is the same melody but now you're moving the phrase down an octave except for that first note."

This creates an octave displacement that generates instant bebop vocabulary. Practice on each diatonic arpeggio in C major.

### Week 3-4: Application

**Daily practice: 20-30 minutes**

**Exercise 4: Arpeggio Up, Scale Down (10 min)**

The simplest recipe for musical arpeggios, drawn from Jens's counterpoint-inspired exercise: play the arpeggio ascending, then come back down using scale tones. "The arpeggio is like a leap in one direction that you need to resolve by moving in the opposite direction."

Do not be rigid -- follow your ear. "That very variation sounds great -- go with what you can get to sound good."

**Exercise 5: Superimposed Arpeggios on a Single Chord (10 min)**

Take a Dm7 chord. Play these arpeggios over it:
1. Dm7 (root position) -- basic chord tones
2. Fmaj7 (from the 3rd) -- gives you the 3rd, 5th, 7th, 9th
3. Am7 (from the 5th) -- gives you the 5th, 7th, 9th, 11th

Compose short lines using each arpeggio. Notice how each one colors the Dm7 differently.

Repeat for G7:
1. G7 (root)
2. Bm7b5 (from the 3rd) -- 3rd, 5th, 7th, 9th
3. Dm7 (from the 5th) -- 5th, 7th, 9th, 11th

**Exercise 6: Arpeggios Through a Song (10 min)**

Choose a standard (Autumn Leaves or There Will Never Be Another You). Play through the entire form using one arpeggio per chord. Focus on voice leading from chord to chord -- find the arpeggio note closest to where you are when the chord changes.

### Week 5-6: Integration

**Exercise 7: Combining Arpeggios with Enclosures and Chromatic Notes (15 min)**

Take the ascending triad enclosure exercise from Jens's bebop lesson: for each note of a C major triad, add a scale note above and a chromatic note below. This "is used by everyone including Barry Harris" and creates "instant bebop."

Practice on all diatonic triads and then on seventh chord arpeggios.

**Exercise 8: Compose Lines Combining Multiple Arpeggio Techniques (15 min)**

Over a II-V-I in C major, compose 8-bar lines that combine:
- Ascending arpeggios with scale descents
- Triplet arpeggios with leading notes
- Superimposed arpeggios
- Enclosures targeting chord tones

Write these out. Memorize the ones you like. As Jens teaches: "Learning to compose lines is a skill you need to develop, but I'm sure you can see how it will help your improvisation."

## Common Mistakes to Avoid

1. **Practicing only full arpeggio positions.** Jens is clear: one-octave arpeggios within the scale are what you use in solos. Full positions are technical exercises, not musical building blocks.

2. **Always playing arpeggios ascending from the root.** "Chances are that you're not using descending arpeggios as much, and they are actually less common if you transcribe a lot of solos -- but it is a beautiful sound."

3. **Treating arpeggios as isolated exercises.** As Jens compares to cooking: arpeggios are one ingredient. You need to combine them with scale notes, chromatic notes, and rhythm to make music.

4. **Running through every possible arpeggio shape.** Jens warns: "Most beginners waste time running through every possible arpeggio position and inversion, things they never actually use in real solos."

5. **Neglecting the connection to the scale.** Diatonic arpeggios practiced within the scale position are far more useful than isolated arpeggio shapes because they keep you connected to the key and the surrounding harmony.

6. **Forgetting rhythm.** A triplet arpeggio with a leading note is a lick. A straight eighth-note arpeggio is an exercise. The difference is rhythm.

## Key Takeaways

- Arpeggios are the most important melodic building blocks in jazz -- more common in solos than scale runs
- One-octave arpeggios in the scale position are what you actually use when improvising
- The diatonic arpeggio exercise (from Barry Harris) connects arpeggios to the scale and the harmony
- Superimposed arpeggios (from the 3rd, 5th, or 7th of a chord) add color and extensions
- Triplet rhythms and chromatic leading notes turn arpeggios into instant bebop vocabulary
- Always practice arpeggios in the context of a chord progression or song
- Composing your own lines with arpeggios is essential for developing improvisation skills

## What's Next

After mastering arpeggio practice, move on to:
- [03 - Chord Tone Soloing](03-chord-tone-soloing.md) -- If you need to revisit the basics of using chord tones in solos
- [05 - Pentatonic Jazz](05-pentatonic-jazz.md) -- Add pentatonic patterns on top of your arpeggio foundation for a different melodic flavor
- [07 - II-V-I Progressions](07-ii-v-i.md) -- Apply your arpeggio skills to the most important jazz progression
prerequisites: Solid knowledge of diatonic seventh chord arpeggios in at least one key (see [03 - Chord Tone Soloing](03-chord-tone-soloing.md))
Comfortable with one-octave arpeggios for maj7, m7, dom7, and m7b5
Ability to play through a II-V-I using arpeggios
Basic understanding of how arpeggios relate to scales (stacking thirds)

title: Pentatonic Scales in Jazz
overview:
The pentatonic scale is every guitarist's favorite scale -- and for good reason. It is easy to play, feels great under the fingers, and has a built-in melodic structure that shapes your phrases in ways that scales and arpeggios alone cannot. But most guitarists use the pentatonic scale in a rock or blues context and never explore its enormous potential in jazz. As Jens Larsen teaches, pentatonic scales unlock fresh melodic ideas, modern jazz sounds, and even beautiful chord voicings that you simply will not discover through traditional scale and arpeggio practice.

The key insight Jens emphasizes is this: "Music isn't just about the notes -- it's about the sound. The pentatonic scale has a built-in melodic structure that shapes your phrases, making your lines sound different from typical major scale or arpeggio-based licks." When you use the E minor pentatonic over a C major 7 chord, for example, you are not just playing "C major scale notes" -- you are accessing the 3rd, 5th, 6th (13th), major 7th, and 9th of the chord, and the five-note scale structure creates melodic shapes and intervals that a seven-note scale never would.

This guide covers how to use pentatonic scales on major 7, minor 7, and dominant 7 chords, how to find the right pentatonic for each chord, and how to develop the patterns and substitutions that give your solos a distinctly modern jazz quality.
content:
# Practice Guide: Pentatonic Scales in Jazz

## Overview

The pentatonic scale is every guitarist's favorite scale -- and for good reason. It is easy to play, feels great under the fingers, and has a built-in melodic structure that shapes your phrases in ways that scales and arpeggios alone cannot. But most guitarists use the pentatonic scale in a rock or blues context and never explore its enormous potential in jazz. As Jens Larsen teaches, pentatonic scales unlock fresh melodic ideas, modern jazz sounds, and even beautiful chord voicings that you simply will not discover through traditional scale and arpeggio practice.

The key insight Jens emphasizes is this: "Music isn't just about the notes -- it's about the sound. The pentatonic scale has a built-in melodic structure that shapes your phrases, making your lines sound different from typical major scale or arpeggio-based licks." When you use the E minor pentatonic over a C major 7 chord, for example, you are not just playing "C major scale notes" -- you are accessing the 3rd, 5th, 6th (13th), major 7th, and 9th of the chord, and the five-note scale structure creates melodic shapes and intervals that a seven-note scale never would.

This guide covers how to use pentatonic scales on major 7, minor 7, and dominant 7 chords, how to find the right pentatonic for each chord, and how to develop the patterns and substitutions that give your solos a distinctly modern jazz quality.

## Prerequisites

- Comfortable with at least one minor pentatonic scale position (e.g., E minor or A minor pentatonic)
- Understanding of basic chord types (maj7, m7, dom7)
- Some experience with chord tone soloing (see [03 - Chord Tone Soloing](03-chord-tone-soloing.md))
- Knowledge of the major scale and its modes is helpful but not required

## Key Concepts

### Finding Pentatonic Scales for Each Chord Type

Jens teaches a systematic approach. Every major scale contains three minor pentatonic scales. For C major, they are:

- **A minor pentatonic:** A C D E G
- **D minor pentatonic:** D F G A C
- **E minor pentatonic:** E G A B D

Each of these can be used over chords in the key of C major, but some work better than others depending on the chord.

### Pentatonic Scales on Major 7 Chords

For a Cmaj7 chord:

- **A minor pentatonic** (from the 6th) -- gives you A(6th), C(root), D(9th), E(3rd), G(5th). Essentially a C6/9 sound.
- **E minor pentatonic** (from the 3rd) -- gives you E(3rd), G(5th), A(6th/13th), B(maj7th), D(9th). Richer because it includes the major 7th.
- **B minor pentatonic** (from the Lydian scale, if C is the IV chord in G major) -- gives you B(maj7), D(9th), E(3rd), F#(#11), A(13th). A floating, Lydian sound.

As Jens recommends: "Whenever you're improvising over a maj7 chord, you can use the minor pentatonic scale from the third to get a fresh modern sound."

### Pentatonic Scales on Minor 7 Chords

For a Cm7 chord, the minor pentatonic from the root (C minor pentatonic) works, but also explore:
- **G minor pentatonic** (from the 5th) -- adds the 9th and 11th for a Dorian color
- **D minor pentatonic** -- for an even more extended sound

### Pentatonic Scales on Dominant 7 Chords

This is where things get particularly interesting. On a G7:
- **D minor pentatonic** -- basic dominant sound with 5th, b7, root, 9th, 11th
- **A minor pentatonic** -- adds the 9th and 13th for a brighter color

For altered dominants, you can use pentatonics from melodic minor to access altered tensions.

### Pentatonic Patterns

Jens demonstrates specific pentatonic patterns that create a distinctly different sound from scale runs or arpeggios. Two essential patterns:
1. Groups of three notes ascending through the pentatonic (using small sweeps)
2. Intervallic patterns (skipping notes within the pentatonic)

"These patterns lead to different melodic ideas compared to using arpeggios or traditional scales."

### Pentatonic Chords

A powerful technique Jens teaches: turn the pentatonic scale into three-note chords by playing three adjacent notes at a time. "This is a fill to fit a C major 7, and the pentatonic scale that I'm using here is E minor pentatonic." The combined sound of these chord fragments creates beautiful movement and color.

## Recommended Video Sequence

Watch these Jens Larsen videos in order:

1. **3 Powerful Pentatonic Tricks No One Talks About** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=XMH2UHWMBBA -- Using E minor pentatonic over Cmaj7, pentatonic patterns, and pentatonic chords
2. **The Great Thing About Pentatonic Scales For Jazz And How To Use Them** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=vgc4hsaTWxA -- Overview of how pentatonics work differently in jazz than in rock/blues
3. **5 Pentatonic Scales That Sound Great On A Maj7** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=Om4EI23a9JU -- Five specific pentatonic choices for major 7 chords with sound examples
4. **Pentatonic Scale - How To Not Sound Like The Blues** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=Ld8MpSxwKkY -- Breaking out of blues patterns and into jazz pentatonic usage
5. **7 Pentatonic Tricks That Will Make You Play Better Jazz Solos** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=TMLd2CKsbK0 -- Seven advanced techniques for pentatonic improvisation
6. **Easy Jazz Licks - How To Use The Pentatonic Scale** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=3Fq2Y4AaQ1g -- Simple lick construction using pentatonic scales over jazz changes
7. **Pentatonic Chords are Magic! Amazing New Way To Use The Scale!** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=R78USz7oQg8 -- Turning pentatonic scales into beautiful chord runs and fills
8. **Soloing over a II V I with Pentatonic scales** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=5mIZGywe8tA -- Applying pentatonic scales to the most important jazz progression

## Practice Routine

### Week 1-2: Foundation

**Daily practice: 20-30 minutes**

**Exercise 1: E Minor Pentatonic over Cmaj7 (10 min)**

Play the E minor pentatonic scale (E G A B D) over a Cmaj7 vamp or drone. Notice how each note relates to the chord:
- E = 3rd
- G = 5th
- A = 13th
- B = maj7
- D = 9th

Improvise freely. Focus on creating melodies that sound different from what you would play with the C major scale. Experiment with different rhythms and phrase lengths. Tempo: 100-120 BPM medium swing.

**Exercise 2: Three Pentatonic Scales in C Major (10 min)**

Play through all three minor pentatonics available in C major:
1. A minor pentatonic (in one position)
2. D minor pentatonic (in one position)
3. E minor pentatonic (in one position)

For each, play over a Cmaj7 chord and listen to how the character changes. E minor will sound the most "jazzy" because of the B (major 7th).

**Exercise 3: Pentatonic Patterns (10 min)**

Learn these two essential patterns on E minor pentatonic:

**Pattern 1 -- Groups of 3:** E-G-A, G-A-B, A-B-D, B-D-E (ascending)
**Pattern 2 -- Skip pattern:** E-A, G-B, A-D, B-E (ascending in intervals)

Practice ascending and descending. These create the melodic shapes that Jens describes as "different from typical major scale or arpeggio-based licks." Use a metronome at 80-100 BPM.

### Week 3-4: Application

**Daily practice: 20-30 minutes**

**Exercise 4: Pentatonic Scales on Each Chord Type (10 min)**

Build a reference chart and practice each:

| Chord | Pentatonic Choice | Scale Degree |
|---|---|---|
| Cmaj7 | E minor pent | from the 3rd |
| Dm7 | A minor pent | from the 5th (Dorian color) |
| G7 | D minor pent | from the 5th |
| Am7 | C minor pent | from the b3 (or E minor pent from 5th) |

Play each pentatonic over its chord with a backing track. Focus on hearing the relationship.

**Exercise 5: Pentatonic Solo on Autumn Leaves (10 min)**

Solo through Autumn Leaves using only pentatonic scales. For each chord, choose an appropriate pentatonic and stay within it. This forces you to hear the chord changes through the pentatonic lens. As Jens demonstrates in his lesson on playing standards with pentatonic scales, this is a powerful constraint that develops new melodic ideas.

**Exercise 6: Mixing Pentatonic and Arpeggio Approaches (10 min)**

As Jens shows: "You can even mix them -- starting with an arpeggio and then going into pentatonic patterns." Practice transitioning between:
- Arpeggio phrase leading into a pentatonic phrase
- Pentatonic phrase resolving on a chord tone
- Alternating between approaches every two bars

### Week 5-6: Integration

**Exercise 7: Pentatonic Chords (15 min)**

Take E minor pentatonic and play three notes at a time on strings 4-3-2 or 3-2-1, moving through the position:

As Jens teaches: "I'm turning the pentatonic scales into chords by playing three notes at a time. I'm really just thinking of them as playing notes in the scale -- I'm not thinking four different chords."

Practice these chord runs as fills over Cmaj7, then try them over Dm7 (using A minor pentatonic) and G7 (using D minor pentatonic).

**Exercise 8: Pentatonic Substitutions on Dominant Chords (15 min)**

For more advanced players, explore using pentatonic scales from outside the key on dominant chords:
- On G7, try Db minor pentatonic for an altered sound
- On G7, try Eb minor pentatonic for a Lydian dominant color

These create tension that resolves when you move to the I chord. Practice on a II-V-I, using the "outside" pentatonic only on the V chord.

## Common Mistakes to Avoid

1. **Only using the minor pentatonic from the root.** On a Cm7, C minor pentatonic works but is the least interesting choice. The pentatonic from the 5th (G minor) or 3rd adds more color.

2. **Playing the same blues licks over jazz changes.** As Jens teaches, the pentatonic scale in jazz requires different phrasing, patterns, and rhythmic approaches than in blues or rock.

3. **Ignoring the chord tones within the pentatonic.** Even when using pentatonics, be aware of which notes are chord tones and which are extensions. Landing on a chord tone on a strong beat still matters.

4. **Using D minor pentatonic on Cmaj7.** As Jens notes: "The D minor pentatonic is not really going to sound like a C major seven, mainly because of the F." Be selective about which pentatonic you choose.

5. **Not exploring pentatonic chords.** This is a unique and powerful application that most guitarists overlook entirely. The chord runs created from pentatonic groupings are "surprisingly easy and already built into the way that we play the scale."

## Key Takeaways

- The pentatonic scale has a built-in melodic structure that creates phrases distinct from scale runs and arpeggios
- For maj7 chords, use the minor pentatonic from the 3rd (e.g., E minor pent over Cmaj7) for the richest jazz sound
- Each major key contains three minor pentatonic scales you can use
- Pentatonic patterns (groups of 3, intervallic skips) create fresh melodic ideas
- Pentatonic chords (three-note groupings) make beautiful fills and chord runs
- Mix pentatonic approaches with arpeggios for variety in your solos
- The pentatonic scale works on all chord types -- maj7, m7, and dom7 -- with different scale choices for each

## What's Next

After mastering pentatonic jazz applications, move on to:
- [03 - Chord Tone Soloing](03-chord-tone-soloing.md) -- Revisit chord tone foundations to strengthen your pentatonic target notes
- [04 - Arpeggios](04-arpeggios.md) -- Combine pentatonic approaches with arpeggio vocabulary
- [06 - Jazz Blues](06-jazz-blues.md) -- The perfect context for mixing blues pentatonics with jazz pentatonic approaches
prerequisites: Comfortable with at least one minor pentatonic scale position (e.g., E minor or A minor pentatonic)
Understanding of basic chord types (maj7, m7, dom7)
Some experience with chord tone soloing (see [03 - Chord Tone Soloing](03-chord-tone-soloing.md))
Knowledge of the major scale and its modes is helpful but not required

content:
# Practice Guide: Jazz Blues

## Overview

Jazz blues is where jazz guitar begins for most players, and it remains one of the most important forms throughout your entire musical life. As Jens Larsen shares: "Jazz blues is an amazing sound. It was one of the first things that got me interested in jazz, and I still remember being completely blown away the first time I heard Charlie Parker and Joe Pass play amazing jazz blues solos."

But there is a crucial distinction that many beginners miss: jazz blues is not the same as regular blues. The tempo, phrasing, techniques, and vocabulary are completely different. As Jens puts it: "If you listen to how Wes Montgomery plays a blues solo, it's very different from the phrasing and feel of someone like Albert King. This isn't about one being better than the other. They're both great, but I don't want Wes to sound like Albert King, or the other way around."

The jazz blues form (typically in F or Bb) is the gateway to jazz harmony, comping, and soloing. It introduces you to dominant 7th chords, II-V-I progressions, and the art of blending blues vocabulary with bebop language. Jens spent years figuring out this balance, and his key insight is counterintuitive: "Surprisingly, the solution wasn't about adding complexity. It was about simplifying things."

## Prerequisites

- Comfortable with shell voicings for dom7, m7, and maj7 chords
- Basic knowledge of the blues scale or minor pentatonic scale
- Ability to comp through a basic 12-bar blues
- Familiarity with the sound of jazz -- listen to some Joe Pass, Wes Montgomery, or Grant Green blues recordings

## Key Concepts

### The Jazz Blues Chord Progression (in Bb)

The basic jazz blues progression Jens uses for comping lessons:

| Bar | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1-4 | Bb7 | Eb7 | Bb7 | Bb7 |
| 5-8 | Eb7 | Edim | Bb7 | G7 |
| 9-12 | Cm7 | F7 | Bb7 | (Cm7 F7) |

For the comping lesson in Bb, Jens simplifies: "You only need six chords" as shell voicings. The turnaround (bars 11-12) uses a II-V (Cm7 - F7) to cycle back.

### Jazz Blues Soloing -- Keep It Simple

Jens's most important insight for jazz blues soloing: limit the amount of notes you use. "Bebop lines on an F7 chord are often packed with chromaticism, arpeggios, and extensions. But for jazz blues, it's much more about phrasing and rhythm than cramming in a lot of notes."

The approach:
1. Start with just the **basic triad** of each chord (e.g., F major triad for F7)
2. Keep phrases **short** -- use quarter notes, not endless eighth-note runs
3. Stay within **one octave** -- do not run up and down the entire neck
4. Focus on **rhythm and phrasing** over note choice

As Jens observes about Wes Montgomery: "Wes isn't playing long flowing streams of eighth notes. Instead, the phrases are a lot shorter, and he's really focusing on using quarter notes."

### Triads as the Foundation

For jazz blues, the major triad of each dominant chord is your primary tool:
- F7 chord: use F major triad (F A C)
- Bb7 chord: use Bb major triad (Bb D F)

"The basic triad of the chord is often all you need for a blues."

### The Pentatonic Scale Expansion

After triads, expand to the minor pentatonic/blues scale, but use it differently than in rock:
- Focus on chord tones within the pentatonic
- Use the pentatonic scale to create riff-based phrases
- Add the 6th (13th) for a jazzier color

### Phrasing and Rhythm

Jazz blues phrasing fundamentals:
- Use **pickups** (short notes before the phrase starts)
- Leave **space** between phrases
- **Quarter notes** are more important than eighth notes
- Phrases typically stay within a small range (one octave or less)
- Accents on offbeats give the jazz bounce

## Recommended Video Sequence

Watch these Jens Larsen videos in order:

1. **Make Sure You Practice Jazz Blues Like This!** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=1u5gOCEBujY -- The complete guide to jazz blues soloing, from triads to pentatonics, with Wes Montgomery analysis
2. **Exactly How To Nail Jazz Blues Comping (It's Simple!)** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=C8cziTSa6pA -- Jazz blues comping with shell voicings, rhythm development, and Joe Pass-style fills
3. **Jazz Blues Chords - How To Make It Sound Like Jazz** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=3XfB7SHVoH8 -- Making simple chords sound like real jazz on a blues
4. **Jazz Blues - 3 Easy Techniques That Make You Sound Better** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=JOLcMkAMr70 -- Three accessible techniques for improving your blues soloing
5. **The PRO way to mix Blues And Jazz!** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=A3biCrvGrOY -- How to balance blues vocabulary with jazz vocabulary
6. **Jazz Blues - How To Solo Only Using Triads And Why It Is Powerful** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=gt7e8PZttpU -- The triad-only approach to jazz blues soloing
7. **5 Comping Exercises for Jazz Rhythm on the Blues** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=6U10Q1T1w9c -- Five rhythm exercises specifically for blues comping
8. **A Simple Jazz Blues Approach That Makes You Sound Better** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=zcj3Q8jrEik -- A streamlined approach to sounding good on a jazz blues quickly

## Practice Routine

### Week 1-2: Foundation

**Daily practice: 20-30 minutes**

**Exercise 1: Learn the Jazz Blues Chords with Shell Voicings (10 min)**

In the key of Bb, learn these shell voicings and play through the 12-bar form:

```
| Bb7 | Eb7 | Bb7 | Bb7  |
| Eb7 | Edim | Bb7 | G7   |
| Cm7 | F7  | Bb7 | Cm7 F7 |
```

As Jens teaches: "For a simple jazz blues in Bb, you only need six chords." Play whole notes first, just getting the changes smooth. Then add the Charleston rhythm (beat 1, beat 2-and). Metronome at 120 BPM for a medium swing feel.

**Exercise 2: Split Bass and Chord Comping (10 min)**

Using the shell voicing split technique: play the bass note on beat 1, the chord (rootless, just 3rd and 7th) on the 2-and, then add variations. As Jens explains: "When you want to add more rhythm to your playing, you've got two parts to play with, low and high."

Practice through the entire 12-bar form until it feels easy and groovy.

**Exercise 3: Triad-Only Soloing (10 min)**

Over the jazz blues changes, solo using ONLY major triads:
- Bb7: use Bb major triad (Bb D F)
- Eb7: use Eb major triad (Eb G Bb)
- G7: use G major triad (G B D)
- Cm7: use C minor triad (C Eb G)
- F7: use F major triad (F A C)

Keep phrases short (2-4 notes). Use mostly quarter notes. Stay within one octave. As Jens says: "If you just run up and down the triads, it won't sound like jazz blues."

### Week 3-4: Application

**Daily practice: 20-30 minutes**

**Exercise 4: Add Rhythm and Phrasing to Triad Solos (10 min)**

Now that you can play triads over the changes, add:
- **Pickups:** single notes before phrases (on the "and" of beat 4)
- **Rests:** leave at least one bar of silence between phrases
- **Quarter-note emphasis:** make quarter notes the rhythmic backbone

Record yourself and listen back. Compare your phrasing to Wes Montgomery -- are your phrases short enough? Is there enough space?

**Exercise 5: Expand to Pentatonic Scale (10 min)**

Add the minor pentatonic (or mixolydian) on top of your triads:
- F7: use F mixolydian or F minor pentatonic
- Bb7: use Bb mixolydian or Bb minor pentatonic

Keep the same short phrasing approach but now you have more notes to work with. "The key is to keep the focus on the chord tones. The listener's ear should be drawn toward the chord tones of each chord."

**Exercise 6: Comping with Top-Note Melody (10 min)**

Add melody notes on top of your shell voicings, as Jens teaches: "Instead of jumping between complicated grips, you just change or add a new top note to the chord that you're already playing."

On Bb7, practice adding these top notes: D (3rd), F (5th), G (13th), Ab (b7). Create a short melodic phrase in the top voice while keeping the basic chord underneath.

### Week 5-6: Integration

**Exercise 7: Full Solo with Mixed Vocabulary (15 min)**

Solo over the 12-bar jazz blues in Bb combining:
- Triads
- Pentatonic phrases
- One or two bebop phrases (arpeggio + chromatic enclosure)
- Plenty of space and quarter-note melodies

The goal is to sound like you are playing jazz blues, not bebop over a blues. As Jens learned: "The solution wasn't about adding complexity. It was about simplifying things."

**Exercise 8: Comping with Passing Chords and Fills (15 min)**

Add chromatic passing chords to your shell voicing comping, as Jens demonstrates: "Chromatic leading notes on guitar are often really simple -- you're just looking at the note you want to end on and then shifting the voicing so that you can resolve back into the voicing you want to hit."

Also practice adding melodic fills between comping patterns, the way Joe Pass does.

## Blues Heads to Learn

These are standard jazz blues melodies worth learning:
- **Billie's Bounce** (Charlie Parker) -- key of F
- **Now's the Time** (Charlie Parker) -- key of F
- **Tenor Madness** (Sonny Rollins) -- key of Bb
- **Blue Monk** (Thelonious Monk) -- key of Bb
- **Straight No Chaser** (Thelonious Monk) -- key of F
- **Bag's Groove** (Milt Jackson) -- key of F

Learning these melodies gives you built-in vocabulary and teaches you the phrasing style of jazz blues.

## Common Mistakes to Avoid

1. **Playing rock/blues licks over jazz blues.** As Jens experienced: "I tried to use the vocabulary that I knew, stuff that I picked up from Clapton, Steve Ray Vaughan, Albert King, but that sound didn't translate to jazz blues at all."

2. **Playing too many notes.** Jazz blues is about simplicity and rhythm, not showing off how many notes you know. "It's much more about phrasing and rhythm than cramming in a lot of notes."

3. **Running eighth-note lines continuously.** Wes Montgomery and the jazz blues masters use short phrases with space. "Wes isn't playing long flowing streams of eighth notes."

4. **Staying on one chord's worth of vocabulary for the whole blues.** Even with simple triads, change your notes when the chord changes. Hearing the chord movement is essential.

5. **Ignoring the comping side.** Jazz blues comping is just as important as soloing. The rhythm, groove, and fills you develop while comping feed directly into your solo vocabulary.

## Key Takeaways

- Jazz blues is fundamentally different from rock/blues -- the phrasing, rhythm, and vocabulary are distinct
- Start with the major triad of each chord -- this alone can create convincing jazz blues phrases
- Keep phrases short, use quarter notes, and leave space
- Shell voicings are all you need for effective jazz blues comping
- The split bass/chord technique creates instant groove
- Expand gradually from triads to pentatonics to bebop vocabulary
- Learn classic blues heads (Billie's Bounce, Blue Monk) for built-in jazz blues vocabulary
- Record yourself and listen back -- phrasing and feel are more important than note choice

## What's Next

After mastering the jazz blues, move on to:
- [01 - Shell Voicings](01-shell-voicings.md) -- Revisit shell voicings if your comping needs strengthening
- [05 - Pentatonic Jazz](05-pentatonic-jazz.md) -- Explore pentatonic substitutions on dominant chords in the blues
- [07 - II-V-I Progressions](07-ii-v-i.md) -- The II-V-I turnaround in the blues leads naturally to studying this progression in depth
prerequisites: Comfortable with shell voicings for dom7, m7, and maj7 chords
Basic knowledge of the blues scale or minor pentatonic scale
Ability to comp through a basic 12-bar blues
Familiarity with the sound of jazz -- listen to some Joe Pass, Wes Montgomery, or Grant Green blues recordings
