title: The II-V-I Progression
overview:
The II-V-I progression is the backbone of jazz harmony. It appears in virtually every jazz standard, often multiple times per song, and mastering it is non-negotiable for any jazz guitarist. As Jens Larsen demonstrates, once you can recognize and play II-V-I progressions fluently, you will realize that "you can play most jazz songs" because so much of jazz harmony is built from this single pattern.

The II-V-I consists of three chords built on the 2nd, 5th, and 1st degrees of a major scale. In C major: Dm7 (II) - G7 (V) - Cmaj7 (I). What makes it so powerful is the strong harmonic motion: the V chord creates tension that resolves to the I chord, and the II chord sets up the V with a smooth descending fifth motion. As Joe Pass explains: "If I have a 2-5, forget the two. You don't need it" -- meaning the II chord is really just an elaboration of the V chord, and the whole progression boils down to dominant-to-tonic resolution.

Jens uses the II-V-I as the testing ground for nearly every jazz concept: chord voicings, arpeggios, target notes, enclosures, pentatonic scales, and chromatic language. If you can make it sound good on a II-V-I, you can make it sound good anywhere.
content:
# Practice Guide: The II-V-I Progression

## Overview

The II-V-I progression is the backbone of jazz harmony. It appears in virtually every jazz standard, often multiple times per song, and mastering it is non-negotiable for any jazz guitarist. As Jens Larsen demonstrates, once you can recognize and play II-V-I progressions fluently, you will realize that "you can play most jazz songs" because so much of jazz harmony is built from this single pattern.

The II-V-I consists of three chords built on the 2nd, 5th, and 1st degrees of a major scale. In C major: Dm7 (II) - G7 (V) - Cmaj7 (I). What makes it so powerful is the strong harmonic motion: the V chord creates tension that resolves to the I chord, and the II chord sets up the V with a smooth descending fifth motion. As Joe Pass explains: "If I have a 2-5, forget the two. You don't need it" -- meaning the II chord is really just an elaboration of the V chord, and the whole progression boils down to dominant-to-tonic resolution.

Jens uses the II-V-I as the testing ground for nearly every jazz concept: chord voicings, arpeggios, target notes, enclosures, pentatonic scales, and chromatic language. If you can make it sound good on a II-V-I, you can make it sound good anywhere.

## Prerequisites

- Shell voicings for m7, dom7, and maj7 chords (see [01 - Shell Voicings](01-shell-voicings.md))
- One-octave arpeggios for Dm7, G7, and Cmaj7 within a single scale position
- Basic understanding of the C major scale and where these chords come from
- Ability to hear the resolution from V to I (play G7 to Cmaj7 and really listen to the movement)

## Key Concepts

### Recognizing II-V-I in Standards

Jazz standards are full of II-V-I progressions, both in the main key and in temporary keys. As Jens teaches, being able to spot them instantly makes learning songs dramatically faster:

- **Autumn Leaves:** Cm7 - F7 - Bbmaj7 (II-V-I in Bb), Am7b5 - D7 - Gm (II-V-I in G minor)
- **Fly Me to the Moon:** Am7 - Dm7 - G7 - Cmaj7 (the Dm7-G7-Cmaj7 is a II-V-I in C)
- **All the Things You Are:** packed with II-V-I progressions cycling through keys
- **Satin Doll:** built almost entirely on II-V progressions

As Jens emphasizes: "Once you recognize these, you don't have to learn each chord individually. You're learning groups of chords that belong together."

### The Joe Pass Simplification

Jens discusses Joe Pass's approach at length: Pass often thinks of a II-V as just a V chord. "Instead of thinking about two separate chords, you can just focus on the five chord." Barry Harris teaches the same concept. Even Charlie Parker's solos show this -- "Parker hits a C# right on the first beat [of the II chord] and continues with an A7 arpeggio. He's thinking about the five chord, not the two chord."

This does not mean you ignore the II chord, but it means the notes you play over the II chord can often be the same notes you would play over the V chord, since they share the same scale.

### Voice Leading in Comping

For comping the II-V-I, smooth voice leading is paramount. With shell voicings, the voice leading is built in:
- Dm7 to G7: the 7th (C) drops to the 3rd (B), the 3rd (F) stays close to or becomes the 7th (F)
- G7 to Cmaj7: similar smooth resolution

With Drop 2 voicings, you have four inversions to find the smoothest path.

### Target Notes for Soloing

Jens's target note approach for II-V-I soloing:

- **On the II chord (Dm7):** 3rd (F) and 5th (A) are strong targets. "The seventh [C] sounds too much like a C chord."
- **On the V chord (G7):** 3rd (B) is the strongest target. The 5th (D) and b7 (F) also work well.
- **On the I chord (Cmaj7):** 3rd (E) is a clear resolution point. 5th (G) is also excellent.

As Jens teaches: "Don't just play notes. Aim for a target note. Every phrase should use notes that drive forward and land on that target note."

### Minor II-V-I

The minor version uses a half-diminished chord (m7b5) as the II chord and often an altered dominant as the V:

In D minor: Em7b5 (II) - A7alt (V) - Dm7 or Dm6 (I)

Jens covers this extensively, noting that it opens up melodic minor scale options on the V chord.

## Recommended Video Sequence

Watch these Jens Larsen videos in order:

1. **II V I - You Need To Practice This For Solos** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=5hmSQuMIf-w -- Essential exercises for soloing over the II-V-I progression
2. **II V I - Who Has The Best Approach For Jazz Beginners?** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=foNzxO5rnj4 -- Comparing different approaches to learning II-V-I (Barry Harris, Joe Pass, and more)
3. **Joe Pass's Jazz Chord Trick Changes Everything** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=sXiSlWC09O4 -- How Joe Pass simplifies progressions and what you can learn from it
4. **Best approach: Target notes on a II V I** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=q6TRWFf_WQo -- Choosing the right target notes for melodic II-V-I soloing
5. **2 5 1 - How To Solo with Diatonic Arpeggios** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=7q2LB45ts0M -- Complete lesson on using arpeggios through a II-V-I
6. **Minor II V I - 3 Levels You Want To Know** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=LO8f79Eh6EE -- Three levels of minor II-V-I from basic to advanced
7. **Simple And Easy Approach To A Minor 2 5 1** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=xZxwX6YAJ48 -- A beginner-friendly minor II-V-I lesson
8. **5 Levels Of II V I Jazz Chord Extensions - Comping Boost** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=wU5szdPRJVU -- Five levels of chord extensions for comping the II-V-I

## Practice Routine

### Week 1-2: Foundation

**Daily practice: 20-30 minutes**

**Exercise 1: Shell Voicing II-V-I in All Keys (10 min)**

Play II-V-I with shell voicings in these keys over two weeks:
- Week 1: C, F, Bb, Eb (common flat keys)
- Week 2: G, D, A, Ab, Db (remaining keys)

Use both string sets (root on 5th string and root on 6th string). As Jens demonstrates, the voice leading between these chords should be smooth and close -- no jumping around the neck.

Play with a metronome at 100 BPM, Charleston rhythm.

**Exercise 2: Arpeggios Through the II-V-I (10 min)**

In C major, play the diatonic arpeggios for each chord in the progression:
- Dm7: D F A C
- G7: G B D F
- Cmaj7: C E G B

Practice connecting them smoothly. As Jens teaches: "Just come up with some lines, use rhythm, and make up some melodies. After a while, you'll start to hear stronger melodies and more interesting rhythms."

Start rubato, then add a metronome at 72 BPM. Practice in the keys of C, F, and Bb.

**Exercise 3: Two-Note Per Chord Soloing (10 min)**

From Jens's exercise: pick two chord tones per chord that are close together:
- Dm7: F (3rd) and A (5th)
- G7: B (3rd) and D (5th)
- Cmaj7: E (3rd) and G (5th)

Solo using only these notes. "When you take away the stress of picking notes, your brain is free to experiment with rhythm, dynamics, and phrasing." Focus entirely on creating interesting rhythms and melodies with just these six notes across three chords.

### Week 3-4: Application

**Daily practice: 20-30 minutes**

**Exercise 4: Target Note Practice (10 min)**

Choose a target note for each chord change:
- Land on F (3rd of Dm7) when the II chord arrives
- Land on B (3rd of G7) when the V chord arrives
- Land on E (3rd of Cmaj7) when the I chord arrives

Improvise lines that always aim for these target notes, arriving on beat 1 of each chord. As Jens explains from the George Benson analysis: "His lines don't just fill space. They're aiming for the next chord almost like the melody is pushing you forward."

Gradually vary which target notes you use (try 5ths, then mix 3rds and 5ths).

**Exercise 5: Add Scale Notes and Chromatic Approaches (10 min)**

Expand your II-V-I soloing by adding:
- Diatonic passing tones between arpeggio notes
- Chromatic enclosures before target notes (scale note above, chromatic note below)
- Chromatic passing tones that resolve to chord tones

As Jens teaches: "You can level up the lines and add some real jazz character by adding chromatic passing notes. Make sure you resolve those chromatic notes to a chord tone."

**Exercise 6: Spot II-V-Is in a Standard (10 min)**

Take a lead sheet for Autumn Leaves or All the Things You Are. Circle every II-V-I progression. Note:
- What key is each II-V-I in?
- Does it resolve (complete I chord) or is it a II-V that moves somewhere else?
- Are there consecutive II-V-Is in different keys?

Then play the shell voicings through the song, thinking in terms of II-V-I groups rather than individual chords.

### Week 5-6: Integration

**Exercise 7: Minor II-V-I (15 min)**

Learn the minor II-V-I in D minor: Em7b5 - A7 - Dm7 (or Dm6)

Play shell voicings, then arpeggios. For the A7, experiment with:
- Basic A7 arpeggio (A C# E G)
- Altered extensions (Bb melodic minor scale over A7alt)
- As Jens teaches in his minor II-V-I lessons, the altered dominant opens up beautiful tension and resolution

Practice in keys of D minor, G minor, C minor, and A minor.

**Exercise 8: Combine Comping and Soloing (15 min)**

Over a backing track or metronome, alternate between:
- 2 choruses of comping the II-V-I (with rhythm variations and passing chords)
- 2 choruses of soloing over the II-V-I (with target notes, arpeggios, and chromatic language)

This builds the complete skill set. Record yourself and listen for:
- Is the voice leading smooth in your comping?
- Do your solo lines clearly outline the chord changes?
- Is there forward motion -- does each phrase lead to the next chord?

## Recognizing II-V-I in Common Standards

Here is a quick reference for spotting II-V-I in songs you likely already know:

| Standard | Key II-V-I Examples |
|---|---|
| Autumn Leaves | Cm7-F7-Bbmaj7 (Bb major); Am7b5-D7-Gm (G minor) |
| Satin Doll | Dm7-G7 (C); Em7-A7 (D); repeated throughout |
| Fly Me to the Moon | Dm7-G7-Cmaj7 (C major); Bm7b5-E7-Am7 (A minor) |
| All the Things You Are | Multiple keys: Ab, C, Eb, G... II-V-Is everywhere |
| There Will Never Be Another You | Cm7-F7-Bbmaj7 (Bb); Am7b5-D7 (G minor) |

## Common Mistakes to Avoid

1. **Treating the II and V as completely separate chords.** As Joe Pass and Barry Harris teach, the II-V is essentially one harmonic unit. "The notes in the two and the five scales are exactly the same."

2. **Not voice-leading between chords.** Random chord shapes do not sound like jazz. The beauty of II-V-I comping comes from smooth, close voice leading.

3. **Soloing without target notes.** Without a destination, your lines will sound like "you're just trying out some notes and there's no direction in what you're playing."

4. **Ignoring the resolution.** The whole point of II-V-I is the resolution to I. Make sure your V chord lines resolve convincingly to the I chord.

5. **Only practicing in one key.** II-V-I appears in every key. Practice in at least 6-8 keys to be prepared for real standards.

6. **Skipping minor II-V-I.** Many standards contain minor II-V-I progressions. They use different chord qualities (m7b5 and altered dominant) and require separate practice.

## Key Takeaways

- The II-V-I is the most common chord progression in jazz -- learn to spot it in every standard
- Shell voicings with smooth voice leading are all you need for effective II-V-I comping
- The II and V chords share the same scale -- you can think of them as one harmonic unit
- Target notes (especially the 3rd of each chord) give your solos direction and forward motion
- Start soloing with just arpeggios, then add scale notes and chromatic approaches gradually
- Minor II-V-I uses m7b5 and altered dominant -- practice it separately
- Practice in all keys, starting with the most common: C, F, Bb, Eb, G, D

## What's Next

After mastering the II-V-I, apply your skills to:
- [01 - Shell Voicings](01-shell-voicings.md) -- Deepen your comping vocabulary with shell voicing rhythm variations
- [02 - Drop 2 Voicings](02-drop2-voicings.md) -- Voice-lead Drop 2 chords through II-V-I for richer harmonic comping
- [03 - Chord Tone Soloing](03-chord-tone-soloing.md) -- Expand your soloing language beyond arpeggios
- [06 - Jazz Blues](06-jazz-blues.md) -- The jazz blues turnaround is a II-V-I -- apply everything you learned here
prerequisites: Shell voicings for m7, dom7, and maj7 chords (see [01 - Shell Voicings](01-shell-voicings.md))
One-octave arpeggios for Dm7, G7, and Cmaj7 within a single scale position
Basic understanding of the C major scale and where these chords come from
Ability to hear the resolution from V to I (play G7 to Cmaj7 and really listen to the movement)

content:
# Practice Guide: Bebop Vocabulary

## Overview

Bebop is the foundation of jazz language. As Christina Bryce puts it, "Bebop language is still modern day language." If you want your solos to sound like jazz, you need bebop vocabulary -- there is no shortcut around it. The rhythmic playfulness, the chromatic passing tones, the way phrases start and stop in unpredictable places -- that is the essence of the sound.

Jens Larsen's approach to bebop is deeply influenced by what he learned from Barry Harris at the Royal Conservatory in The Hague. Rather than drilling bebop scales up and down (which Jens openly dislikes), the focus is on learning bebop as **melody and flow**. The lines that Charlie Parker, Joe Pass, and George Benson play are elegant because of how they use rhythm and chromatic embellishment -- not because they are running a scale with a passing tone stuck in. Barry Harris could explain bebop melodies in ways that made sense, breaking phrases into flexible chunks that you can internalize and make your own.

The goal is not to memorize licks. It is to understand the building blocks -- enclosures, approach notes, chromatic phrases, and rhythmic devices -- so you can create your own lines that have that authentic bebop flow.

## Prerequisites

- Comfortable with major scale positions (at least one position per key)
- Know diatonic seventh chord arpeggios in at least one key
- Can play through a II-V-I progression with basic chord tones
- Familiar with shell voicings and basic jazz chord progressions

## Key Concepts

**Bebop is about rhythm, not just notes.** Jens emphasizes that "the lines I played were correct but way too boring." Having the right scale and arpeggio means nothing if your lines lack rhythmic interest. Bebop lines change direction on offbeats, not just on beats 1 and 3, creating a lighter, more interesting feel.

**Chromatic enclosures** are the single most important bebop device. An enclosure surrounds a target note with a diatonic note above and a chromatic note below. For a G major triad, the enclosure around G would be: A (scale note above), F# (chromatic note below), resolving to G. This adds tension that resolves beautifully on a chord tone, changes the melodic direction, and gives your lines forward momentum.

**Barry Harris chromatic scale** is a systematic way to add chromatic passing tones. When there is a half step between two scale notes (e.g., E to F in C major), you simply add the chromatic note. When there is no half step, you use the scale note above instead. This produces some of the most interesting and "non-chromatic" passing tones that Barry used constantly.

**One-octave arpeggios** are the real building blocks. Joe Pass says he does not use arpeggios much, but transcribe his solos and you will find one-octave arpeggios everywhere -- 95% of the time. Full position arpeggios are not how bebop works in practice.

**Triplet rhythms** are essential for bebop energy. Playing an arpeggio as an eighth-note triplet with an added chromatic leading note is one of the most common bebop devices. Parker, Benson, and Pass all use this constantly.

## Recommended Video Sequence

Watch these Jens Larsen videos in order:

1. **The Most Important Exercise In Bebop!** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=orGk08z9O30 -- Chromatic enclosures on triads, the Barry Harris chromatic scale, and how to turn simple exercises into instant bebop vocabulary
2. **Why Barry Harris' Approach Is So Much Better Than Bebop Scales!** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=Jria4wPiwdA -- Why running bebop scales misses the point, how Barry teaches melody and flow, enclosures on triads, and the chromatic scale system
3. **The 5 Best Bebop Shortcuts To Fix Your Jazz Phrasing** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=B6Ml7KWDiPw -- Five practical blocks for adding rhythm to your lines, including large interval skips, chromatic passing notes, and legato embellishments inspired by Barry Harris
4. **Why Patterns Are Always Better Than Licks - Parker's Strategy** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=wP-wpC0axP0 -- How Charlie Parker thinks in patterns rather than memorized licks, and how to adopt this approach
5. **The 3 Bebop Licks You Need To Know** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=2iFZdLf7a1o -- Three essential bebop phrases broken down with applications on common progressions
6. **10 Classic Charlie Parker Licks That Will Make You A Better Player** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=L_fhwdcXzr4 -- Study Parker's actual vocabulary and learn to extract patterns from his solos
7. **The 10 Levels of Bebop and The Skills You Want To Master** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=-uQI0c6r89k -- A roadmap from beginner to advanced bebop, covering the skills at each level
8. **This Is Why You Should Study Bebop** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=td2f43uohMc -- The broader case for why bebop study is essential for all jazz styles

## Practice Routine

### Week 1-2: Foundation -- Enclosures and Chromatic Approach Notes

**Daily practice: 20-30 minutes**

- **Exercise 1: Ascending triad enclosures (10 min).** Take a C major triad (C, E, G). For each note, play a scale note above and a chromatic note below, then resolve to the target. So for C: D, B, C. For E: F, D#, E. For G: A, F#, G. Practice this ascending through the triad until it flows. Then apply to G major, F major, and Bb major triads. Stay in one fretboard position (e.g., 7th-10th fret area for C major).

- **Exercise 2: Descending triad enclosures (5 min).** Same concept but descending. The arpeggio moves down while each enclosure moves up. This contrast is part of what makes it sound like bebop.

- **Exercise 3: Extend to seventh chord arpeggios (10 min).** Apply the enclosure concept to a Cmaj7 arpeggio (C, E, G, B). Add scale notes around each arpeggio tone. Compose short lines -- play rubato, find phrases that sound good to you. As Jens says, "if you can't compose a decent lick then it's very unlikely that your solos will be full of fantastic phrases."

- **Exercise 4: Barry Harris chromatic scale (5 min).** In C major, play the scale adding chromatic passing tones between notes a whole step apart (C-C#-D-D#-E-*G*-F-F#-G-G#-A-A#-B-*D*-C). Notice the spots where you use the scale note above instead of a chromatic note -- those are the interesting ones. Practice slowly until you can play it evenly.

### Week 3-4: Application -- Building Lines

**Daily practice: 20-30 minutes**

- **Exercise 5: Enclosures over a II-V-I in C major (10 min).** Take Dm7-G7-Cmaj7. For each chord, use the triad of that chord and add enclosures around the chord tones. Compose lines rubato first, then with a metronome at 100 BPM. Target the third and fifth of each chord as arrival points.

- **Exercise 6: Triplet arpeggio with leading note (10 min).** Play each diatonic arpeggio as an eighth-note triplet with a chromatic leading note before the first note. For Dm7: C#-D-F-A. For G7: F#-G-B-D. Practice through the entire C major scale, then use on a II-V-I. This is "instant bebop" -- Parker and Benson use it constantly.

- **Exercise 7: Compose 4-bar bebop phrases (10 min).** Over a II-V-I backing track, compose lines that combine enclosures, triplet arpeggios, and scale fragments. Record yourself. Focus on changing direction on offbeats, not just on beats 1 and 3. End phrases on offbeats -- that is literally where the name "bebop" comes from.

### Week 5-6: Integration -- Real Vocabulary

**Daily practice: 20-30 minutes**

- Transcribe 4-8 bars of a Charlie Parker blues solo (e.g., KC Blues, Now's The Time, or Blues for Alice). Identify the enclosures, the approach notes, the triplet arpeggios. Learn the phrase on guitar, then make variations.
- Apply your bebop vocabulary to a jazz blues in F or Bb. Use a backing track. Focus on mixing bebop eighth-note lines with shorter blues-influenced phrases.
- Practice over Autumn Leaves or There's No Greater Love. Use enclosures to target the third of each chord at the point of chord change.

## Common Mistakes to Avoid

1. **Running bebop scales up and down.** As Jens says, "the lines you get are really boring." Having chord tones on every downbeat via a scale is not the same as playing bebop. Focus on melody, not scale patterns.

2. **Only changing direction on downbeats.** This makes your lines heavy and predictable. You need to also change direction on offbeats -- that is what makes bebop light and rhythmically interesting.

3. **Accenting every downbeat.** This sounds like Texas boogie, not bebop. Practice playing eighth notes more evenly so you can place accents intentionally on notes that matter within the line.

4. **Using full-position arpeggios.** Jazz solos use one-octave arpeggios 95% of the time. Drilling two-octave patterns up and down the neck is not efficient for building real vocabulary.

5. **Memorizing licks without understanding the pattern.** Parker thinks in patterns that he can move, invert, and transform. If you memorize a lick as a finger pattern, you cannot use it flexibly. Understand the enclosure, the approach note, the rhythmic device -- then make your own variations.

## Key Takeaways

- Bebop is about rhythm and melodic flow, not running scales with a passing tone
- Chromatic enclosures (diatonic note above, chromatic note below, target note) are the single most important building block
- The Barry Harris chromatic scale provides a systematic framework for adding chromatic notes
- One-octave arpeggios played as triplets with leading notes are "instant bebop"
- Always compose your own lines from the building blocks -- that is how you internalize the language
- End phrases on offbeats, change direction on offbeats, and place accents intentionally
- Record yourself -- you cannot tell how you really sound while you are busy playing

## What's Next

After mastering this, move on to:
- [09 - Altered Dominant](09-altered-dominant.md) -- Adding tension to your V7 chords using the altered scale
- [11 - Rhythm Changes](11-rhythm-changes.md) -- Apply your bebop vocabulary to one of the most important jazz forms
prerequisites: Comfortable with major scale positions (at least one position per key)
Know diatonic seventh chord arpeggios in at least one key
Can play through a II-V-I progression with basic chord tones
Familiar with shell voicings and basic jazz chord progressions

The key insight is that you should not start from theory. You should start from the **sound**. As Jens says in his video on the topic, "It is the sound that is important."

An altered dominant is a dominant 7th chord where the extensions are altered -- sharp 9, flat 9, flat 13, flat 5 (sharp 11) -- and it is almost always a chord that **resolves**. The altered scale is the 7th mode of melodic minor. For G7 altered, you play Ab melodic minor from G to G. This is not just abstract theory -- it is an incredibly practical shortcut. If you already know your melodic minor scales, you already know your altered scales. You just need to learn how to hear them, how each note resolves, and how to create lines that exploit that tension-resolution relationship.
content:
# Practice Guide: Altered Dominant

## Overview

The altered scale is how you add tension and that "cool outside sound" to your solos on dominant 7th chords. For many players, it feels confusing and abstract -- Jens struggled with it too until his teacher gave him a simple trick that made everything click. The key insight is that you should not start from theory. You should start from the **sound**. As Jens says in his video on the topic, "It is the sound that is important."

An altered dominant is a dominant 7th chord where the extensions are altered -- sharp 9, flat 9, flat 13, flat 5 (sharp 11) -- and it is almost always a chord that **resolves**. G7 altered resolves to Cmaj7. D7 altered resolves to Gmaj7. That resolution is what makes it work. Without it, the altered sound just floats aimlessly.

The altered scale is the 7th mode of melodic minor. For G7 altered, you play Ab melodic minor from G to G. This is not just abstract theory -- it is an incredibly practical shortcut. If you already know your melodic minor scales, you already know your altered scales. You just need to learn how to hear them, how each note resolves, and how to create lines that exploit that tension-resolution relationship.

## Prerequisites

- Comfortable soloing over basic II-V-I progressions with diatonic material
- Know your major scale and basic bebop vocabulary (see [08 - Bebop Vocabulary](08-bebop-vocabulary.md))
- Can play shell voicings for maj7, min7, and dom7 chords
- Familiar with at least one position of the melodic minor scale

## Key Concepts

**The altered scale = 7th mode of melodic minor.** For G7 altered, the scale is Ab melodic minor played from G to G: G, Ab, Bb, B, Db, Eb, F, G. The notes are: root, b9, #9, 3, b5(#11), b13, b7. Notice that the major third (B) is not the third note in the scale -- it comes after the #9. This is unusual but makes the scale rich with tension.

**Every note except the root wants to resolve.** This is Jens's Rule #1 and the most important thing to understand. Each altered note has a natural resolution to a note in the target chord:
- Ab (b9) resolves down to G (5th of Cmaj7)
- Bb (#9) resolves down to G through the Ab
- B (3rd) resolves up to C (root of Cmaj7)
- Db (b5) resolves down to C (root of Cmaj7)
- Eb (b13) resolves up to E (3rd of Cmaj7)
- F (b7) resolves down to E (3rd of Cmaj7)

Understanding these resolutions lets you create three-note altered licks that actually sound musical.

**Hear it as a sound, not a formula.** Jens recommends comparing an unaltered G7 to an altered G7 in the same II-V-I progression. The altered version has much more tension on the V chord -- the b9, b5, and b13 create strong voice-leading motion into the resolution chord. Play both versions back to back until you can hear the difference instantly.

**Tritone substitution is a gateway to altered sounds.** A Db7 (tritone sub of G7) uses many of the same notes as G7 altered. Db Lydian dominant = Ab melodic minor = G altered. This means any line you play thinking "Db7" over a G7 chord will give you altered sounds.

## Recommended Video Sequence

Watch these Jens Larsen videos in order:

1. **Altered Scale - Make Sure You Learn This First!** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=EfV5QZD5SzA -- The foundation: three essential rules, how each note resolves, and the trick that made it click for Jens
2. **It Is The Sound That Is Important! - Altered Scale** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=QEF4R2mTqNU -- Learning the altered sound by ear first, with examples from Wes Montgomery and Joe Pass, plus how to hear resolutions
3. **Altered Scale - How To Make It Sound Amazing** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=pAkipvT2CtQ -- Taking the foundations further with actual solo lines and more advanced applications
4. **Tritone Substitution Is a Really Great Hack** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=NlqvZ0Q_Wpk -- How tritone subs connect to altered dominants, with diatonic arpeggio exploration over melodic minor
5. **The Most Important Melodic Minor Modes In One Song** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=RBpagEeInfY -- See altered dominant in context alongside other melodic minor modes on Bernie's Tune
6. **3 Ways to Play an Altered Dominant Chord** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=7Ma8nNHo_EA -- Practical voicings for altered dominant chords on guitar
7. **Altered Scale - 3 Great Pentatonic Solutions** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=6L46JP5DZrA -- Simplified pentatonic approaches to navigating altered sounds

## Practice Routine

### Week 1-2: Foundation -- Hearing and Playing the Scale

**Daily practice: 20-30 minutes**

- **Exercise 1: Learn the scale shape (5 min).** For G7 altered, play Ab melodic minor from G to G in one position (e.g., 3rd fret area on the 6th string). Finger it so that G is on the 6th string 3rd fret, then Ab-Bb-B-Db-Eb-F-G ascending. Know which note is which interval: root, b9, #9, 3, b5, b13, b7.

- **Exercise 2: Compare altered vs. unaltered in a II-V-I (10 min).** Play Dm7-G7-Cmaj7 with shell voicings. First use a normal G Mixolydian line on the G7. Then play the same progression using G altered on the V chord. Go back and forth. Let your ear absorb the difference. This is the most important exercise at this stage.

- **Exercise 3: Resolution pairs (10 min).** Play each note of the altered scale on G7, then resolve it to its target on Cmaj7. Ab resolves to G. Bb resolves through Ab to G. B resolves up to C. Db resolves down to C. Eb resolves up to E. F resolves down to E. Play these as two-note phrases with the chord change underneath.

- **Exercise 4: Three-note altered licks (5 min).** Combine an altered note, its resolution, and one connecting tone. For example: Eb (b13) - E (3rd of Cmaj7) - D (passing tone). Or: Ab (b9) - G (5th of Cmaj7) - A (6th). Compose your own three-note phrases and play them over the V-I change.

### Week 3-4: Application -- Building Lines Over II-V-I

**Daily practice: 20-30 minutes**

- **Exercise 5: Diatonic arpeggios within the altered scale (10 min).** The arpeggios built on the notes of Ab melodic minor give you powerful sounds. Try: Abm(maj7) -- a minor-major 7 arpeggio starting on the b9. Or Bmaj7#5 -- a major 7 sharp 5 from the 3rd. Or Db7 -- a dominant 7 from the b5. Each gives you a different color within the altered sound.

- **Exercise 6: Tritone sub approach (10 min).** On G7 altered, think Db7 and play Db Mixolydian #11 (Lydian dominant) material. Use a Db major triad, a Db7 arpeggio, or a Db Lydian dominant scale fragment. This often feels more natural than thinking "G altered" because you have a clear root to orient around.

- **Exercise 7: Full II-V-I lines with altered V (10 min).** Over Dm7-G7alt-Cmaj7, play diatonic material on Dm7, switch to altered material on G7, and resolve cleanly to Cmaj7. The goal is seamless connection. Target the 3rd or 5th of Cmaj7 as your arrival note from the altered line.

### Week 5-6: Integration -- In Context on Standards

**Daily practice: 20-30 minutes**

- Apply altered dominant to every V7 chord in Autumn Leaves. Not every V7 needs to be altered -- learn to mix altered and unaltered for variety.
- Practice on a jazz blues in Bb. The I7, IV7, and V7 chords can all take altered sounds when they resolve. Start with just the V7 (F7 altered resolving to Bb).
- Transcribe a short Wes Montgomery or Joe Pass passage that uses altered dominant. Identify the altered notes and their resolutions. Learn it in the original key, then transpose to two other keys.

## Common Mistakes to Avoid

1. **Using the altered scale on non-resolving dominants.** The altered scale creates tension that needs to resolve. On a static funk vamp on a dominant chord, altered will sound wrong. Save it for V7 chords that move to I.

2. **Thinking of it as a separate scale to memorize.** If you know Ab melodic minor, you know G altered. Do not learn another fingering pattern -- use the melodic minor relationship.

3. **Starting with theory instead of sound.** As Jens says, "the problem is that it's not music when I'm describing it with numbers and letters." Play the altered sound in context, compare it to unaltered, and let your ears guide you before you analyze.

4. **Playing the altered scale as a scale run.** Running up and down the altered scale is not how it is used in solos. Use fragments, arpeggios within the scale, and short targeted phrases that resolve.

5. **Ignoring the resolution.** The beauty of altered dominant is in the resolution. If you play a brilliant altered line but land on a weak note on the I chord, the whole phrase falls flat. Always know where you are going.

## Key Takeaways

- The altered scale is the 7th mode of melodic minor (G altered = Ab melodic minor from G)
- Every note except the root creates tension that wants to resolve to a specific note in the target chord
- Learn it as a sound first by comparing altered and unaltered V7 chords in a II-V-I
- The tritone substitution relationship (G altered = Db Lydian dominant) gives you a practical shortcut
- Use short, targeted phrases (three-note licks) rather than scale runs
- Altered dominant only works on resolving V7 chords -- do not use it on static dominants
- Diatonic arpeggios within the altered scale give you powerful, focused lines

## What's Next

After mastering this, move on to:
- [10 - Melodic Minor](10-melodic-minor.md) -- Explore all the important melodic minor modes, not just the altered scale
- [08 - Bebop Vocabulary](08-bebop-vocabulary.md) -- Combine your altered lines with bebop enclosures and approach notes for richer vocabulary
prerequisites: Comfortable soloing over basic II-V-I progressions with diatonic material
Know your major scale and basic bebop vocabulary (see [08 - Bebop Vocabulary](08-bebop-vocabulary.md))
Can play shell voicings for maj7, min7, and dom7 chords
Familiar with at least one position of the melodic minor scale

content:
# Practice Guide: Melodic Minor Applications

## Overview

Melodic minor is a beautiful and essential part of the jazz sound. It is not just one scale -- it is a family of modes that each solve a specific harmonic problem. Jens Larsen's practical approach cuts through the theory and focuses on the three modes that matter most: melodic minor itself (on minor chords), Lydian dominant (mode 4, on dominant chords with a #11), and the altered scale (mode 7, on resolving dominant chords). If you know these three, you cover the vast majority of situations where melodic minor appears in jazz.

Jens uses Bernie's Tune as a teaching vehicle for melodic minor because it naturally contains all three of the most important modes in a single song. The key insight is that you should learn melodic minor **on actual music**, not as isolated scale patterns. When you practice modes in the context of a chord progression, you hear how each mode connects to the harmony and you internalize the sound much faster.

The arpeggios within melodic minor are where the real vocabulary lives. Unlike major scale modes, the diatonic arpeggios of melodic minor produce unusual and colorful sounds -- minor-major 7th chords, major 7th sharp 5, dominant 7th flat 5 -- that are the building blocks of modern jazz lines.

## Prerequisites

- Solid command of the major scale and its diatonic arpeggios
- Comfortable with II-V-I progressions in major keys
- Have worked through the [Altered Dominant guide](09-altered-dominant.md)
- Know shell voicings and basic voice leading

## Key Concepts

**Melodic minor on the tonic.** When you see a minor 6 or minor-major 7 chord (like Dm6 or Dm(maj7)), the melodic minor scale from the root is your first choice. This is the simplest application because you are playing the scale from its root. The diatonic arpeggios to focus on are: the min(maj7) from the root, the maj7#5 from the 3rd, and the half-diminished from the 6th (which is the same notes as a minor 6 chord).

**Lydian dominant (mode 4) on dominant chords.** When a dominant chord has a #11 (or is functioning as a tritone substitution), Lydian dominant is the right mode. For Bb7 with a #11, the scale is F melodic minor played from Bb. The key arpeggios are: the dom7 from the root and the half-diminished from the 3rd. But the really useful arpeggios are the ones that include the #11 -- the dom7b5 (replace the 5th with the #11) and the 7#5 from the 7th degree.

**Altered scale (mode 7) on resolving V7 chords.** Covered in depth in the [Altered Dominant guide](09-altered-dominant.md). For G7 altered, play Ab melodic minor from G. Use this when the dominant chord resolves and you want maximum tension.

**One melodic minor scale = three applications.** F melodic minor gives you: Fm(maj7) for F minor tonic chords, Bb Lydian dominant for Bb7#11, and E altered for E7alt. Learning one fingering pattern in one position unlocks vocabulary for three different harmonic situations.

## Recommended Video Sequence

Watch these Jens Larsen videos in order:

1. **The Most Important Melodic Minor Modes In One Song** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=RBpagEeInfY -- Bernie's Tune as a vehicle for learning all three essential modes: tonic minor, Lydian dominant, and altered, with specific arpeggio applications for each
2. **Altered Scale - Make Sure You Learn This First!** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=EfV5QZD5SzA -- Deep dive into mode 7 (altered), with three rules for making it sound right
3. **This is Why Melodic Minor Is Awesome** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=DWRAJukOgTI -- Broader overview of why melodic minor is so important in jazz
4. **The 3 Hidden Arpeggios in Melodic Minor** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=mI3O0OUXxMI -- Discovering the unusual arpeggios within melodic minor that give you the most interesting lines
5. **Melodic Minor - How to Make Beautiful Lines** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=Xku9YNWwox8 -- Turning melodic minor theory into actual solo lines
6. **Lydian Dominant - 10 Licks - What is the Best Arpeggio?** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=yhkUQaYqxNs -- Focused study of the Lydian dominant mode with practical lick ideas
7. **How To Make Minor Blues Sound Amazing with Melodic Minor** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=nIQRkCVTMNo -- Applying melodic minor concepts to minor blues, one of the most common jazz contexts

## Practice Routine

### Week 1-2: Foundation -- The Three Modes

**Daily practice: 20-30 minutes**

- **Exercise 1: D melodic minor -- tonic minor (10 min).** Play D melodic minor in one position (D-E-F-G-A-B-C#-D). Then play the diatonic arpeggios: Dm(maj7), Em7, Fmaj7#5, G7, A7, Bm7b5, C#m7b5. Focus on three key arpeggios for soloing over Dm6/Dm(maj7): the Dm(maj7), the Fmaj7#5, and the Bm7b5 (= Dm6). Compose short lines using each.

- **Exercise 2: F melodic minor as Bb Lydian dominant (10 min).** Play F melodic minor starting from Bb. The notes are: Bb-C-D-E-F-G-Ab-Bb. The E natural is the #11 -- that is the characteristic note. The most useful arpeggios are Bb7 and Dm7b5 (from the 3rd). But to really nail the Lydian dominant sound, try the Bb7b5 arpeggio (Bb-D-E-Ab) which includes the #11. Also try E7#5 (a pivot from the #11). Compose lines targeting the #11.

- **Exercise 3: Ab melodic minor as G altered (10 min).** Play Ab melodic minor from G to G. Review the resolution concepts from the Altered Dominant guide. Focus on short phrases that resolve from G7alt to Cmaj7.

### Week 3-4: Application -- Bernie's Tune and Standards

**Daily practice: 20-30 minutes**

- **Exercise 4: Bernie's Tune A section (15 min).** The A section of Bernie's Tune uses Dm(maj7) (D melodic minor) and Bb7 (F melodic minor as Lydian dominant). Practice soloing through the A section, switching between these two melodic minor applications. Use diatonic arpeggios as your primary material.

- **Exercise 5: Connect modes across chord changes (10 min).** On a II-V-I in minor (e.g., Em7b5 - A7alt - Dm), use A altered (Bb melodic minor from A) on the V chord and D melodic minor on the I chord. Practice the transition point -- where your altered line resolves into the minor tonic.

- **Exercise 6: Lydian dominant on tritone subs (5 min).** Take a II-V-I in C major: Dm7-G7-Cmaj7. Replace G7 with Db7 (tritone sub). Play Db Lydian dominant (Ab melodic minor from Db). Notice that this is the same notes as G altered -- just organized differently.

### Week 5-6: Integration -- Multiple Contexts

**Daily practice: 20-30 minutes**

- Apply melodic minor modes to Solar, which has minor tonic chords, dominant chords, and II-V-I progressions in both major and minor. This is a perfect vehicle.
- Practice minor blues using D melodic minor on the tonic, G Lydian dominant on the IV chord, and A altered on the V chord.
- Transcribe a short passage from a recording that uses melodic minor (e.g., any Pat Metheny ballad, or a Wes Montgomery minor tune). Identify which mode is being used and why.

## Common Mistakes to Avoid

1. **Treating melodic minor as one scale instead of three tools.** Each mode solves a different problem. The tonic minor mode is stable and home-like. Lydian dominant is colorful but grounded. Altered is tense and needs resolution. Do not mix up their applications.

2. **Ignoring the characteristic tones.** For Lydian dominant, it is the #11. For altered, it is the b9 and b13. For tonic minor, it is the major 7th over a minor chord. If your lines do not include these notes, you are not really capturing the sound of the mode.

3. **Using basic diatonic arpeggios without modification.** Jens points out that the standard Bb7 and Dm7b5 arpeggios over Lydian dominant sound great but do not really nail the Lydian dominant sound because they lack the #11. You need to explore modified arpeggios like dom7b5 and 7#5 to capture the character.

4. **Practicing modes in isolation from chord progressions.** A mode only makes sense in the context of a chord. Always practice with a chord progression, even if it is just a II-V-I backing track.

5. **Trying to learn all seven modes at once.** Jens is clear: three modes cover the vast majority of what you need. Master these before exploring the more exotic modes.

## Key Takeaways

- Melodic minor has three essential modes: tonic minor (mode 1), Lydian dominant (mode 4), and altered (mode 7)
- One melodic minor fingering gives you vocabulary for three different harmonic situations
- The diatonic arpeggios of melodic minor are where the real vocabulary lives -- learn the unusual ones (maj7#5, dom7b5, 7#5)
- Bernie's Tune is an ideal practice vehicle because it contains all three important modes
- The characteristic tones (#11 for Lydian dominant, b9/b13 for altered, major 7 for tonic minor) must be present in your lines
- Always learn modes on actual songs, not as isolated patterns

## What's Next

After mastering this, move on to:
- [11 - Rhythm Changes](11-rhythm-changes.md) -- Apply your melodic minor vocabulary to a demanding chord progression
- [09 - Altered Dominant](09-altered-dominant.md) -- Go deeper on the altered scale specifically if you need more work there

If you want to play bebop, you must be able to navigate Rhythm Changes fluently.

The progression is deceptively simple on the surface -- the A section is essentially a I-VI-II-V turnaround repeated, and the bridge is a series of dominant 7th chords cycling through the circle of fourths. But the real challenge is playing over it at tempo with interesting, flowing lines. This is where all your bebop vocabulary, arpeggio skills, and harmonic knowledge come together.

This is practical, efficient, and it swings.
content:
# Practice Guide: Rhythm Changes

## Overview

Rhythm Changes is one of the most important chord progressions in jazz, second only to the blues. Based on George Gershwin's "I Got Rhythm," its 32-bar AABA form has been the basis for countless jazz compositions -- from Charlie Parker's "Anthropology" and "Moose the Mooche" to Sonny Rollins's "Oleo." If you want to play bebop, you must be able to navigate Rhythm Changes fluently.

The progression is deceptively simple on the surface -- the A section is essentially a I-VI-II-V turnaround repeated, and the bridge is a series of dominant 7th chords cycling through the circle of fourths. But the real challenge is playing over it at tempo with interesting, flowing lines. This is where all your bebop vocabulary, arpeggio skills, and harmonic knowledge come together.

Jens Larsen's approach, heavily influenced by Joe Pass and Charlie Parker, emphasizes simplification. Joe Pass's advice to "forget the two" chord and think of II-V as just V applies perfectly here. Parker's solos on Rhythm Changes reveal that he often treats large sections of the A part as simply Bb major, reserving specific harmonic targeting for the moments where it matters most. This is practical, efficient, and it swings.

## Prerequisites

- Strong command of bebop vocabulary (see [08 - Bebop Vocabulary](08-bebop-vocabulary.md))
- Can solo over II-V-I progressions in multiple keys
- Comfortable with altered dominant concepts (see [09 - Altered Dominant](09-altered-dominant.md))
- Know the diatonic arpeggios in Bb major and the keys of the bridge (D, G, C, F)

## Key Concepts

**The AABA form.** Rhythm Changes is 32 bars: 8 bars of A, 8 bars of A (repeat), 8 bars of B (bridge), 8 bars of A (last A). The A sections are in Bb major. The bridge moves through dominant chords: D7 (2 bars), G7 (2 bars), C7 (2 bars), F7 (2 bars) -- cycling through the circle of fourths back to the key.

**The A section changes.** The basic changes are:
| Bb Maj7 | G7 | Cm7 | F7 | Dm7 | G7 | Cm7 | F7 |
Many players simplify this. Following Joe Pass's principle, you can reduce the II-V pairs: think G7 instead of Cm7-G7, and F7 instead of Cm7-F7. Or even simpler: the entire A section can be treated as **Bb major** with strategically placed chord tones at key moments. Parker does this constantly.

**Joe Pass's chord reduction principle.** As Jens teaches, in a II-V progression, the II chord is "really just sort of a fancy suspension of the V chord." The notes of Dm7 and G7 come from the same scale. So thinking G7 instead of Dm7-G7 works perfectly. For Rhythm Changes, this cuts your chord changes in half and lets you focus on making melodic lines rather than frantically chasing chord symbols.

**The bridge strategy.** The bridge is four dominant 7th chords, each lasting two bars: D7-G7-C7-F7. Each is a V chord of the next. This is where altered dominants shine -- you can use the altered scale on each chord to create tension that resolves beautifully to the next dominant. Or keep it simple and use Mixolydian on each.

**Substitutions for the A section.** Advanced players use chord substitutions on the A section: tritone subs, Coltrane changes, diminished passing chords, or chromatic II-V patterns. But the foundation must be solid first. As Jens advises in his work on Parker's strategies, "patterns are always better than licks" -- learn moveable patterns that work through the progression rather than memorizing licks for each bar.

## Recommended Video Sequence

Watch these Jens Larsen videos in order:

1. **Joe Pass on Rhythm Changes - 3 Solid Bebop Strategies** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=u6WX2gWmCb0 -- Joe Pass's practical approach to Rhythm Changes: chord reduction, bebop strategies, and how to make it swing
2. **Rhythm Changes - part 1** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=IT-K4Y9uxnw -- Comprehensive introduction to the form, the changes, and basic soloing approaches
3. **Rhythm Changes - part 2** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=5GdEf5lq_xs -- Advanced substitutions and more sophisticated soloing strategies
4. **3 types of Rhythm Changes licks you NEED to know** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=TFF415jbPC0 -- Essential vocabulary patterns specifically designed for Rhythm Changes
5. **Rhythm Changes Chords - Hidden in the Easy Chords** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=Ov6fYJHwULo -- Chord voicings and comping strategies for the form
6. **Why Patterns Are Always Better Than Licks - Parker's Strategy** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=wP-wpC0axP0 -- Parker's approach to using moveable patterns rather than fixed licks, directly applicable to Rhythm Changes
7. **10 Classic Charlie Parker Licks That Will Make You A Better Player** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=L_fhwdcXzr4 -- Parker vocabulary that works perfectly over Rhythm Changes

## Practice Routine

### Week 1-2: Foundation -- The Form and Basic Strategies

**Daily practice: 20-30 minutes**

- **Exercise 1: Learn the form cold (5 min).** Play the basic Rhythm Changes chords in Bb with shell voicings: |BbMaj7 G7|Cm7 F7|Dm7 G7|Cm7 F7| for the A section. Play through the full 32 bars (AABA) until the form is automatic. Use the Charleston rhythm.

- **Exercise 2: A section -- arpeggios only (10 min).** Solo through the A section using only one-octave arpeggios of each chord. Dm7 arpeggio, G7 arpeggio, Cm7 arpeggio, F7 arpeggio, etc. Start rubato, then add a metronome at 120 BPM. Focus on voice-leading: find arpeggio notes that connect smoothly from one chord to the next.

- **Exercise 3: A section -- Bb major simplification (10 min).** Now solo through the A section using only the Bb major scale. Target the 3rd and 5th of each chord as arrival notes at the chord change. This is Parker's basic approach -- the scale handles the overall flow, and strategic chord tones create the sense of harmony. Notice how much easier this is than tracking every chord.

- **Exercise 4: Bridge -- dominant arpeggios (5 min).** On the bridge (D7-G7-C7-F7), play the arpeggio of each dominant chord. Two bars per chord gives you plenty of space. Target the 3rd of each chord on beat 1 of the new chord.

### Week 3-4: Application -- Vocabulary and Flow

**Daily practice: 20-30 minutes**

- **Exercise 5: Bebop patterns through the A section (10 min).** Take one bebop pattern -- for example, the ascending triad enclosure from the Bebop Vocabulary guide -- and move it through each chord of the A section. The pattern stays the same; only the chord tones change. This is exactly what Parker does.

- **Exercise 6: Altered bridge (10 min).** On the bridge, use the altered scale on each dominant: D altered (Eb melodic minor from D), G altered (Ab melodic minor from G), C altered (Db melodic minor from C), F altered (Gb melodic minor from F). Focus on resolution -- each altered line should resolve clearly to the next dominant chord.

- **Exercise 7: Full chorus -- mix strategies (10 min).** Solo through a full 32-bar chorus, mixing your approaches. Maybe Bb major simplification on the first A, arpeggios on the second A, altered dominants on the bridge, and bebop patterns on the last A. Use a Rhythm Changes backing track at 160-180 BPM.

### Week 5-6: Integration -- Performance Level

**Daily practice: 20-30 minutes**

- Practice at performance tempos (180-220 BPM). Rhythm Changes is traditionally played fast. At these tempos, the simplified approach (treating the A section as Bb major with target notes) becomes essential.
- Transcribe one chorus of a Parker solo on Anthropology or Moose the Mooche. Identify where he uses arpeggios, where he uses scale-based lines, where he targets chord changes, and where he ignores them.
- Practice the chord reduction concept: solo over the A section thinking only G7 and F7 (eliminating the II chords). Then add back the II chords with approach notes when you want more harmonic detail.
- Comp Rhythm Changes for yourself using shell voicings with the Charleston rhythm while a backing track plays. This builds your form awareness from a different angle.

## Common Mistakes to Avoid

1. **Trying to hit every chord change.** With two chords per bar at a fast tempo, frantically arpeggiating each chord sounds like an exercise, not music. Simplify. Think bigger phrases that flow across multiple bars.

2. **Treating the A section and the bridge identically.** The A section is diatonic and home-like. The bridge is a sequence of dominant chords cycling away from the key. Your approach should reflect this -- simpler on the A, more harmonically specific on the bridge.

3. **Never playing at tempo.** Rhythm Changes is meant to be played fast. If you only practice at 120 BPM, you will never develop the ability to simplify and let the lines flow at 200+ BPM. Push the tempo regularly.

4. **Ignoring the turnaround.** The last two bars of each A section (Cm7-F7 or variations) are the turnaround that brings you back. This is where a strong bebop phrase or an altered dominant lick can really shine.

5. **Only soloing, never comping.** Learning to comp Rhythm Changes gives you a deeper understanding of the form and helps you hear the harmonic rhythm from the inside. Alternate between soloing and comping in your practice.

## Key Takeaways

- Rhythm Changes is a 32-bar AABA form in Bb major -- one of the most important progressions in jazz
- Simplify the A section: Joe Pass says "forget the two" chord -- think of II-V as just V
- The A section can often be treated as Bb major with strategically placed chord tones
- The bridge (D7-G7-C7-F7) is where altered dominants and more specific harmonic material shine
- Patterns that move through the progression are more valuable than licks for individual chords
- Practice at performance tempo (180+ BPM) regularly -- this forces useful simplification
- Learn bebop heads on the form (Anthropology, Oleo) to internalize the rhythmic feel

## What's Next

After mastering this, move on to:

content:
# Practice Guide: Walking Bass and Chords

## Overview

Walking bass with chord stabs is one of the most rewarding techniques in jazz guitar. It turns you into a complete rhythm section -- bass, chords, and groove all at once. Jens Larsen was completely sold on this technique after hearing Joe Pass play walking bass and chords behind Ella Fitzgerald, and he spent a lot of time practicing and putting it to use on gigs, especially in duo settings.

The approach is straightforward: construct a bass line on the lowest two strings (E and A), then add chord stabs from shell voicings or drop voicings on the remaining strings. The bass line provides the harmonic foundation and forward motion, while the chord stabs add color and rhythm. Jens teaches this in levels, starting with the simplest bass lines and gradually adding more sophisticated chord insertions and chromatic passing chords.

The priority is always the bass line. As Jens emphasizes in his walking bass lessons, "the priority is really to keep the bass line moving." If you start thinking too much about the chords, you will mess up the time. The bass line should be clear, logical, and rhythmically solid. The chords are added around it -- often on offbeats like the "and" of beat 1 -- leaving your fingers free to continue the bass line.

## Prerequisites

- Solid knowledge of shell voicings on the middle string set (D-G-B strings)
- Can play basic chord progressions with voice leading
- Comfortable with chord tones and scale tones for common jazz chords
- Familiar with a few jazz standards (blues in Bb, All The Things You Are, Autumn Leaves)

## Key Concepts

**Bass line construction.** The bass line uses only the 6th and 5th strings. For each chord, you want the root on beat 1. On beat 3 (for chords lasting one bar), place another chord tone -- typically the 5th or 3rd. Fill in beats 2 and 4 with passing tones (scale tones or chromatic approach notes) that logically connect one root to the next. Think of it as: root - passing tone - chord tone - approach note to next root.

**Approach notes.** The most important connecting device is the approach note on beat 4, leading into the root of the next chord on beat 1. A half step below (chromatic approach from below) or a half step above (chromatic from above) works most of the time. You can also approach from a whole step (diatonic approach). The chromatic approach creates the strongest pull.

**Adding chords.** Once you have a walking bass line, add shell voicings (3rd and 7th on the middle strings) at chord changes. The simplest approach is to play the chord right when the harmony changes -- usually on beat 1 alongside the bass note. But Jens often places the chord stab on the "and" of beat 1, which adds syncopation and frees the fingers to continue the bass line.

**Delayed chord placement.** At higher tempos, Jens recommends placing the chord on an offbeat rather than right on beat 1. This keeps the bass line flowing and prevents you from getting locked down. "If you start thinking too much about the chords then you're gonna mess up the time."

**Chromatic passing chords.** Once the basic technique is comfortable, you can add chromatic approach chords -- sliding a voicing up or down a half step into the target chord. This adds movement and sophistication without requiring any new chord shapes.

**Sustained chords.** A useful variation is to let a chord sustain while the bass line moves underneath it. This creates a more orchestral, layered sound and is especially effective when the bass line walks through an arpeggio pattern.

## Recommended Video Sequence

Watch these Jens Larsen videos in order:

1. **5 Levels of Walking Bass And Chords - Great Comping Approach** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=cNblxzAk8t0 -- The complete progressive approach: constructing a bass line, adding shell voicings, chromatic passing chords, and advanced variations
2. **Walking Bass and Chords - Bb Blues - Jazz Guitar Lesson** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=z6U_b6KmJHI -- Detailed walkthrough of walking bass on a Bb blues, with analysis of bass line choices, chord voicings, and chromatic embellishments
3. **Walking Bass Jazz Guitar Lesson on All The Things You Are** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=wD_CcsyN-oI -- A full chorus example at medium-up tempo (170-175 BPM), showing how to adapt the technique to a real standard with practical tips for faster tempos
4. **Walking bass and chords guitar lesson - Solar** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=tERCY0QQcvQ -- Another standard application with a different harmonic context
5. **Walking Bass and Chords - part 1** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=fe6OiEm5FHA -- Foundational concepts and first exercises
6. **Walking Bass and Chords - part 2** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=MR3VmIS7cMI -- Expanding the technique with more chord types and passing chord ideas
7. **Autumn Leaves - Beautiful Jazz Chords (5 Levels)** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=Msd4tPlvm5g -- Includes walking bass as one of the levels on Autumn Leaves, showing the bossa nova groove split and walking bass approach

## Practice Routine

### Week 1-2: Foundation -- Bass Lines Without Chords

**Daily practice: 20-30 minutes**

- **Exercise 1: Compose a bass line on a II-V-I in C (10 min).** Use Cmaj7-Em7-A7-Dm7-G7-Cmaj7 (Jens's progression from his walking bass lesson). Place root on beat 1, a chord tone on beat 3 (5th or 3rd), and fill beats 2 and 4 with scale tones or chromatic passing tones. Write it out first, then play with a metronome at 100 BPM.

  Example skeleton:
  - Cmaj7: C (root) - E (3rd) - G (5th) - approach to E
  - Em7: E - ... - B (5th) - approach to A

  Use only the 6th and 5th strings. Keep it simple -- as Jens says, "most bass players seem to manage."

- **Exercise 2: Bass line on a Bb blues (10 min).** Write out a walking bass line for a 12-bar blues in Bb. Root on beat 1 of each chord change. Use the triad and chromatic approach notes. The Bb7 bar can walk up the Bb major triad (Bb-D-F) with a leading note to Eb. Pay attention to the approach note on beat 4 of each bar -- this is what creates the forward motion.

- **Exercise 3: Improvise bass lines (10 min).** Once you have a composed bass line, start improvising variations. Keep the roots on beat 1, but change the inner beats. Practice with a metronome, then with a backing track. Focus on smooth, logical voice leading from one bar to the next.

### Week 3-4: Application -- Adding Chords

**Daily practice: 20-30 minutes**

- **Exercise 4: Add shell voicings at chord changes (10 min).** Take your composed bass line from Exercise 1. At each chord change, add the shell voicing (3rd and 7th on the D and G strings) simultaneously with the bass note on beat 1. For Cmaj7: bass C on the 5th string + shell voicing (E and B) on the D and G strings. Practice slowly -- the coordination takes time.

- **Exercise 5: Move chords to offbeats (10 min).** Instead of placing the chord on beat 1, try placing it on the "and" of 1. Play the bass note on beat 1, then add the chord stab on the upbeat. This is closer to how Jens actually plays it in his examples -- the bass line keeps moving, and the chord is inserted where it fits naturally. This also adds syncopation to the groove.

- **Exercise 6: Chromatic passing chords (10 min).** Take a basic chord change like Bb7 to Eb7. Before arriving at the Eb7 shell voicing, approach it from a half step below (D7 voicing sliding up to Eb7) or a half step above (E7 sliding down). Jens describes this in the Bb blues lesson: "I have a leading note... which is just to create a little bit of variation in the bass line." Apply this at one or two points in your 12-bar blues arrangement.

### Week 5-6: Integration -- Full Tunes

**Daily practice: 20-30 minutes**

- Practice walking bass and chords on a complete Bb blues at 120-140 BPM. Mix in chromatic passing chords, delayed chord placement, and occasionally sustained chords with moving bass.
- Learn the first 8 bars of All The Things You Are with walking bass and chords. At the medium-up tempo Jens demonstrates (170-175 BPM), simplify the chord insertions. Place chords on the "and" of 1 and keep the bass line as the priority.
- Practice Autumn Leaves, starting with the simpler bossa nova split (bass note on beats 1 and 3, chord on beats 2 and 4) before graduating to full walking bass.
- Record yourself and listen back. The bass line should sound even and clear. The chords should add color without disrupting the groove.

## Common Mistakes to Avoid

1. **Prioritizing chords over the bass line.** The bass line is the foundation. If you sacrifice the bass line to grab a chord, the time will suffer. As Jens says, "the priority is really to keep the bass line moving."

2. **Using bass notes all over the neck.** Keep bass notes on the 6th and 5th strings only. This is where you naturally place roots for shell voicings and drop voicings, and it leaves the higher strings available for chord stabs.

3. **Always placing chords on beat 1.** This sounds stiff and takes away from the walking bass feel. Experiment with offbeat chord placement (the "and" of 1 or beat 2) to create syncopation and groove.

4. **Making the bass line too complicated.** A walking bass line should primarily use chord tones and stepwise motion. Triads and chromatic approach notes handle 90% of what you need. Do not try to play virtuosic bass solos.

5. **Ignoring the approach note to the next bar.** Beat 4 is the most important beat in a walking bass line. It should lead clearly into the root of the next chord, typically by half step. This is what creates the sense of forward motion.

6. **Playing at too fast a tempo too soon.** Walking bass and chords requires coordination between multiple layers. Start at 100 BPM and gradually increase. Jens notes that at higher tempos you need to make practical choices to keep things simple.

## Key Takeaways

- Walking bass uses only the 6th and 5th strings; chords go on the middle and upper strings
- Root on beat 1, chord tone on beat 3, approach note on beat 4 -- this is the basic bass line formula
- Add shell voicings at chord changes, preferably on offbeats for a more natural groove
- The bass line is always the priority -- do not sacrifice time for chord insertions
- Chromatic passing chords (sliding up or down a half step into the target voicing) add color without new shapes
- Start with composed bass lines, then gradually improvise
- At faster tempos, simplify the chord placement and keep the bass moving

## What's Next

After mastering this, move on to:
- [13 - Chord Melody](13-chord-melody.md) -- Another solo guitar technique that builds on your voicing knowledge
- [14 - Comping Patterns](14-comping-patterns.md) -- Develop your rhythmic vocabulary for chord accompaniment

Can play basic chord progressions with voice leading

content:
# Practice Guide: Chord Melody

## Overview

Chord melody is one of the most fun things to play on jazz guitar -- you are playing the melody, harmony, and rhythm all at once, like an entire orchestra on one instrument. But most students approach it completely wrong. They start by trying to sight-read complicated arrangements written by others, which is a dead end. As Jens Larsen puts it, "playing chord melody should not be the same as learning a piece of classical music."

Jens's method is simple and practical: start with the melody, add chords under it using shapes you already know (shell voicings), and gradually add color, rhythm, and passing chords. Despite the name "chord melody," the melody is actually more important than the chords. If the melody disappears because you are too busy thinking about chord shapes, the arrangement fails. Know the melody so well that you can move it around the neck, and the chords will fall into place.

The foundation of Jens's approach is three-note shell voicings. These split the strings into three sets: bass note on the 5th or 6th string, chord tones (3rd and 7th) on the middle strings (D-G), and the melody on the top two strings (B-E). This "two-note" method -- shell voicing plus melody note -- gives you a complete harmonic picture with just 3-4 notes, leaving your fretting hand free to move the melody without wrestling with complex grips.

## Prerequisites

- Know shell voicings for all common chord types (maj7, min7, dom7, m7b5) with roots on both the 5th and 6th strings
- Can play through a jazz standard with chords in time
- Familiar with the concept of II-V-I progressions
- Know the melodies of a few standards (Autumn Leaves, Satin Doll, or similar)

## Key Concepts

**Melody first, always.** Before touching a single chord, learn the melody on the top two strings (B and E strings). If you can play the melody there, you have room underneath for chords. As Jens says, "Really knowing the melody will make everything 100 times easier."

**Shell voicings as the foundation.** A shell voicing is root + 3rd + 7th. On guitar, the root sits on the 5th or 6th string, and the 3rd and 7th sit on the middle string set (D and G strings). This leaves the B and E strings completely free for melody. The chord is only three notes, easy to grab, and it covers the essential harmonic information.

**The "two-five" shortcut.** Jazz harmony is full of II-V progressions. Once you know one way to play a II-V with shell voicings, you can play most of any standard. Satin Doll, for example, is "basically a II-V festival" as Jens says. Billy Strayhorn uses the progression as a repeating motif that shifts around the song.

**Adding melody to chords.** Instead of learning hundreds of chord inversions, take your shell voicing and change or add the top note to create the melody. Think of it as the same chord with a different melody note on top. For a Bb7 shell voicing, you might have D (3rd) as the top note, or F (5th), or G (13th), or Ab (7th) -- each creates a different "chord" but you are really just moving one note.

**Chromatic passing chords.** Adding chromatic movement between chords is easier than it sounds. Take the voicing you want to arrive at and approach it from a half step above or below. Jens demonstrates this on Autumn Leaves: "you can add a passing chord to the Ebmaj7 and this turns out to be an E7 which is of course a tritone substitute but notice that it's really just two notes that move down a fret."

**The process for building an arrangement:**
1. Learn the melody on the top two strings
2. Play through the chords separately (shell voicings)
3. Combine: place a chord on beat 1 of each bar with the melody on top
4. Add rhythm between melody phrases (chord responses)
5. Add chromatic passing chords and inner voice movement
6. Polish and personalize

## Recommended Video Sequence

Watch these Jens Larsen videos in order:

1. **Stop Making Chord Melody Hard -- Start With This** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=d1Ro4G6Wc9o -- Step-by-step method using Satin Doll, the shell voicing + melody approach, and the II-V shortcut
2. **Master Chord Melody Without Learning 10,000 Chord Shapes** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=ru4ZTG6nTSk -- The correct process: melody first, chords second, with All The Things You Are as the teaching vehicle
3. **Autumn Leaves - Beautiful Chord Melody For Jazz Beginners** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=3UvUr3FOuVk -- Progressive Autumn Leaves arrangement from simple to embellished, with chromatic passing chords explained
4. **Chord Melody Made Simple -- Start Playing Today** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=f_HOaeaQSGQ -- Another accessible entry point with practical tips for getting started immediately
5. **Chord Melody - Get These 3 Things Right For That Beautiful Sound** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=1p2dpe0OLzo -- Three essential elements that make chord melody sound professional
6. **Chord Solos In Easy Mode (Play It Instantly!)** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=hsDYz80400E -- How to solo with chords using rootless voicings and simple melody options, with rhythm as the key ingredient
7. **Autumn Leaves - How To Use Drop 2 For An Easy Chord Melody** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=zq3IKsgWmCk -- Expanding beyond shell voicings into drop 2 voicings for a fuller sound
8. **Chord Melody - 5 Beautiful Methods You Want To Know** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=MEg1hsra6GU -- Five different techniques for creating beautiful chord melody arrangements

## Practice Routine

### Week 1-2: Foundation -- Melody and Basic Chords

**Daily practice: 20-30 minutes**

- **Exercise 1: Learn the melody on the top two strings (10 min).** Choose a standard (Satin Doll or Autumn Leaves are ideal). Play the entire melody using only the B and E strings. Stay in one area of the neck if possible. For Autumn Leaves in G minor, the melodic motif moves down the neck, which makes chord melody especially natural. Know the melody cold before adding any chords.

- **Exercise 2: Play the shell voicing progression (10 min).** Play through the same tune using only shell voicings. Two versions: roots on the 5th string, then roots on the 6th string. Use the Charleston rhythm (beat 1, then the "and" of 2). Notice how the voicings flow from one to the next with minimal hand movement -- that is the beauty of shell voicings.

- **Exercise 3: Combine melody and chords (10 min).** Place a shell voicing on beat 1 of each bar. Add the melody note on the top string, adjusting fingering as needed. For the first attempt, keep it simple: chord on beat 1, melody notes between the chords. Do not try to harmonize every melody note -- just catch the main ones.

### Week 3-4: Application -- Rhythm and Color

**Daily practice: 20-30 minutes**

- **Exercise 4: Add rhythmic chord responses (10 min).** Between melody phrases, insert chord stabs. Autumn Leaves has a motivic structure that makes this easy: the melody states a phrase, and you respond with a chord rhythm. This call-and-response approach is more musical than trying to put a chord under every melody note.

- **Exercise 5: Melody note variations on chords (10 min).** For each chord in the progression, find 2-3 melody note options on the top strings. For a Bb7 shell voicing, you might use D, F, G, or Ab as top notes. Practice switching between these options to create melodic interest within the chord melody. As Jens teaches, "instead of jumping between complicated grips, you just change or add a new top note."

- **Exercise 6: Chromatic passing chords (10 min).** Add one or two chromatic approach chords to your arrangement. The simplest approach: take the target chord voicing and approach it from one fret above or below. On Autumn Leaves, try approaching the Ebmaj7 from E7 (one fret above), or approaching the D7 from Eb7 (one fret above, then slide down). Listen to how these chromatic movements add flow and sophistication.

### Week 5-6: Integration -- Complete Arrangements

**Daily practice: 20-30 minutes**

- Play through your complete arrangement of one standard (Autumn Leaves or Satin Doll) at a medium tempo with a metronome. Record yourself. Listen for: Is the melody audible and clear? Do the chords support or obscure the melody? Is there rhythmic variety?
- Create a second arrangement using a different starting position on the neck. If your first arrangement uses roots on the 5th string, try one with roots on the 6th string. This gives you options for different registers and different melody voicings.
- Explore drop 2 voicings as an upgrade from shell voicings. Drop 2 voicings have four notes and a fuller sound. Apply them to the same standards you have been working on.
- Try a slow ballad (Misty, Body and Soul) where you have more time for inner voice movement and passing chords. Slower tempos allow for more harmonic detail.

## Common Mistakes to Avoid

1. **Starting with someone else's complex arrangement.** As Jens warns, this is "the worst thing you can do." You end up struggling to sight-read difficult fingerings without understanding why the voicings were chosen. Build your own arrangement from the ground up.

2. **Letting the melody disappear.** This is "a very common beginner problem." The melody is the song. Make sure it is always the loudest voice, on the highest string, and clearly articulated. If you cannot hear the melody, simplify the chords.

3. **Using too many chord shapes.** Shell voicings with melody notes on top cover an enormous range of harmonic situations. You do not need 10,000 chord shapes. Jens's method works with just a few flexible voicing types.

4. **Ignoring rhythm.** Chord melody is not just about chords and melody -- it is about groove. Add rhythmic variety: chord on beat 1, anticipations, offbeat stabs, rests between phrases. A boring rhythm makes even beautiful voicings sound lifeless.

5. **Trying to harmonize every single melody note.** Not every note needs a chord under it. Some melody notes can ring on their own. The call-and-response approach (melody phrase, then chord response) is often more musical than wall-to-wall harmonization.

6. **Choosing the wrong tempo for the arrangement.** As Jens explains, "the tempo you choose makes a big difference. At a slower tempo you have more space for things like passing chords and fills. But if you try that in a fast version it can sound messy and crowded." Match your harmonic density to the tempo.

## Key Takeaways

- Melody first, always -- learn it on the top two strings before touching chords
- Shell voicings (root + 3rd + 7th) are the ideal foundation, splitting the strings into bass/chord/melody
- Build your own arrangements rather than reading others' transcriptions
- The II-V shortcut covers most jazz harmony -- one shape, many songs
- Add color by changing the top note of a shell voicing, not by learning new chord shapes
- Chromatic passing chords are simple: approach the target voicing from a half step above or below
- Rhythm and call-and-response phrasing make the arrangement sound musical, not mechanical

## What's Next

After mastering this, move on to:
- [12 - Walking Bass and Chords](12-walking-bass-chords.md) -- Another solo guitar technique that combines bass line and chords
- [14 - Comping Patterns](14-comping-patterns.md) -- Develop rhythmic vocabulary that enhances your chord melody arrangements

Can play through a jazz standard with chords in time
Familiar with the concept of II-V-I progressions
Know the melodies of a few standards (Autumn Leaves, Satin Doll, or similar)

title: Comping Patterns
overview:
Even if you are like Guitar George and know all the chords, that will not get you anywhere if you do not have rhythms to use while playing behind a soloist. Comping -- short for accompanying -- is arguably the skill you will use most as a jazz guitarist. You comp far more than you solo, and if you can comp well, you can work. As Jens Larsen emphasizes, "rhythm is the most important part of jazz and you want to get this right."

Jens's approach to comping focuses on building a vocabulary of short rhythmic patterns and learning to combine them into musical phrases. He teaches this with shell voicings, which are simple three-note chords that clearly convey the harmony while being easy enough to play so you can focus on rhythm rather than wrestling with complex grips. The philosophy is practical: learn a few solid rhythms, then practice combining them like words into sentences, and sentences into stories.

A crucial insight from Jens's conversation with pianist Aimee Nolte is that comping is a conversation. In a band setting, you need to support the soloist, react to what is happening, and leave space. Overplaying is the most common comping mistake. The rhythms you practice should be tools for musical dialogue, not patterns you repeat mindlessly.
