# Practice Guide: Comping Patterns

## Overview

Even if you are like Guitar George and know all the chords, that will not get you anywhere if you do not have rhythms to use while playing behind a soloist. Comping -- short for accompanying -- is arguably the skill you will use most as a jazz guitarist. You comp far more than you solo, and if you can comp well, you can work. As Jens Larsen emphasizes, "rhythm is the most important part of jazz and you want to get this right."

Jens's approach to comping focuses on building a vocabulary of short rhythmic patterns and learning to combine them into musical phrases. He teaches this with shell voicings, which are simple three-note chords that clearly convey the harmony while being easy enough to play so you can focus on rhythm rather than wrestling with complex grips. The philosophy is practical: learn a few solid rhythms, then practice combining them like words into sentences, and sentences into stories.

A crucial insight from Jens's conversation with pianist Aimee Nolte is that comping is a conversation. In a band setting, you need to support the soloist, react to what is happening, and leave space. Overplaying is the most common comping mistake. The rhythms you practice should be tools for musical dialogue, not patterns you repeat mindlessly.

## Prerequisites

- Know shell voicings for all basic chord types (maj7, min7, dom7, m7b5) with roots on 5th and 6th strings
- Can play through at least one jazz standard with chords in time
- Have a basic sense of swing feel (listening to jazz recordings regularly)
- Familiar with reading simple rhythm notation

## Key Concepts

**The Charleston rhythm is the foundation.** This is a two-note rhythm: one on beat 1, one on the "and" of 2. It has a clear downbeat and syncopation. As Jens says, "if you only know one comping rhythm it should be that." It is "quite magical" -- it sounds full, has enough space for a soloist, and serves as a great exercise in anticipating chords because the second chord hit comes on the "and" of 2.

**The shifted Charleston.** Move the Charleston rhythm an eighth note forward: the first note lands on the "and" of 1, the second on beat 3. This adds much more energy and forward push. Jens uses this to create intros and to build excitement.

**The inverted Charleston.** Mirror the Charleston in the bar: place a note on the "and" of 3, driving toward beat 1 of the next bar. This creates a strong resolution effect, almost like a stop-chorus feel on a blues. Combined with other rhythms, it adds variety and momentum.

**Rhythm is melody.** This is Jens's most important point about comping. Short rhythmic patterns are like words. To say something, you need to put the words together into a sentence. Already with just the Charleston and the shifted Charleston, you can create four-bar comping phrases that tell a story. The goal is not to play the same one-bar pattern over and over -- it is to compose rhythmic phrases.

**Shell voicing split.** Shell voicings naturally divide into bass note (6th or 5th string) and chord (middle strings). This split gives you two "instruments" to play with. The bass note anchors the harmony, and the chord part can be played on offbeats, creating a groove almost like a rhythm section within yourself. This is especially powerful for bossa nova comping and for adding more rhythmic interest.

**Anticipation.** One of the most characteristic jazz comping devices is anticipating the chord change -- playing the new chord slightly before beat 1 (on the "and" of 4 of the previous bar). This is difficult to get used to but essential for the jazz feel. As Jens's comping rhythms lesson explains, "anticipating the harmony is a huge part of jazz."

**Quarter note rhythms.** While offbeats and syncopation get most of the attention, quarter note rhythms (playing on beats 2 and 4, or beats 1 and 3) are a legitimate and important part of the comping vocabulary. They ground the groove and provide contrast to the syncopated patterns.

**Groups of three.** Three eighth notes create a cross-rhythm feel against the 4/4 meter. This is "extremely common in jazz comping rhythms" and creates tension that resolves when you return to a normal pattern.

## Recommended Video Sequence

Watch these Jens Larsen videos in order:

1. **The 7 Comping Rhythms That Really Matter** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=-450hfJp0I4 -- Seven essential rhythms with demonstrations on standards, plus how to think of rhythmic patterns as words in a sentence
2. **Comping Rhythms - 10 Examples You Need To Know** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=zmAS3fcLWbc -- Ten rhythm patterns played three times each at medium tempo, including Charleston, shifted Charleston, anticipation, groups of three, and motivic development
3. **Jazz Chords: The 5 Rhythms You Want To Know** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=EllY2d2weKY -- Developing rhythms from the Charleston, adding extra notes, shifting rhythms in the bar, and adding chromatic passing chords
4. **Exactly How To Nail Jazz Blues Comping (It's Simple!)** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=C8cziTSa6pA -- Applied comping on a Bb jazz blues with shell voicings, the bass/chord split, rhythm, melody in chords, and a Joe Pass-style example
5. **Jazz Comping - A Difficult Conversation With Aimee Nolte** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=58YGhoUBo7w -- Piano-guitar comping etiquette, when to comp and when to lay out, and how to share the role in a band
6. **The 5 Skills That Make You Great At Comping** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=FG-nLEi9CDk -- Five key abilities beyond just knowing chords, including rhythm, listening, and reacting to the soloist
7. **From Basic Jazz Chords To Beautiful Comping** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=D7-CBARhX-M -- Taking basic shell voicings and transforming them into professional-sounding comping
8. **5 Comping Exercises for Jazz Rhythm on the Blues** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=6U10Q1T1w9c -- Specific exercises to develop your comping rhythm on a jazz blues

## Practice Routine

### Week 1-2: Foundation -- Core Rhythms

**Daily practice: 20-30 minutes**

- **Exercise 1: Charleston on a single chord (5 min).** Take a Cm7 shell voicing. Play the Charleston rhythm (beat 1, "and" of 2) for four bars. Then switch to an F7, then Bbmaj7. Repeat until the rhythm feels automatic. This is your baseline -- you should know this rhythm as well as you know how to play a barre chord.

- **Exercise 2: Charleston on a full tune (10 min).** Play through Autumn Leaves or Take The A Train using only the Charleston rhythm. Keep the chord changes accurate and in time. This is harder than it sounds because you have to anticipate each chord to play it on the "and" of 2. Use a metronome at 120-140 BPM.

- **Exercise 3: Shifted Charleston (5 min).** Practice the shifted version ("and" of 1, beat 3) on a single chord, then on a blues. Notice how this pushes the energy forward compared to the regular Charleston.

- **Exercise 4: Combine two rhythms (10 min).** Alternate between the Charleston and the shifted Charleston every two bars on a simple tune. This is where it starts to feel like musical conversation. The Charleston grounds it, the shifted Charleston pushes it forward.

### Week 3-4: Application -- More Rhythms and Blues Comping

**Daily practice: 20-30 minutes**

- **Exercise 5: Shell voicing split on a Bb blues (10 min).** Split your shell voicings into bass note and chord. For Bb7: play the bass Bb, then the chord (D-Ab) separately. Practice alternating bass and chord to create a groove. Add the bossa nova pattern (Jens demonstrates this on Autumn Leaves): bass on beats 1 and 3, chord on the "and" of 1, beat 2, and the "and" of 3. This groove is incredibly versatile.

- **Exercise 6: Add melody to your chords (10 min).** On a Bb7 shell voicing, practice changing the top note: D (3rd), F (5th), G (13th), Ab (7th). Move between these while keeping a rhythm going. As Jens teaches, "instead of jumping between complicated grips, you just change or add a new top note to the chord you're already playing." This is how your comping gets melody.

- **Exercise 7: Anticipation practice (10 min).** Play through a II-V-I with the new chord arriving on the "and" of 4 of the previous bar. Start slowly -- this feels wrong until you get used to it. Then apply to a full blues. This is one of the most difficult but important comping skills to develop.

### Week 5-6: Integration -- Full Comping

**Daily practice: 20-30 minutes**

- **Exercise 8: Four-bar rhythmic phrases.** Compose four-bar comping rhythms that tell a story: two bars of Charleston, one bar of shifted Charleston, one bar of the inverted pattern. Play these through a full standard. Vary the phrases on each repeat.

- **Exercise 9: Comp with a recording.** Put on a jazz recording (a trio or quartet without guitar is ideal). Comp along with the form. React to the soloist: play more when they rest, less when they are playing dense lines. This is where comping becomes a real skill, not just a pattern exercise.

- **Exercise 10: Add chromatic passing chords.** When changing from one chord to the next, approach the new voicing from a half step above or below. As Jens demonstrates on the blues: "all you're doing is that you're looking at the note you want to end on and then you're just taking that voicing and shifting it so that you can resolve back into the voicing you want to hit." This adds sophistication with no new chord shapes.

- **Exercise 11: Comp behind yourself.** Record a simple solo (or use a looper), then practice comping behind it. This develops your awareness of what comping sounds like from the soloist's perspective.

## Common Mistakes to Avoid

1. **Playing the same rhythm on every bar.** This is the equivalent of saying the same word over and over. Comping rhythms are words -- you need to combine them into sentences. Vary your patterns every 2-4 bars.

2. **Overplaying.** As Jens and Aimee Nolte discuss, the biggest comping mistake is not leaving space. When the soloist is playing busy lines, simplify your comping. When they rest, you can fill. Comping is a conversation, not a monologue.

3. **Ignoring dynamics.** Playing every chord at the same volume makes your comping flat and lifeless. Use dynamics to shape phrases: softer during the verse, stronger at climactic moments, accent the anticipations.

4. **Sustaining chords too long.** In jazz comping, even written quarter notes are usually played short. Long sustained chords sound heavy and un-jazzy. Keep chord stabs crisp and short unless you are specifically going for a pad-like effect.

5. **Only comping with complex voicings.** Shell voicings are "simple and easy to play three note versions of the seventh chords" that "really get the harmony across." You do not need big five- or six-note voicings to sound professional. The rhythm and voice-leading matter more than the chord complexity.

6. **Not listening to the bass player.** In a band with bass, do not double the root on every chord. Use rootless voicings (just the 3rd and 7th from the shell voicing) to stay out of the bass player's way. As Jens shows, the rootless versions of shell voicings are "much more powerful than you think."

7. **Comping over the piano player.** Jens's advice from the Aimee Nolte conversation: in a jam session with piano, "just be a horn player." Only comp when cued or when the pianist takes a break. Trying to elbow your way in never helps the music.

## Key Takeaways

- The Charleston rhythm is the single most important comping rhythm -- master it first
- Think of rhythms as words: learn individual patterns, then practice combining them into musical phrases
- Shell voicings split naturally into bass + chord, giving you two layers of rhythmic possibility
- Anticipation (playing the new chord on the "and" of 4) is essential for the jazz feel
- Add melody to your comping by changing the top note of your shell voicing, not by learning new shapes
- Leave space: comping is a conversation with the soloist, not a solo in itself
- Chromatic passing chords (approach from a half step above or below) add sophistication with no new shapes
- Keep chord stabs short and crisp -- sustained notes are the exception, not the rule

## What's Next

After mastering this, move on to:
- [12 - Walking Bass and Chords](12-walking-bass-chords.md) -- Take your comping to the next level with walking bass lines underneath
- [13 - Chord Melody](13-chord-melody.md) -- Apply your rhythmic vocabulary to chord melody arrangements

title: Quartal Harmony
overview:
Quartal harmony -- building chords and melodies from stacked perfect fourths instead of the traditional thirds -- is one of the most distinctive sounds in modern jazz. It's the sound you hear in McCoy Tyner's piano voicings, Wayne Shorter's compositions, and countless post-bop recordings. On the guitar, quartal voicings are surprisingly practical because the instrument is largely tuned in fourths, making these shapes feel natural under your fingers.

Jens Larsen approaches quartal harmony from multiple angles: as chord voicings for comping, as arpeggios for soloing, and as a way of thinking about melody that breaks free from the thirds-based patterns most guitarists default to. His method emphasizes learning quartal structures within the context of songs and chord types you already know, so you can immediately hear and use them rather than treating them as abstract theory.

The goal is not to replace traditional harmony but to add a modern color to your palette. Quartal voicings have a distinctive open, ambiguous quality -- they don't sound as clearly "major" or "minor" as tertian chords -- and this ambiguity is exactly what makes them so useful over modal vamps, minor chords, and dominant chords where you want a contemporary sound.
content:
# Practice Guide: Quartal Harmony

## Overview

Quartal harmony -- building chords and melodies from stacked perfect fourths instead of the traditional thirds -- is one of the most distinctive sounds in modern jazz. It's the sound you hear in McCoy Tyner's piano voicings, Wayne Shorter's compositions, and countless post-bop recordings. On the guitar, quartal voicings are surprisingly practical because the instrument is largely tuned in fourths, making these shapes feel natural under your fingers.

Jens Larsen approaches quartal harmony from multiple angles: as chord voicings for comping, as arpeggios for soloing, and as a way of thinking about melody that breaks free from the thirds-based patterns most guitarists default to. His method emphasizes learning quartal structures within the context of songs and chord types you already know, so you can immediately hear and use them rather than treating them as abstract theory.

The goal is not to replace traditional harmony but to add a modern color to your palette. Quartal voicings have a distinctive open, ambiguous quality -- they don't sound as clearly "major" or "minor" as tertian chords -- and this ambiguity is exactly what makes them so useful over modal vamps, minor chords, and dominant chords where you want a contemporary sound.

## Prerequisites

- Comfortable with basic shell voicings (root, 3rd, 7th) on the middle string sets
- Knowledge of major scale positions (at least two positions)
- Familiarity with diatonic seventh chord arpeggios in a key
- Understanding of basic chord types: maj7, m7, dom7, m7b5

## Key Concepts

**What is a quartal voicing?** A chord built by stacking intervals of a perfect fourth (5 half-steps) rather than the major/minor thirds used in traditional harmony. For example, starting on C: C-F-Bb is a three-note quartal voicing.

**Diatonic quartal voicings:** Just like diatonic triads come from harmonizing a scale in thirds, quartal voicings come from harmonizing a scale in fourths. In C major, the three-note quartal voicings are: C-F-B, D-G-C, E-A-D, F-B-E, G-C-F, A-D-G, B-E-A.

**Three-part vs. four-part quartal harmony:** Three-note quartal voicings are the foundation. Four-part quartal voicings (four stacked fourths) produce a bigger, more pianistic sound -- this is the McCoy Tyner approach that Jens demonstrates in his Jazz Blues lesson.

**Quartal voicings as chord substitutes:** A single quartal shape can function as many different chord types depending on the bass note. This "magic chord" quality -- one grip, many functions -- is what Jens highlights as a practical advantage for guitarists who don't want to memorize thousands of shapes.

**Quartal arpeggios for soloing:** When you arpeggiate quartal structures over chord changes, you get wide, angular intervals that sound distinctly modern compared to running up thirds-based arpeggios. This is the melodic side of quartal harmony.

## Recommended Video Sequence

Watch these Jens Larsen videos in order:

1. **Jazz Chord Essentials - 3 part Quartal Harmony** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=ITRYMElc3Js -- Foundation lesson on building three-note quartal voicings from scales and applying them to chord progressions.

2. **Jazz Chord Essentials - 4 part quartal harmony** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=aBCMAl8u1xI -- Expanding to four-note quartal voicings for a fuller, more pianistic sound on guitar.

3. **Quartal harmony in solos** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=j0JLEU4xhd8 -- How to use quartal arpeggios as melodic material in your improvisations, creating angular modern lines.

4. **m7 Chords - How to use Quartal Harmony in a Solo** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=XbqMDl8ipcY -- Specific application of quartal sounds over minor 7th chords, the most natural home for quartal harmony.

5. **Quartal Harmony on a Jazz Blues - McCoy Tyner** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=RcioU2aHha8 -- Applying quartal voicings to a jazz blues, demonstrating the McCoy Tyner approach on guitar.

6. **5 Quartal Harmony licks - Modern Jazz Sounds** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=aL8gSJxRE6E -- Five practical lick examples that show how quartal ideas become real vocabulary in your solos.

7. **Half Diminished Chords - This is how to use Quartal Arpeggios** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=0I2zEAefWg0 -- Using quartal arpeggios over m7b5 chords, a context where they sound especially effective.

8. **Altered Dominant Licks with Quartal Harmony** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=iaSvzgIhFCw -- Applying quartal structures to altered dominant chords for modern tension and resolution.

## Practice Routine

### Week 1-2: Foundation -- Three-Note Quartal Voicings

**Daily practice: 20-30 minutes**

- **Exercise 1: Diatonic quartal voicings on strings 2-3-4.** Take the C major scale and build three-note quartal voicings on every scale degree. Play them ascending and descending through the position. Learn the shapes -- some will have a perfect fourth on bottom and an augmented fourth on top (or vice versa), and one will have two perfect fourths. Get to know which is which by ear.

- **Exercise 2: Same exercise on strings 1-2-3.** The string set matters on guitar because of the tuning irregularity between the G and B strings. The shapes will be different. Practice these diatonic quartal voicings ascending and descending.

- **Exercise 3: Play a simple standard (e.g., Autumn Leaves) using only three-note quartal voicings.** For each chord in the progression, find a quartal voicing that contains at least one important chord tone (3rd or 7th). Don't worry about voice-leading perfection yet -- just get comfortable grabbing quartal shapes over real changes.

### Week 3-4: Application -- Comping and Quartal Arpeggios

**Daily practice: 20-30 minutes**

- **Exercise 4: Quartal voicing voice-leading on a II-V-I.** In C major: find a quartal voicing for Dm7, move to the nearest quartal voicing for G7, then resolve to a quartal voicing for Cmaj7. Aim for smooth voice-leading with minimal hand movement. Practice this in multiple keys.

- **Exercise 5: Quartal arpeggios through a scale.** Play the three-note quartal arpeggios as broken chords (one note at a time) ascending through the C major scale. This is your quartal arpeggio exercise for soloing. Practice with eighth notes, then try eighth-note triplets.

- **Exercise 6: Solo over a static Dm7 vamp using only quartal arpeggios.** This is where quartal sounds are most at home. Arpeggiate different quartal structures from the D dorian scale, connecting them with passing tones. Use a backing track.

### Week 5-6: Integration -- McCoy Tyner Sounds and Beyond

- **Exercise 7: Quartal voicing comping on a jazz blues.** Follow Jens's approach in the McCoy Tyner lesson -- use quartal voicings to comp through a Bb or G blues. Mix quartal voicings with your regular shell voicings for contrast.

- **Exercise 8: Four-part quartal voicings.** Extend your three-note quartal voicings by adding a fourth note (another fourth on top). These are bigger voicings that work well on the lower string sets (strings 3-4-5-6). Apply them to a modal tune like So What or Impressions.

- **Exercise 9: Combine quartal and tertian ideas in a solo.** Over Autumn Leaves or another standard you know well, alternate between your regular arpeggio-based lines and quartal arpeggio phrases. The contrast between the two approaches is what creates interest.

## Common Mistakes to Avoid

1. **Using quartal voicings everywhere.** They work beautifully over m7, sus4, and modal contexts, but can sound vague over strong dominant-to-tonic resolutions where the listener needs to hear the 3rd and 7th clearly. Mix quartal and tertian voicings.

2. **Ignoring the bass note context.** A quartal voicing in isolation is ambiguous -- it only becomes a specific chord in context. Always be aware of what the bass player is doing, because the same quartal shape sounds completely different over different roots.

3. **Only learning shapes without hearing the sound.** Quartal voicings have a distinctive "open" quality. Sing the voicings, play them slowly, and compare them to the tertian voicings you already know for the same chord. Train your ear to recognize the quartal color.

4. **Neglecting rhythm when comping with quartal voicings.** As Jens emphasizes in his comping rhythm lessons, the rhythm matters as much as the voicing. Don't let the novelty of quartal shapes distract you from solid rhythmic comping.

5. **Playing quartal arpeggios as predictable patterns.** When soloing, avoid just running up and down stacked fourths mechanically. Use quartal arpeggios as fragments within longer phrases, mix directions, and connect them with stepwise motion.

## Key Takeaways

- Quartal voicings are built from stacked fourths and have an open, modern sound that complements traditional jazz harmony
- Three-note quartal voicings are practical on guitar and one shape can function as many different chords
- Practice quartal structures diatonically through scales, just like you would with triads and seventh-chord arpeggios
- Quartal harmony is most effective over minor 7th chords, modal vamps, and as a modern color in blues comping
- The McCoy Tyner piano sound translates well to guitar using four-part quartal voicings on lower string sets
- Use quartal arpeggios in your solos for angular, wide-interval lines that break out of thirds-based patterns

## What's Next

After mastering quartal harmony, move on to:
- [17-outside-playing.md](17-outside-playing.md) -- Quartal structures are useful tools for playing outside the changes
- [18-tritone-substitution.md](18-tritone-substitution.md) -- Combine quartal voicings with tritone subs for modern reharmonization
- [15-quartal-harmony.md](15-quartal-harmony.md) pairs well with pentatonic chord concepts (see Jens's "Pentatonic Chords are Magic" lesson)

Knowledge of major scale positions (at least two positions)
Familiarity with diatonic seventh chord arpeggios in a key

title: Chromatic Enclosures & Approach Notes
overview:
Chromatic enclosures are one of the fundamental building blocks of bebop vocabulary. They are the small, dissonant melodic figures that surround a target note -- approaching it from above and below with chromatic (and sometimes diatonic) notes before resolving. This technique is what gives bebop its characteristic sense of tension and release at the note level, and it is essential for making your solos sound like jazz rather than scale exercises.

Jens Larsen treats chromatic enclosures not as isolated tricks but as part of a broader approach to melody that he learned from Barry Harris workshops. As Jens explains in his bebop exercise lessons, "a huge part of the jazz and bebop sound comes from using chromatic notes, but only if you do it the right way -- instead of just focusing on notes, you want to think in phrases." The enclosure is a phrase-level concept: it creates rhythmic energy and melodic direction by always moving toward a strong chord tone.

What makes Jens's approach distinctive is his insistence on practicing enclosures within the context of arpeggios and real chord progressions, not in isolation. He follows the Barry Harris method of attaching chromatic notes to triads and seventh-chord arpeggios so that the enclosures are always connected to the harmony. This is what separates musical chromatic playing from random chromatic noodling.
content:
# Practice Guide: Chromatic Enclosures & Approach Notes

## Overview

Chromatic enclosures are one of the fundamental building blocks of bebop vocabulary. They are the small, dissonant melodic figures that surround a target note -- approaching it from above and below with chromatic (and sometimes diatonic) notes before resolving. This technique is what gives bebop its characteristic sense of tension and release at the note level, and it is essential for making your solos sound like jazz rather than scale exercises.

Jens Larsen treats chromatic enclosures not as isolated tricks but as part of a broader approach to melody that he learned from Barry Harris workshops. As Jens explains in his bebop exercise lessons, "a huge part of the jazz and bebop sound comes from using chromatic notes, but only if you do it the right way -- instead of just focusing on notes, you want to think in phrases." The enclosure is a phrase-level concept: it creates rhythmic energy and melodic direction by always moving toward a strong chord tone.

What makes Jens's approach distinctive is his insistence on practicing enclosures within the context of arpeggios and real chord progressions, not in isolation. He follows the Barry Harris method of attaching chromatic notes to triads and seventh-chord arpeggios so that the enclosures are always connected to the harmony. This is what separates musical chromatic playing from random chromatic noodling.

## Prerequisites

- Ability to play diatonic seventh-chord arpeggios through a major scale
- Comfort with basic II-V-I progressions in at least two keys
- Familiarity with chord tones (root, 3rd, 5th, 7th) for major 7, minor 7, and dominant 7 chords
- Understanding of what "chord tones on strong beats" means in the context of bebop

## Key Concepts

**Target notes:** Before you can enclose a note, you need to know what you are aiming for. Target notes are the chord tones (especially the 3rd and 7th) that you want to land on when the chord changes. As Jens explains: "Using target notes when you're improvising is a really powerful tool because it allows you to choose in advance where you want to go with your solo. You're always moving forward and thinking ahead."

**Single chromatic approach from below:** The simplest form -- approach a chord tone from one half-step below. For example, approaching the 3rd of Cmaj7 (E) by playing Eb on the beat before.

**Single chromatic approach from above:** Approach from one half-step above. Approaching E by playing F (which is also a diatonic note in C major, so this one does double duty).

**Double chromatic enclosure:** Surround the target note from both sides -- one note above and one note below, both chromatic. For example, to enclose E: play F then Eb, landing on E. Or Eb then F, landing on E. The direction matters rhythmically.

**Diatonic + chromatic combination:** Use a diatonic scale note from above and a chromatic note from below (or vice versa). This is extremely common in bebop and creates a slightly less "crunchy" sound than a full chromatic enclosure.

**The Barry Harris enclosure exercise:** As Jens demonstrates, take a basic triad and add an enclosure around each note. The ascending version adds a scale note above and a chromatic note below each chord tone. The descending version reverses the directions. Jens calls this "incredibly simple but the concept is used everywhere in bebop -- not only on triads or arpeggios."

## Recommended Video Sequence

Watch these Jens Larsen videos in order:

1. **The Most Important Exercise In Bebop!** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=orGk08z9O30 -- The foundational enclosure exercise on triads and arpeggios, including the Barry Harris chromatic scale approach. Start here.

2. **Why Barry Harris has the Best Method for Chromatic Notes** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=6NnFgdgOnc0 -- Deep dive into Barry Harris's specific approach to chromatic notes and why Jens considers it superior to generic "bebop scale" thinking.

3. **Chromatic notes in solos part 1** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=fpxxt4ohHSA -- How chromatic notes function in actual solo lines, with analysis of how the pros use them.

4. **Chromatic approach - 5 Jazz guitar Licks with chromatic notes** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=qLUTeiADkow -- Five practical lick examples showing chromatic approach notes in real musical contexts.

5. **How to Use Chromatic Ideas in Jazz Licks The Right Way** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=FJqKznR6Y20 -- The "right way" vs. the wrong way to incorporate chromatic ideas, with emphasis on phrasing and resolution.

6. **10 Great Chromatic Ideas in Jazz Licks (Easy to Weird)** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=ARaRArDhHLQ -- A spectrum from simple to advanced chromatic techniques, showing how the concept scales up.

7. **The Real Secret About Chromatic Phrases And Great Jazz Licks** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=hzX2cgNOvxA -- What makes chromatic phrases sound musical rather than mechanical.

8. **Best approach: Target notes on a II V I** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=q6TRWFf_WQo -- How to choose target notes over a II-V-I and play toward them, the essential framework for using enclosures musically.

## Practice Routine

### Week 1-2: Foundation -- The Enclosure Exercise

**Daily practice: 20-30 minutes**

- **Exercise 1: Ascending triad enclosure.** Take a C major triad (C-E-G). For each note, play a diatonic note above, then a chromatic note below, then the chord tone. Ascending: D-B-C, F-Eb-E, A-F#-G. Practice this slowly until the pattern is automatic. Then practice through all diatonic triads in C major.

- **Exercise 2: Descending triad enclosure.** Reverse the pattern. Descending: F#-A-G, Eb-F-E, B-D-C. Again, practice through all diatonic triads.

- **Exercise 3: Expand to seventh-chord arpeggios.** Apply the same enclosure pattern to Cmaj7 (C-E-G-B), Dm7 (D-F-A-C), G7 (G-B-D-F). This is the exercise Jens demonstrates as "incredibly simple but unbelievably powerful."

- **Exercise 4: Single chromatic approach notes over a II-V-I.** Over Dm7-G7-Cmaj7, play simple lines that approach each chord's 3rd from a half-step below. Keep it slow, make sure you land the target note on a strong beat.

### Week 3-4: Application -- Enclosures in Context

**Daily practice: 20-30 minutes**

- **Exercise 5: Enclosures over Autumn Leaves.** For each chord change, pick one target note (the 3rd or 7th of the new chord) and approach it with a double chromatic enclosure. Play quarter notes at first -- the enclosure on beats 3-4, landing on beat 1 of the new chord.

- **Exercise 6: Mix enclosure types.** Over a II-V-I backing track, alternate between single approach from below, single from above, and double enclosure on each chord. Get comfortable choosing different enclosure types spontaneously.

- **Exercise 7: Eighth-note triplet enclosures.** As Jens discusses in his rhythms lesson, using an eighth-note triplet for the enclosure "adds some rhythmical tension and more energy and momentum towards that target note." Practice enclosures as triplets resolving on the downbeat.

- **Exercise 8: Connect enclosures with arpeggio fragments.** Play two beats of arpeggio, then an enclosure into the next chord tone. This is how enclosures actually appear in bebop -- not in isolation, but woven into longer phrases.

### Week 5-6: Integration -- Making Enclosures Sound Natural

- **Exercise 9: Transcribe an enclosure.** Find one phrase from a George Benson or Joe Pass solo (Jens references both frequently) that uses a clear enclosure. Learn it by ear, play it in several keys, and analyze which type of enclosure it uses.

- **Exercise 10: Improvise over a blues with enclosures as your main tool.** On a jazz blues in Bb or G, make a conscious effort to use at least one enclosure per chord change. Aim for the 3rd of each chord. Record yourself and listen back.

- **Exercise 11: Chromatic voice-leading in chords.** Apply chromatic approach principles to your comping -- approach chord voicings from a half-step above or below. This extends the enclosure concept from single-note lines to harmony.

## Common Mistakes to Avoid

1. **Resolving enclosures on weak beats.** The whole point of an enclosure is to create tension that resolves on a strong beat (1 or 3). If you land the target note on a weak beat, the enclosure loses its rhythmic purpose. As Jens emphasizes, rhythmic placement is everything.

2. **Using enclosures on every single note.** Over-enclosing makes your lines sound mechanical and predictable. In real bebop, enclosures are sprinkled in -- one or two per phrase, not on every chord tone. Contrast is what makes them effective.

3. **Practicing enclosures without knowing the target note.** If you don't know what chord tone you're aiming for, the enclosure is meaningless. Always start by identifying your target note, then add the enclosure. As Jens says about target notes: "You eliminate a solo where it sounds like you're just trying out some notes and there's no direction."

4. **Ignoring the Barry Harris approach.** Many players learn enclosures as an isolated "trick." Jens strongly advocates for the Barry Harris method of building enclosures into arpeggio exercises, so they become part of your fundamental vocabulary rather than something you bolt on.

5. **Making enclosures too fast or too flashy.** Beginners often rush through chromatic notes. Let the dissonance be heard. A well-placed enclosure at a moderate tempo sounds far more musical than a blurred chromatic run.

## Key Takeaways

- Chromatic enclosures surround a target chord tone from above and below, creating tension that resolves on a strong beat
- The Barry Harris method of adding enclosures to triad and arpeggio exercises is the most efficient way to internalize this vocabulary
- Always know your target note before adding the enclosure -- the enclosure serves the target, not the other way around
- Enclosures work best as part of longer phrases, not as isolated events -- weave them into arpeggio-based lines
- Eighth-note triplets are a particularly effective rhythm for enclosures, adding both melodic and rhythmic tension
- Chromatic approach notes are not just for single-note lines -- they can be applied to chord voicings for chromatic voice-leading

## What's Next

After mastering chromatic enclosures, move on to:
- [17-outside-playing.md](17-outside-playing.md) -- Enclosures are a "safe" form of chromaticism; outside playing takes chromatic displacement further
- [19-transcription-method.md](19-transcription-method.md) -- Transcribing solos is the best way to hear how masters use enclosures in real musical contexts
- The bebop phrasing concepts in Jens's "5 Best Bebop Shortcuts" lesson build directly on the enclosure foundation

Understanding of what "chord tones on strong beats" means in the context of bebop

overview:
Playing "outside" means deliberately using notes, scales, or harmonic structures that don't belong to the chord you're soloing over, creating a controlled sense of tension that resolves back "inside" the harmony. It is one of the most exciting and risky areas of jazz improvisation -- when done well, it adds drama and surprise to your solos; when done poorly, it just sounds like wrong notes.

Jens Larsen's approach to outside playing is characteristically practical. As he explains: "The point of using outside material is to make it sound wrong because you want the listener to feel the tension that needs to resolve, and that helps make the solo more interesting and gives it a story. Playing things that don't fit the chord is pretty easy -- but playing something that moves away and comes back so that it still sounds like music is much more difficult." The key insight is that outside playing is fundamentally about resolution, not just dissonance.

Jens covers several distinct approaches to playing outside: side-slipping (shifting your musical ideas up or down a half-step), superimposing arpeggios from unrelated keys, using symmetrical scales (whole-tone, diminished) as bridges to outside territory, and reharmonization techniques. He emphasizes that you should learn these techniques one at a time, always over a musical context like a static chord vamp or a standard you already know.
content:
# Practice Guide: Outside Playing

## Overview

Playing "outside" means deliberately using notes, scales, or harmonic structures that don't belong to the chord you're soloing over, creating a controlled sense of tension that resolves back "inside" the harmony. It is one of the most exciting and risky areas of jazz improvisation -- when done well, it adds drama and surprise to your solos; when done poorly, it just sounds like wrong notes.

Jens Larsen's approach to outside playing is characteristically practical. As he explains: "The point of using outside material is to make it sound wrong because you want the listener to feel the tension that needs to resolve, and that helps make the solo more interesting and gives it a story. Playing things that don't fit the chord is pretty easy -- but playing something that moves away and comes back so that it still sounds like music is much more difficult." The key insight is that outside playing is fundamentally about resolution, not just dissonance.

Jens covers several distinct approaches to playing outside: side-slipping (shifting your musical ideas up or down a half-step), superimposing arpeggios from unrelated keys, using symmetrical scales (whole-tone, diminished) as bridges to outside territory, and reharmonization techniques. He emphasizes that you should learn these techniques one at a time, always over a musical context like a static chord vamp or a standard you already know.

## Prerequisites

- Solid ability to solo "inside" over II-V-I progressions and jazz blues
- Comfort with at least two scale positions in every key
- Knowledge of the altered scale (melodic minor from the 7th degree)
- Understanding of tritone substitution basics
- Experience with target notes and chromatic enclosures (see guides 16 and 18)

## Key Concepts

**Side-slipping (half-step displacement):** The most common and accessible outside technique. You take a phrase or scale pattern you know and shift it up or down a half-step from where it "should" be. As Jens demonstrates, if you're soloing over G7, you can shift into Ab7 material -- "almost all the notes in that scale sound outside, and in fact the Ab7 works as a tritone substitute for the dominant of G7."

**The importance of repetition as an anchor:** Jens reveals a crucial technique for making side-slipping work: "I'm moving from the normal solo into the outside material by repeating a phrase or a part of a phrase and giving the listener something to hold on to when things start to get weird." This idea of using a repeated rhythmic or melodic motif as a bridge is essential.

**Tension and resolution with long notes:** "Notice how I'm playing long notes that really drive home the tension they create before resolving with a short phrase that moves back into the G7 chord." Outside playing is not about running fast -- it is about letting the tension be heard and then releasing it.

**Whole-tone scale for outside playing on minor chords:** Jens shows how the whole-tone scale emerges naturally from the top part of a minor-major 7 chord (the augmented triad), creating a scale that sounds distinctly outside but has a logical harmonic connection to the chord. He emphasizes that "it's more about how you resolve it in the end."

**Triads as outside structures:** Rather than thinking in scales, you can superimpose triads from distant keys over a chord. This gives you strong, clear melodic shapes that happen to be "wrong" -- and because triads are inherently musical, they sound intentional rather than random.

**Reharmonization as an outside approach:** Playing your solo as if the chords have temporarily changed. This is a more advanced technique that Jens explores in his lessons on reharmonization over Solar and other standards.

## Recommended Video Sequence

Watch these Jens Larsen videos in order:

1. **Playing Outside - Side slipping - Outside Jazz Approaches** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=6mdjJOoqFsQ -- The foundational lesson on side-slipping and half-step displacement, the most practical entry point for outside playing.

2. **How To Play Outside - A Few Great Jazz Solo Secrets** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=4DQs_yxWUnM -- Multiple approaches to outside playing with musical examples and analysis of how to make it work.

3. **How To Use Outside Playing - A Few Secrets And Some Important Advice** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=PJqxMTykdg0 -- Practical strategies including phrase repetition as an anchor, long-note tension, and resolution techniques.

4. **Whole-tone scale for outside playing** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=8Ec5WgF33Hs -- Using the whole-tone scale over minor chords, showing how it connects to melodic minor and augmented triad structures.

5. **Outside Reharmonizing - Playing Outside - Reharmonization Jazz Guitar Lesson** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=QYhfyDtShgs -- Advanced technique of reharmonizing in real-time while soloing, treating outside playing as temporary modulation.

6. **Reharmonization in a Jazz Guitar Solo - Outside ideas on Solar** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=v6lFQD1vTj4 -- Applied example of outside reharmonization techniques on a real standard.

7. **Kenny Burrell - How to Make the Blues Sound Outside** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=g9If9TAmK44 -- How a master uses outside ideas in a blues context, proving that outside playing doesn't have to be abstract or inaccessible.

8. **Diminished Scale - How To Make Beautiful Lines** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=49dhaWmBol8 -- The diminished scale as a tool for creating outside tension over dominant chords, with practical line-building approaches.

## Practice Routine

### Week 1-2: Foundation -- Side-Slipping

**Daily practice: 20-30 minutes**

- **Exercise 1: Half-step shift on a static chord.** Put on a G7 backing track or vamp. Solo comfortably in G mixolydian for 4 bars, then shift everything you play up a half-step (Ab) for 2 bars, then resolve back to G for 2 bars. Focus on making the shift deliberate and the resolution clear.

- **Exercise 2: Phrase repetition as a bridge.** Over the same G7 vamp, play a short 2-beat phrase inside, then repeat the same rhythmic phrase shifted up a half-step, then play it one more time back inside. This is the "giving the listener something to hold on to" technique Jens describes.

- **Exercise 3: Long notes for tension.** When you're in the "outside" zone (shifted up or down a half-step), hold one or two notes for a full beat or longer. Let the dissonance sit. Then resolve with a quick inside phrase. Practice resisting the urge to rush through the outside notes.

### Week 3-4: Application -- Multiple Outside Techniques

**Daily practice: 20-30 minutes**

- **Exercise 4: Whole-tone scale over a minor chord.** Over a Gm7 or Gm(maj7) vamp, practice the Bb whole-tone scale as Jens describes it. Play short fragments -- 4-5 notes -- then resolve back to G dorian or G melodic minor. The resolution is more important than the outside playing itself.

- **Exercise 5: Superimposed triads.** Over a Dm7 chord, play major triads from distant keys: Eb major, Ab major, B major. Each one sounds distinctly "outside." Practice connecting each foreign triad to a resolution -- an inside arpeggio or chord tone on Dm7.

- **Exercise 6: Outside playing on a II-V-I.** The V chord (G7) is the most natural place for outside playing because it already wants to create tension. Side-slip on the G7 for one bar, then resolve clearly to Cmaj7. Practice this over the full II-V-I so the outside moment has context.

### Week 5-6: Integration -- Outside Playing in Real Music

- **Exercise 7: Outside playing over a jazz blues.** Pick one chorus to play "safe" and one chorus to include outside moments. Aim for 2-3 outside phrases per chorus, each with a clear resolution. The dominant chords in bars 4, 8, 9, and 10 are natural spots for tension.

- **Exercise 8: Combine techniques.** In a single solo over Autumn Leaves or another standard, use side-slipping on one chord, a whole-tone fragment on another, and a superimposed triad on a third. The goal is to have multiple tools available, not just one approach.

- **Exercise 9: Reharmonization solo.** Over a standard you know well, play your solo as if one or two chords per 8-bar section have been replaced by unexpected substitutions. This is the most advanced form of outside playing -- you're creating your own harmonic reality in the moment.

## Common Mistakes to Avoid

1. **Not resolving.** This is the biggest mistake. Outside playing without resolution is just wrong notes. Every outside phrase must come back inside. As Jens emphasizes, the outside material needs to "move away and come back so that it still sounds like music."

2. **Playing outside before you can play inside.** If your inside playing is weak, outside playing will just sound like you don't know the changes. Master the harmony first. Jens's outside playing lessons assume strong inside knowledge.

3. **Rushing through the outside notes.** Let the tension breathe. Jens specifically notes the power of "playing long notes that really drive home the tension." If you rush, the listener never registers the outside sound, and the resolution has no impact.

4. **No rhythmic or melodic connection between inside and outside.** The transition into outside territory should have a musical logic -- a repeated phrase, a sequence, a rhythmic pattern. Don't just randomly jump to a distant key. Use Jens's technique of repeating a phrase and shifting it.

5. **Using outside playing as a crutch.** Outside playing is a spice, not the main course. In a 3-chorus solo, you might use 4-6 outside moments total. If everything is outside, nothing sounds outside -- you've just lost the changes.

## Key Takeaways

- Outside playing is about controlled tension and resolution -- the resolution matters more than the tension
- Side-slipping (half-step displacement) is the most practical starting point and works on almost any chord type
- Repeat a phrase or rhythmic motif when transitioning to outside material so the listener has an anchor
- Let outside notes ring -- long notes create more tension than fast chromatic runs
- The whole-tone scale is especially effective for outside playing over minor chords
- Superimposed triads give you strong melodic shapes that sound intentionally outside
- Always master playing inside a harmony before attempting to play outside of it

## What's Next

After developing outside playing skills, move on to:
- [18-tritone-substitution.md](18-tritone-substitution.md) -- Tritone substitution provides a harmonic framework for some outside sounds
- [15-quartal-harmony.md](15-quartal-harmony.md) -- Quartal arpeggios from distant keys make excellent outside structures
- [20-practice-methodology.md](20-practice-methodology.md) -- How to balance outside playing practice with the rest of your routine

title: Tritone Substitution
overview:
Tritone substitution is one of the most powerful harmonic devices in jazz -- a way to replace a dominant chord with another dominant chord a tritone (six half-steps) away. It works because the two chords share the same 3rd and 7th (just swapped), creating a smooth voice-leading alternative that adds beautiful chromatic motion to chord progressions. As Jens Larsen puts it: "Tritone substitution is a great way to create some surprising sounds in your solos or in your melody arrangements -- definitely something you want to have in your toolbox."

Jens approaches tritone substitution from both a theoretical and a visual/practical angle, which he notes "works really well for this topic and it also works really well on the guitar." His key insight is that on guitar, shell voicings for a dominant chord and its tritone substitute are nearly identical shapes -- "D flat 7 is actually pretty much the same chord except for the root, because the 3rd and 7th are shared, they just turn around." This visual/physical connection makes tritone subs more intuitive on guitar than on many other instruments.

Beyond comping, Jens emphasizes that tritone substitution is "a great way to open up another difficult topic, namely the altered dominant, and especially when it comes to soloing over altered dominance -- if you use tritone substitution as a way of getting into that, then you get a lot of options and it makes a lot more sense." This connection between tritone subs and the altered scale is one of the most important theoretical links in jazz.
content:
# Practice Guide: Tritone Substitution

## Overview

Tritone substitution is one of the most powerful harmonic devices in jazz -- a way to replace a dominant chord with another dominant chord a tritone (six half-steps) away. It works because the two chords share the same 3rd and 7th (just swapped), creating a smooth voice-leading alternative that adds beautiful chromatic motion to chord progressions. As Jens Larsen puts it: "Tritone substitution is a great way to create some surprising sounds in your solos or in your melody arrangements -- definitely something you want to have in your toolbox."

Jens approaches tritone substitution from both a theoretical and a visual/practical angle, which he notes "works really well for this topic and it also works really well on the guitar." His key insight is that on guitar, shell voicings for a dominant chord and its tritone substitute are nearly identical shapes -- "D flat 7 is actually pretty much the same chord except for the root, because the 3rd and 7th are shared, they just turn around." This visual/physical connection makes tritone subs more intuitive on guitar than on many other instruments.

Beyond comping, Jens emphasizes that tritone substitution is "a great way to open up another difficult topic, namely the altered dominant, and especially when it comes to soloing over altered dominance -- if you use tritone substitution as a way of getting into that, then you get a lot of options and it makes a lot more sense." This connection between tritone subs and the altered scale is one of the most important theoretical links in jazz.

## Prerequisites

- Solid understanding of II-V-I progressions in major and minor keys
- Ability to play shell voicings (root, 3rd, 7th) for dominant chords
- Knowledge of the major scale and mixolydian mode
- Familiarity with chord function (what a dominant chord "wants" to resolve to)
- Basic understanding of voice-leading between chords

## Key Concepts

**Why it works -- shared guide tones:** The 3rd and 7th of G7 are B and F. The 3rd and 7th of Db7 are F and Cb (enharmonically B). They share the same tritone interval -- just inverted. As Jens explains: "G7 and Db7 actually share 3rd and 7th, it turns around -- for G7 the F is the 7th, for Db7 that's the 3rd, but the notes are the same."

**Tritone sub in a II-V-I:** In a standard II-V-I in C major (Dm7 - G7 - Cmaj7), the tritone sub replaces G7 with Db7, giving you Dm7 - Db7 - Cmaj7. This creates a beautiful chromatic bass line: D - Db - C. Jens demonstrates: "When I do this with shell voicings, the G7 and Db7 actually sound really similar, but if I start adding some more extensions to the chords, then you can really hear the difference and all the extra sounds that we get."

**Extensions on tritone subs:** When you play Db7 instead of G7, the extensions of Db7 become altered extensions of G7. The Db7's natural 9th (Eb) is G7's #5/b13. The Db7's natural 13th (Bb) is G7's #9. This is why tritone substitution naturally leads to altered dominant sounds.

**Connection to the altered scale:** Playing Db mixolydian over a G7 chord gives you all the altered extensions of G7. This is one of the easiest entry points into the altered scale -- rather than thinking "G altered = Ab melodic minor from G to G," you can think "just play Db7 material." As Jens puts it in his altered scale lessons, this approach "makes a lot more sense" for many players.

**The tritone scale:** A more advanced concept Jens explores -- a synthetic scale that combines elements of both the original dominant and its tritone substitute, giving you vocabulary that works from either perspective.

**Extending tritone subs to the entire II-V:** You can also substitute the II chord along with the V, replacing Dm7-G7 with Abm7-Db7 resolving to Cmaj7. This is the "backdoor" approach that creates even more chromatic motion.

## Recommended Video Sequence

Watch these Jens Larsen videos in order:

1. **Tritone substitution** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=YjZAN0NnIj0 -- The foundational explanation: what tritone subs are, why they work, and basic applications in progressions and voicings.

2. **Tritone Substitution Is a Really Great Hack** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=NlqvZ0Q_Wpk -- Jens's comprehensive lesson covering theory, visual approach on guitar, connection to altered dominants, and practical comping/soloing applications.

3. **Altered Scale - Make Sure You Learn This First!** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=EfV5QZD5SzA -- The altered scale explained as melodic minor from the 7th degree, with the crucial connection to tritone substitution for soloing.

4. **It Is The Sound That Is Important! - Altered Scale** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=QEF4R2mTqNU -- Learning the altered sound by ear first using examples from Wes Montgomery and Joe Pass, with tritone sub context.

5. **Minor II V I options - Melodic Minor, Phrygian Chords and Tritone Substitutions** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=UAD467eo_bc -- Applying tritone subs in minor key II-V-I progressions, with Phrygian chord alternatives.

6. **Solar - Jazz Guitar Lesson - Melodic Minor, Altered Scale and Tritone subs** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=fGu0ebNNEHQ -- Applied lesson showing tritone subs and altered scale in the context of a real standard.

7. **Modern Jazz Reharmonization Techniques: Tritone sub, Coltrane Changes and Modal** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=oBWjUvEGohw -- Advanced reharmonization using tritone subs as one of several tools, applied to All The Things You Are.

8. **Tritone Scale** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=_Pj0CertIeM -- The synthetic tritone scale that combines both sides of the tritone substitution for unique melodic possibilities.

## Practice Routine

### Week 1-2: Foundation -- Understanding and Hearing Tritone Subs

**Daily practice: 20-30 minutes**

- **Exercise 1: Shell voicing comparison.** Play G7 as a shell voicing (root on 6th string). Now play Db7 as a shell voicing. Notice how the 3rd and 7th are the same notes, just swapped. Do this for all 12 dominant chords and their tritone substitutes. Hear the similarity.

- **Exercise 2: II-V-I with and without tritone sub.** In C major, play Dm7 - G7 - Cmaj7 using shell voicings. Then play Dm7 - Db7 - Cmaj7. Listen to the chromatic bass motion (D - Db - C). Practice this in all 12 keys, but prioritize the keys of standards you play (C, F, Bb, Eb, G).

- **Exercise 3: Tritone sub with extensions.** Once the shell voicings are comfortable, add extensions. Play Db9, Db13, etc. over where G7 would go. Notice how these "normal" extensions of Db7 create the exotic altered sounds over G7 (b9, #9, b13).

- **Exercise 4: Hear it in context.** Put on a II-V-I backing track and comp the progression using the tritone sub every other time through. Alternate: normal G7, then Db7, then normal, then Db7. Train your ear to hear both as valid.

### Week 3-4: Application -- Soloing Over Tritone Subs

**Daily practice: 20-30 minutes**

- **Exercise 5: Db mixolydian over G7.** Over a G7 vamp, solo using the Db mixolydian scale (Db Eb F Gb Ab Bb Cb). This is the "tritone sub soloing" approach -- playing the scale of the substitute chord. Notice how it gives you all the altered tensions of G7 without thinking about the altered scale formula.

- **Exercise 6: Tritone sub arpeggios in a II-V-I.** Over Dm7-G7-Cmaj7, play Dm7 arpeggio - Db7 arpeggio - Cmaj7 arpeggio. The Db7 arpeggio (Db F Ab Cb) contains the b5, b7, b9, and 3rd of G7 -- pure altered sound with a simple arpeggio shape.

- **Exercise 7: Chromatic line approach.** Over the II-V-I, create lines that use the chromatic bass descent (D-Db-C) as a melodic thread. Start each phrase on the root of the current chord, moving chromatically into the next chord. This makes the tritone sub audible in your single-note lines.

- **Exercise 8: Apply to a standard.** Take Autumn Leaves and identify every V7 chord. Practice soloing over the tune, using tritone sub vocabulary on each V7. Don't change the comping -- just think Db7 where G7 is written, Eb7 where A7 is written, etc.

### Week 5-6: Integration -- Advanced Applications

- **Exercise 9: Tritone sub comping on a jazz blues.** A Bb blues has several dominant chords (Bb7, Eb7, F7). Practice comping the blues and substituting tritone subs on some of the dominant chords. Not every V chord needs a tritone sub -- choose strategically for the best voice-leading.

- **Exercise 10: Substitute the entire II-V.** Replace Dm7-G7 with Abm7-Db7 resolving to Cmaj7. This creates a completely chromatic approach from a half-step above. Practice both comping and soloing with this extended substitution.

- **Exercise 11: Combine tritone subs with other reharmonization techniques.** Following Jens's approach in his All The Things You Are reharmonization lesson, apply tritone subs alongside other techniques (modal interchange, Coltrane changes) to create your own reharmonization of a standard.

## Common Mistakes to Avoid

1. **Using tritone subs on non-resolving dominant chords.** Jens is clear that "this is something that only really works for dominant chords that resolve." A static dominant vamp (like a blues I chord) doesn't benefit from tritone substitution in the same way. The magic is in the resolution.

2. **Overusing tritone subs.** If every V chord gets a tritone sub, the effect becomes predictable and loses its surprise. Use them selectively -- one or two per tune is often enough to add color.

3. **Neglecting voice-leading.** The beauty of tritone subs is in the chromatic voice-leading. If your voicings don't take advantage of this (e.g., big jumps between chords), you lose the smooth motion that makes tritone subs sound sophisticated.

4. **Not connecting tritone subs to the altered scale.** Understanding that Db7 over G7 gives you the altered sound is one of the most powerful theoretical insights in jazz. If you learn tritone subs without making this connection, you're missing half the value.

5. **Forgetting the bass player.** In a group setting, if the bass player is playing G and you comp Db7, communication is key. Either cue the bass player, or use tritone sub voicings without the root (which is the most common real-world approach anyway).

## Key Takeaways

- Tritone substitution replaces a dominant chord with the dominant a tritone away, working because they share the same 3rd and 7th
- On guitar, shell voicings for a chord and its tritone sub are nearly identical shapes -- just the root changes
- The chromatic bass motion (D - Db - C in a II-V-I) is the most audible and beautiful effect of tritone substitution
- Soloing over a tritone sub is one of the easiest ways to access altered dominant sounds -- just play the mixolydian scale of the substitute chord
- Tritone subs work best on resolving dominant chords and should be used selectively for maximum effect
- This concept connects directly to the altered scale, reharmonization, and modern jazz harmonic thinking

## What's Next

After mastering tritone substitution, move on to:
- [17-outside-playing.md](17-outside-playing.md) -- Tritone subs provide a harmonic justification for many outside sounds
- [15-quartal-harmony.md](15-quartal-harmony.md) -- Quartal voicings over tritone subs create modern reharmonizations
- Explore Jens's lessons on Coltrane Changes and advanced reharmonization for the next level of harmonic substitution
prerequisites: Solid understanding of II-V-I progressions in major and minor keys
Ability to play shell voicings (root, 3rd, 7th) for dominant chords
Knowledge of the major scale and mixolydian mode
Familiarity with chord function (what a dominant chord "wants" to resolve to)
Basic understanding of voice-leading between chords

content:
# Practice Guide: Transcription Method

## Overview

Transcription -- learning solos by ear and internalizing them -- is one of the most powerful tools for developing jazz vocabulary, phrasing, and musicality. But as Jens Larsen points out, the common advice to "just transcribe some solos" is often given "without any context," making it "not the best advice." The value of transcription depends entirely on how you do it, what you choose to transcribe, and what you do with the material afterward.

Jens's approach to transcription emphasizes learning by ear over writing things down. As he explains: "The solos that I actually learned the most from were not solos that I've written out. I just spent some time learning how to play them. Some of the main benefits from learning a solo by ear are really better if you don't try to write it down -- if you really just stick with learning it by ear and keeping it in your memory, that's the way that you can really learn the most." This is not about notation or analysis software; it is about deeply absorbing the musical content.

The goal of transcription in Jens's method is not to accumulate a library of memorized solos but to extract flexible vocabulary -- phrases, rhythmic ideas, and approaches to harmony -- that you can adapt and use in your own playing. As he demonstrates in his "5 Jazz Blues Licks" lesson, transcribed material becomes the raw material for your own musical language: "In these examples you're going to find small phrases and bits and pieces of things that I've actually checked out from solos and transcriptions -- phrases from George Benson, Wes Montgomery, John Scofield, Kenny Burrell, and Grant Green."

## Prerequisites

- Ability to play through at least a few jazz standards with basic chord knowledge
- Familiarity with basic music theory (keys, intervals, chord types)
- Access to a music player that can slow down audio without changing pitch
- Patience and willingness to spend time with your ear before reaching for your instrument

## Key Concepts

**Learning by ear vs. writing it down:** Jens is clear on this distinction. Writing out a solo can be useful for analysis, but the deepest learning comes from internalizing the music aurally and physically (on your instrument) without paper as a crutch. When you learn by ear, the phrasing, dynamics, and feel become part of your muscle memory in a way that reading notation never achieves.

**Choosing the right solo to transcribe:** Not every solo is equally useful. The best solos to start with are those that match your current level and teach specific skills. As Jens and other teachers recommend in his collaborative video: start with Django Reinhardt's "Minor Swing" (1937 recording) for learning chord tones and swing feel; Miles Davis's solo on "So What" for modal playing and phrasing with space; then progress to more complex bop solos.

**Starting small -- phrases, not full solos:** You don't have to transcribe an entire solo to benefit. Often, a 4-bar phrase or even a 2-bar lick will teach you a concept that you can apply across many contexts. Jens regularly demonstrates how a single transcribed phrase from Grant Green or George Benson becomes the seed for multiple variations.

**Analyzing what you've learned:** After you can play the transcribed material, step back and ask: What arpeggios is the player using? Where are the chord tones landing (on which beats)? What chromatic notes are being used and how? How does the rhythm create interest? This analysis turns memorized notes into understood concepts.

**Applying transcribed vocabulary to other contexts:** The real test is whether you can use the transcribed idea in a different key, over a different chord, or in a different song. Jens emphasizes moving transcribed phrases through all 12 keys and applying them to standards you are already playing.

**The difference between copying and absorbing:** As Jens describes his own process, transcribed ideas become "bits and pieces" woven into original musical thoughts -- not direct quotes but absorbed vocabulary that informs your personal style.

## Recommended Video Sequence

Watch these Jens Larsen videos in order:

1. **How To Get The Most Out Of Transcribing** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=lprLYFqmOVM -- Jens's core lesson on his transcription philosophy: why learning by ear is more valuable than writing things down, and how to extract vocabulary from solos.

2. **This Way of Learning Solos Teaches You What Jazz Guitar Is Really About** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=-i8nncUKb-U -- How the process of learning solos teaches you about jazz itself -- the phrasing, the rhythm, the harmonic thinking.

3. **The Solos You Want To Learn By Ear To Play better Jazz Guitar** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=8wy6CYrw7Vc -- Specific solo recommendations from Jens and other teachers, organized by level and what each solo teaches you.

4. **5 Jazz Blues Licks - How To Use Transcribed Ideas** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=6oXkMN_zP9Y -- Practical demonstration of how transcribed phrases from George Benson, Wes Montgomery, Scofield, Kenny Burrell, and Grant Green get woven into original blues lines.

5. **I Wish I Learned Jazz Guitar Like This (Soloing Guide)** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=YP-M-1xcYNk -- Broader soloing approach that shows how transcription fits into the overall process of learning to improvise.

6. **The Phrasing Skills All The Pros Use But Nobody Talks About** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=f8eH8PWe1mk -- Three phrasing techniques you can learn from transcription: melodic direction, rhythmic variety, and phrase connection. Analyzes Joe Pass and George Benson.

7. **Why Patterns Are Always Better Than Licks - Parker's Strategy** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=wP-wpC0axP0 -- How to extract flexible patterns (not just fixed licks) from transcriptions, using Charlie Parker's approach as a model.

## Practice Routine

### Week 1-2: Foundation -- Your First Transcription

**Daily practice: 30-40 minutes**

- **Exercise 1: Choose your first solo.** If you haven't transcribed before, start with Django Reinhardt's solo on "Minor Swing" (1937 recording) or Miles Davis's solo on "So What." Both are relatively slow, use clear note choices, and teach fundamental jazz concepts. Listen to the solo 5 times without your guitar before you begin.

- **Exercise 2: Learn 4 bars by ear.** Start with the first 4 bars. Listen to 2-3 notes at a time. Slow the recording down to 50-70% speed if needed. Find the notes on your guitar by matching pitch. Do not look up a transcription online -- the ear training is the exercise. Spend 15-20 minutes per session.

- **Exercise 3: Play along with the recording.** Once you can play the 4 bars, play along with the original recording at full speed. Match the timing, dynamics, and phrasing as closely as possible. This is where you absorb the feel, not just the notes.

- **Exercise 4: Add 4 more bars each session.** Gradually expand your transcription. Aim to have a full chorus (or at least 16 bars) learned by the end of week 2. Don't rush -- accuracy of phrasing matters more than quantity of notes.

### Week 3-4: Application -- Analyzing and Extracting Vocabulary

**Daily practice: 20-30 minutes**

- **Exercise 5: Analyze the transcribed solo.** For each phrase, identify: What chord is being played over? Which notes are chord tones and which are passing/chromatic notes? Where do the phrases start and end relative to the bar line? What rhythmic patterns recur?

- **Exercise 6: Extract 3 useful phrases.** Pick the 3 phrases that sound best to your ears and that you could imagine using in your own solos. These are your "vocabulary words." Isolate each one and practice it until you can play it from memory without the recording.

- **Exercise 7: Transpose each phrase to 3 other keys.** Take each of your 3 extracted phrases and learn them in at least 3 additional keys. This breaks the phrases free from the specific fretboard position and makes them available anywhere.

- **Exercise 8: Apply phrases to a different song.** Take one of your extracted phrases and try to use it over a II-V-I in a different standard (e.g., if you transcribed from a blues, try the phrase over Autumn Leaves). Modify it as needed to fit the new harmonic context.

### Week 5-6: Integration -- Building Your Personal Vocabulary

- **Exercise 9: Improvise using transcribed material.** Solo over a backing track for the same tune you transcribed, consciously quoting and varying the phrases you learned. The goal is not to reproduce the solo but to weave fragments of it into your own ideas.

- **Exercise 10: Combine transcribed vocabulary with your existing vocabulary.** Record yourself soloing over a familiar standard. Listen back and notice where the transcribed ideas show up naturally. This integration is the sign that the vocabulary has been internalized.

- **Exercise 11: Start your next transcription.** Choose a slightly more challenging solo. If your first was Django or Miles, try a Grant Green or Kenny Burrell blues solo. Apply the same process: ear first, analyze second, extract and apply third.

## Common Mistakes to Avoid

1. **Writing it down before learning it by ear.** As Jens stresses, the solos he learned the most from were the ones he did not write out. The ear-learning process forces deeper internalization. Write it down later if you want to analyze, but learn by ear first.

2. **Choosing solos that are too advanced.** A Charlie Parker solo at 300 BPM is not helpful if you can't hear the individual notes. Start with solos at a moderate tempo with clear note choices. You can always progress to more complex material later.

3. **Learning the notes but ignoring the phrasing.** The notes are only half the story. The rhythm, dynamics, articulation, and feel of a phrase are what make it sound like jazz. As Jens demonstrates, George Benson's phrasing is like a "roller coaster -- exciting and full of movement, always heading towards strong chord tones."

4. **Transcribing without analyzing.** If you learn a solo by ear but never figure out why it works, you've memorized a party trick. The analysis phase -- identifying chord tones, approach notes, rhythmic patterns -- is what turns a transcription into transferable knowledge.

5. **Never applying transcribed ideas to other contexts.** A phrase that lives only in its original key and song hasn't been truly learned. Transpose it, recontextualize it, vary it. As Jens demonstrates with his blues lick examples, transcribed material should become "bits and pieces" that inform your own original ideas.

6. **Trying to transcribe too much too fast.** Four bars of deeply internalized music is worth more than two choruses of vaguely memorized notes. Quality over quantity.

## Key Takeaways

- Learning solos by ear (without writing them down first) is the most effective form of transcription for absorbing phrasing, rhythm, and feel
- Start with solos that match your current level -- Django's "Minor Swing" and Miles's "So What" are excellent starting points
- Extract flexible phrases from transcriptions and transpose them to multiple keys so they become usable vocabulary
- Always analyze transcribed solos: identify chord tones, chromatic approaches, rhythmic patterns, and phrase structure
- The goal is not to memorize complete solos but to absorb musical ideas that become part of your personal language
- The best sign that transcription is working is when transcribed ideas show up naturally in your improvisation without conscious effort

## What's Next

After establishing a transcription practice, move on to:
- [20-practice-methodology.md](20-practice-methodology.md) -- How transcription fits into a balanced overall practice routine
- [16-chromatic-enclosures.md](16-chromatic-enclosures.md) -- Transcribing bebop solos is one of the best ways to hear enclosures in context
- Continue transcribing progressively more complex solos: Grant Green for blues vocabulary, Wes Montgomery for octaves and phrasing, Joe Pass for chord-melody and voice-leading
