content:
# Practice Guide: Practice Methodology

## Overview

How you practice matters at least as much as what you practice. Jens Larsen has spent decades refining his teaching approach around one core principle, which he attributes to Wes Montgomery: "I don't practice anything during the day that I don't use at night on the gig." This philosophy -- that every exercise should connect directly to real music -- runs through all of Jens's practice advice and is echoed by John Scofield's blunt suggestion: "Just practice tunes. Learn the tune. Really learn the tune."

Jens is direct about the biggest practice mistake he sees: "Most jazz beginners spend hours on scale practice that doesn't help them play better solos." His solution, heavily inspired by Barry Harris's teaching workshops, is to anchor everything -- scales, arpeggios, chords, rhythm -- to actual songs. As he explains: "Practicing scales shouldn't be just dry and technical and more than anything else about moving your fingers on the instrument. The goal is to make it easier for you to play the things that you want to play in your solos. It is really that simple."

This guide distills Jens's practice philosophy into an actionable daily framework. The goal is not to tell you exactly what to play every minute, but to give you a structure for organizing your practice time so that every exercise has a musical purpose, every technical drill connects to a song, and your practice sessions consistently translate into better playing on real music.

## Prerequisites

- At least one jazz standard memorized (chords and melody)
- Basic knowledge of shell voicings and diatonic arpeggios
- A metronome and access to backing tracks
- Willingness to honestly evaluate what is and isn't working in your practice

## Key Concepts

**Practice through songs, not in isolation.** This is the single most important principle in Jens's methodology. Scales should be practiced in the context of a song's key centers. Arpeggios should follow the chord progression of a tune you are learning. Even rhythm exercises should use real comping patterns over real changes. As Jens puts it: "There's no point in practicing exercises in a vacuum."

**You play like you practice.** Jens is blunt: "If you only run scales, your solos will sound exactly like that. Predictable and mechanical scale runs." This means that if you spend 80% of your practice time on technique and 20% on making music, your playing will reflect that imbalance. The solution is to make every technical exercise a musical one.

**Barry Harris's approach to scale practice.** Jens credits Barry Harris as a primary influence on his practice philosophy. The key idea: practice scales by attaching them directly to songs, playing the scale through the chord changes so that you see the arpeggios within the scale and hear the chord progression in the exercise. "This way of attaching scales directly to songs is classic Barry."

**Diatonic arpeggios as the bridge between scales and solos.** Jens teaches that "the most efficient way to practice arpeggios is in the scale, not all over the neck." Practice diatonic arpeggios (one octave, from each scale degree) within a single position, then use those same arpeggios to solo over the song's chord progression. The arpeggio exercise and the solo become the same activity.

**Master one position before expanding.** Jens warns against the temptation of learning every scale position: "If you don't have any real vocabulary anywhere, then you're just learning to sound boring all over the neck. It is actually a lot faster to master one position and then expand outwards, making sure that you can actually solo everywhere that you practice."

**Rhythm is fundamental, not optional.** Jazz is about rhythm. As Jens emphasizes, you need to practice comping rhythms and solo rhythms as deliberately as you practice notes and harmony. A solo with great rhythm and simple notes will always sound better than a solo with complex notes and flat rhythm.

**Composition as a practice tool.** Writing your own lines, etudes, and chord-melody arrangements over standards is an underrated form of practice. It forces you to think deeply about the harmony and make specific musical choices, rather than relying on muscle memory.

## Recommended Video Sequence

Watch these Jens Larsen videos in order:

1. **Make Sure You Practice Like This Every Day! - Jazz Guitar** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=60ZngB534AQ -- Jens's daily practice routine framework, built around the Wes Montgomery and Scofield principle of always connecting exercises to real songs.

2. **Jazz Guitar Is Hard Until You Practice Like THIS!** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=PtY6YHsdO5E -- Why standard scale practice fails, the Barry Harris approach to scale exercises, and how to turn exercises into vocabulary.

3. **7 Things I Wish Every Jazz Beginner Would Practice** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=478HBkYTI3Y -- The essential areas every beginner should cover: arpeggios in the scale, soloing over changes, and building vocabulary from day one.

4. **Jazz Practice Routine How To Find The Perfect Balance** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=N6ggZQhPn_k -- How to balance technique, repertoire, and improvisation in your practice time without neglecting any area.

5. **How to Practice Jazz Guitar Smarter (and Save Time) in 2025** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=pWJ31n-o7Ag -- Efficiency-focused practice advice: what to cut, what to prioritize, and how to get more out of less time.

6. **5 Jazz Guitar Exercises That Would Have Saved Me Years** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=RaG55-QblDU -- The specific exercises Jens wishes he had started with earlier, each connected to real musical applications.

7. **This is a good 10 minute practice routine** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=6xg4miye7Eo -- Proof that even a short practice session can be effective if structured correctly.

8. **The Perfect Guitar Practice Routine - 5 tips for Jazz Guitar Practice** - https://youtube.com/watch?v=6SgAInof_Bw -- Five actionable tips for organizing and optimizing your practice sessions.

## Practice Routine

### The Daily Framework

Jens's approach can be organized into a flexible daily structure. The key insight is that every block connects to a song.

**Choose a "song of the week."** All your exercises for the week revolve around one standard. This ensures that scales, arpeggios, chords, and improvisation practice are all connected to the same harmonic context.

### Block 1: Technique Through Harmony (10-15 minutes)

**Scales connected to the song:**
- Play the relevant major/minor scale(s) through the chord progression of your song of the week. Don't just run the scale up and down -- play it while being aware of which chord you are on and which notes are chord tones.
- Practice diatonic arpeggios (one octave, from each degree) within the scale position you are using for the song. As Jens teaches via Barry Harris: these arpeggios should be "right there when you need them for solos."

**Specific exercises:**
- Scale in thirds through the changes
- Diatonic seventh-chord arpeggios ascending through the scale
- Arpeggio of each chord in the song, played in time with the changes (even at a slow tempo)

### Block 2: Chords and Comping (10-15 minutes)

**Voicings connected to the song:**
- Play through the song using shell voicings, focusing on smooth voice-leading between chords
- Add comping rhythms: practice 2-3 of the rhythmic patterns Jens teaches (Charleston, syncopated anticipation, etc.) over the same changes
- Experiment with extensions and substitutions on one or two chords per chorus

**The goal:** Comping should feel as natural as melody. Don't just place chords -- groove with them.

### Block 3: Improvisation (10-15 minutes)

**Solo over the song:**
- Start simple: solo using only arpeggios over the chord changes. No passing tones, no chromatic notes. Just chord tones. This is harder than it sounds and incredibly valuable.
- Add scale tones and passing tones gradually
- Focus on one specific concept per session: today might be "enclosures on the 3rd of each chord," tomorrow might be "rhythmic variation with triplets"

**Record yourself** at least once per week and listen back critically. This is the single most effective feedback mechanism available to a self-taught player.

### Block 4: Repertoire and Ear Training (5-10 minutes)

**Learn new material:**
- Work on memorizing a new section of a standard (melody + chords)
- Transcription practice: learn 4 bars of a solo by ear (see guide 19)
- Sight-read a new chord melody or etude

### Metronome and Backing Track Guidance

Jens has nuanced views on metronome use:
- **Use the metronome on beats 2 and 4** to develop swing feel, not on every beat. This is a fundamental jazz practice technique (see his metronome lesson at https://youtube.com/watch?v=7cEjm9Sj9AU).
- **Backing tracks are useful but have limits.** Jens acknowledges their value for simulating a band context, but warns against becoming dependent on them. Make sure you can also play without a backing track, just with a metronome or even solo.
- **Vary the tempo.** Don't always practice at a comfortable medium tempo. Include slow practice (where you really hear every note) and uptempo practice (where you have to simplify and rely on what you truly know).

### Tracking Progress

- **Weekly recording:** Record yourself playing through your song of the week once per week. Keep these recordings. Listen back after a month and you will hear clear improvement.
- **Rotate your song of the week.** Each week, pick a new standard (or revisit an older one at a higher level). Over a year, this gives you deep experience with 50+ songs.
- **Maintain a practice journal.** A simple log: what song, what concepts you worked on, what felt hard, what clicked. This helps you identify patterns in what works and what doesn't.

## Common Mistakes to Avoid

1. **Practicing exercises that never connect to music.** This is Jens's number one concern. If you can't identify which song or chord progression an exercise relates to, it probably isn't helping you play better. Always ask: "When would I use this in a real musical situation?"

2. **Spending too much time on technique, not enough on playing.** A common trap: spending 45 minutes on scale exercises and 5 minutes on actually playing a tune. Flip the ratio. The playing is the practice.

3. **Trying to master every scale position before learning to solo.** As Jens warns, this leads to "sounding boring all over the neck." Master one position deeply -- scales, arpeggios, chords, and improvisation -- before expanding.

4. **Ignoring rhythm in your practice.** You can have the most sophisticated harmonic vocabulary in the world, but without rhythmic awareness, your solos will be flat. Dedicate time to rhythmic exercises, comping practice, and playing with a metronome on 2 and 4.

5. **Never recording yourself.** Without recordings, you have no objective feedback. Your perception of your own playing while you're doing it is unreliable. Record, listen back, identify one thing to improve, and work on that.

6. **Trying to practice everything every day.** You don't need to cover every topic in every session. It's better to spend 30 focused minutes on 2-3 areas than to rush through 10 topics superficially. Rotate your focus areas across the week.

7. **Skipping the "boring" fundamentals.** Shell voicings, diatonic arpeggios, and simple melodies are not glamorous. But as Jens repeatedly demonstrates, these are the foundation that everything else is built on. "Getting comfortable with soloing over chord changes is an essential skill. Let's start with the basics."

## Key Takeaways

- Anchor every exercise to a real song -- scales, arpeggios, and chords should always be practiced through actual chord progressions
- "You play like you practice" -- if your exercises are musical, your playing will be musical
- Master one fretboard position deeply before expanding outwards
- The Barry Harris approach of diatonic arpeggios within a scale, connected to songs, is the most efficient way to build usable vocabulary
- Balance your practice across technique, chords/comping, improvisation, and repertoire
- Record yourself weekly and track progress -- this honest feedback is invaluable
- Even 10 focused minutes of structured practice is better than an hour of noodling without direction

## What's Next

This guide is the foundation for all other practice guides in this series. Apply this methodology to:
